problems.title: 🐛 [Bug]: Unix Domain Socket isn't trusted as Localhost
problems.issue_body:
### Bug Description

### TLDR: Fiber should trust "Localhost" if nginx or other proxy connects via unix socket..


I have a nginx/1.29.4 serving my fiber backend via unix socket (same device hosting nginx as well as fiber)
### NGINX block (minimal):
```
location ^~ /project/api {
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
        ....
        proxy_pass http://unix:/PROJECT/back.sock;
    }
```
### Old FIBER config (minimal):
```
MainApp := fiber.New(fiber.Config{
		AppName:          "Project",
		ProxyHeader:      fiber.HeaderXForwardedFor,
		TrustProxyConfig: fiber.TrustProxyConfig{Loopback: true},
	})
```
Upto version v3.0.0-rc.3, this setup worked fine and showed the visitor's real IP address when called the `ctx.IP()`
After version v3 dropped, `ctx.IP()` no longer gives the real IP and instead returns `"0.0.0.0"` and IsProxyTrusted() returns false. I changed the config block still no luck:
### New FIBER config (minimal):
```
MainApp := fiber.New(fiber.Config{
		AppName:          "Project",
		ProxyHeader:      fiber.HeaderXForwardedFor,
		TrustProxy:          true,
		TrustProxyConfig: fiber.TrustProxyConfig{Loopback: true},
	})
```
the only way i can make things work is allow "0.0.0.0" as a trusted proxy but that creates security risk
```TrustProxyConfig: fiber.TrustProxyConfig{Proxies: []string{"0.0.0.0"}}```

### How to Reproduce

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
Fiber should be behind nginx
```
MainApp := fiber.New(fiber.Config{
		AppName:          "Project",
		ProxyHeader:      fiber.HeaderXForwardedFor,
		TrustProxy:          true,
		TrustProxyConfig: fiber.TrustProxyConfig{Loopback: true},
	})

MainApp.Get("/ip", func(ctx fiber.Ctx) error {
		return ctx.SendString(ctx.IP())
	})

MainApp.Listen(unixSocket, fiber.ListenConfig{
		ListenerNetwork:    fiber.NetworkUnix,
		UnixSocketFileMode: 0760,
	})
```


### Expected Behavior

ctx.IsProxyTrusted() should return true
visiting `/ip` should give real IP address of the visitor and not "0.0.0.0" 

### Fiber Version

v3.0.0

problems.title: 🐛 bug: fix Unix-socket support in IsProxyTrusted
problems.issue_body:
This pull request addresses a potential runtime panic in the IsProxyTrusted function by adding a nil check for remote IPs and refines proxy trust logic to correctly handle Unix socket connections. It ensures that Unix socket remotes are explicitly controlled by loopback trust settings, preventing unexpected behavior and enhancing the robustness of proxy trust evaluations without impacting existing TCP/UDP remote handling

Fixes #4086

problems.title: 📝 [Proposal]: Typed binding errors with source and field information
problems.issue_body:
### Feature Proposal Description

`Bind().All()` iterates through sources (URI, Body, Query, Header, Cookie) and returns the first raw error it encounters. Callers have no way to determine *which* source or *which* field failed, because the error is an opaque `error` value from the underlying decoder.

This proposal adds a `BindError` type that wraps the original error with structured metadata:

```go
type BindError struct {
    Source string // "uri", "query", "body", "header", "cookie"
    Field  string // struct field name or tag key that failed
    Err    error  // underlying error (preserves errors.Is/As chains)
}

func (e *BindError) Error() string { ... }
func (e *BindError) Unwrap() error { return e.Err }
```

All `Bind()` methods (`URI`, `Query`, `Body`, `Header`, `Cookie`, and `All`) would return `*BindError` when a field fails to bind. `All()` would return the first `*BindError` it encounters, as it does today — but now with source context attached.

Callers use standard `errors.As` to extract the detail when they need it:

```go
var be *fiber.BindError
if errors.As(err, &be) {
    switch be.Source {
    case "uri":
        // ...
    case "body":
        // ...
    }
}
```

### Alignment with Express API

Express's validation ecosystem (express-validator, built on validator.js) returns structured error arrays with `location` (body, query, params, cookies, headers), `path` (field name), `msg`, and `value`. Developers routinely branch on `location` to produce different responses.

Fiber's binding layer is more tightly integrated than Express's — it owns the parsing, not a middleware. That's an advantage: the framework already knows the source and the target field at the point of failure, it just doesn't surface that information today.

### HTTP RFC Standards Compliance

RFC 9110 §15.5 defines distinct semantics for client error status codes:

- **400 Bad Request**: malformed syntax or invalid framing (bad query params, unparseable body)
- **404 Not Found**: the target resource is not found (bad URI path segment)

Today, when `Bind().All()` fails, the developer cannot tell whether the problem was a malformed URI parameter or a bad JSON field. The only practical option is to return a blanket 400. With source information on the error, developers can map URI binding failures to 404 and body/query failures to 400, as the RFC intends.

### API Stability

The change is additive and backward-compatible:

1. `BindError` implements `error` and `Unwrap()`, so existing code using `err.Error()` or `errors.Is(err, ...)` continues to work unchanged.
2. `errors.As(err, &be)` is opt-in — callers who don't care about the source simply ignore it.
3. The `Source` values are fixed to the five binding sources Fiber already defines. The `Field` value comes from struct tag keys already resolved during binding.

No existing API surface changes or deprecations.

### Feature Examples

```go
Today — no way to return the right status code from `All()`:


var req st…

problems.title: 📝 [Proposal]:  Host authorization middleware — DNS rebinding protection via Host header validation
problems.issue_body:
### Feature Proposal Description

 A middleware that validates the incoming Host header against a configurable allowlist, rejecting requests with unrecognized hosts with 403 Forbidden. This protects against DNS rebinding attacks, where an
  attacker-controlled domain resolves to the application's internal IP, causing browsers to send requests with a malicious Host header to the application.

### Alignment with Express API

Express does not have a built-in host authorization middleware. The closest third-party equivalent is host-validation -> https://github.com/brannondorsey/host-validation

### HTTP RFC Standards Compliance

 RFC 9112 (HTTP/1.1), Section 3.2 requires servers to respond with 400 Bad Request to requests that lack a Host header, contain multiple Host headers, or have an invalid Host value. This middleware builds on top
of that baseline requirement.

 RFC 9110 (HTTP Semantics), Section 7.2 ("Host and :authority") establishes that the Host header identifies the target origin server for the request. Section 17.1 ("Establishing Authority") states:

An origin server is responsible for ensuring services controlling its certificate's private key are prepared to reject requests that appear to have been misdirected.

This middleware implements that recommendation at the application layer by rejecting requests whose Host header does not match the expected values — going beyond the RFC's minimum syntactic validation to
provide semantic validation against DNS rebinding and host header injection attacks.

The middleware will:
 - Strip the port from the Host header before matching (per RFC 9110 Section 7.2, the host and port are separate components)
  - Respect X-Forwarded-Host when Fiber's TrustProxy is enabled
  - Return 403 Forbidden by default for unauthorized hosts (not 400, since the request is syntactically valid but semantically unauthorized)

### API Stability

The API surface is minimal and modeled after patterns already proven stable in Fiber:

  1. AllowedHosts []string — mirrors cors.AllowOrigins. String-based allowlists are the most stable configuration pattern.
  2. AllowedHostsFunc func(string) bool — mirrors cors.AllowOriginsFunc. Same escape hatch pattern.
  3. Next, Exclude, ErrorHandler — all existing Fiber conventions used across multiple middleware.
  4. Matching semantics (exact, subdomain wildcard, CIDR) — these are the same patterns Rails has used since Rails 6 (2019) without breaking changes.

No external dependencies are required — only net from the standard library for CIDR parsing. The middleware has no state, no caching, and no side effects beyond rejecting requests, which makes API evolution straightforward.


### Feature Examples

```go
Protect a production API


  app := fiber.New()

  app.Use(hostauthorization.New(hostauthorization.Config{
      AllowedHosts: []string{"api.myapp.com"},
  }))

  app.Get("/users", func(c fiber.Ctx) error {
      return c.JSON(getUsers())
  })

  // Host: api.myapp.com → 200 OK
  //…

problems.title: 🤗 [Question]: About fiber v3 log
🐛 fix(proxy): Ensure BalancerForward overwrites X-Real-IP header
problems.issue_body:
### Motivation

- Prevent header spoofing by making the proxy set the `X-Real-IP` value instead of appending to any existing header.

### Description

- Replace `Header.Add` with `Header.Set` in `BalancerForward` to ensure the `X-Real-IP` header is overwritten.
- Update the example in `docs/middleware/proxy.md` to use `Header.Set` for `X-Real-IP` consistency with runtime behavior.
- Add `Test_Proxy_Balancer_Forward_OverwritesXRealIP` in `middleware/proxy/proxy_test.go` to assert that a pre-existing `X-Real-IP` header is not retained by the forwarded request.

problems.title: 🎨 Style: Add `is`/`has`/`can` prefix to unexported boolean struct fields
problems.issue_body:
Per Go naming conventions, unexported boolean fields should use `is`/`has`/`can`/`should` prefix so they read as true/false questions. Found ~24 violations across internal (unexported) fields:

| File | Current | Suggested |
|---|---|---|
| `app.go:111` | `routesRefreshed` | `hasRoutesRefreshed` |
| `ctx.go:74` | `matched` | `isMatched` |
| `ctx.go:75` | `skipNonUseRoutes` | `shouldSkipNonUseRoutes` |
| `group.go:20` | `anyRouteDefined` | `hasAnyRoute` |
| `bind.go:42` | `dontHandleErrs` | `shouldSkipErrHandling` |
| `bind.go:43` | `skipValidation` | `shouldSkipValidation` |
| `client/request.go:72` | `disablePathNormalizing` | `isPathNormalizingDisabled` |
| `client/client.go:67` | `debug` | `isDebug` |
| `client/client.go:68` | `disablePathNormalizing` | `isPathNormalizingDisabled` |
| `client/cookiejar.go:53,58` | `hostOnly` | `isHostOnly` |
| `client/cookiejar.go:59` | `ok` | `isOk` |
| `log/default.go:29` | `ok` | `isOk` |
| `domain.go:34` | `matched` | `isMatched` |
| `middleware/session/session.go:25` | `fresh` | `isFresh` |
| `middleware/session/middleware.go:21` | `destroyed` | `isDestroyed` |
| `middleware/sse/sse.go:91` | `closed` | `isClosed` |
| `middleware/cache/manager.go:51` | `redactKeys` | `shouldRedactKeys` |
| `middleware/limiter/manager.go:27` | `redactKeys` | `shouldRedactKeys` |
| `middleware/csrf/storage_manager.go:26` | `redactKeys` | `shouldRedactKeys` |
| `middleware/logger/config.go:98` | `enableColors` | `areColorsEnabled` |
| `middleware/logger/config.go:99` | `enableLatency` | `isLatencyEnabled` |

All are unexported — no public API breakage. Purely cosmetic, but improves readability.

Please see the FAQ in our main README.md, then answer the questions below

### What version of gRPC are you using?

Upgrade from v1.59.0 to v1.60.0 for grpc client service.
Clients now take 30 seconds to re-resolve upstream services on connection error (server shutting down).

problems.title: protoc-gen-go-grpc: support proto edition
problems.issue_body:
Please see the FAQ in our main README.md before submitting your issue.

### Use case(s) - what problem will this feature solve?

Please add edition support in protoc-gen-go-grpc.
https://protobuf.dev/editions/overview/

protoc-gen-go@v1.33.0 works with protoc@v26.1 with `--experimental_editions`, but
protoc-gen-go-grpc can't use it

```
protoc-gen-go-grpc: invalid FileDescriptorProto "local_rpc_service.proto": proto: invalid syntax: "editions"
--go-grpc_out: protoc-gen-go-grpc: Plugin failed with status code 1.
```
 
### Proposed Solution

Support "edition" in protoc-gen-go-grpc.

### Alternatives Considered

Keep legacy proto2/3 when using grpc.

### Additional Context

problems.title: If a priority contains multiple localities with pick_first, load is reported incorrectly
problems.issue_body:
This should resolve when dual stack is fully implemented (#6472).  Until then, a partial implementation should be possible without requiring much throwaway work.

- [x] Create a reproduction test case for this scenario and confirm that load is not reported correctly.
- [x] Change pick_first to have one subchannel per address.  Probably skip implementing happy eyeballs for now.
- [x] Set an attribute on every subchannel creation operation (for outlier detection to read).
- [x] Disable outlier detection if pick_first is used (detected via address attribute).  (See C++ version of this change here: https://github.com/grpc/grpc/pull/33336)

That should be all we need to correct this issue.

problems.title: Improve Logger Performance When Disabled
problems.issue_body:
### Use case(s) - what problem will this feature solve?

We're seeing a large memory increase when enabling `grpc-go/xds` on a service, of a sustained ~100mb or about double the previous usage.

We tracked it down to this log line: https://github.com/grpc/grpc-go/blob/8bf2b3ee/balancer/leastrequest/leastrequest.go#L115

Which, for our environment, is creating a single 48kb line.

We don't have Info logging enabled. We have traces indicating the memory usage is from `Printf`, and is being called by the log library regardless if logging is enabled, here: https://github.com/grpc/grpc-go/blob/fbff2abb/grpclog/loggerv2.go#L194 

### Proposed Solution

I propose changing `grpc-go/grpclog` to not call `Printf` if the logger is `io.Discard`. I believe this can be done safely and without changing the interface, in manner similar to how the standard `log` package does it [here](https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/refs/tags/go1.22.5:src/log/log.go;l=203).

For what it's worth, I've personally seen this problem in the past, and saw a large performance improvement at scale, both memory and CPU, doing a very similar fix in a similar log library in Apache Traffic Control [here](https://github.com/apache/trafficcontrol/commit/63b34b1eff08d7b07ffd05969f0d88014e0d3a84). Unlike C, Go `Printf` is quite expensive because it uses Reflection. Simple benchmarking shows `Printf` is about 20x slower than `strconv.Atoi` for an int, and can be enormous for structs.

If you agree, I'm happy to make a PR.

### Alternatives Considered

We might also consider removing that log line. I don't have context for why it was added, or how useful it is. If the consensus is to remove it, I'm happy to make that PR as well. But I believe the issue will likely just recur elsewhere, and the deeper issue is incurring the `Printf` cost for disabled logs, and would very much like to fix it there.

### Additional Context

problems.title: Minor typos
problems.issue_body:
PR #7487 fixes a bunch of typos across the entire project, however it's been deemed too far reaching for a single commit from an unknown author without prior discussion. Understandable.

How should these changes be broken up? cc @purnesh42H

I admit that I didn't take the time to figure out how to install the correct protoc + plugin versions to regenerate all code (https://buf.build takes care of that in my projects) so I wouldn't be surprised if that caused some problems.

FWIW a couple of these typos have already been fixed upstream via https://github.com/grpc/grpc-proto/pull/158.

For anyone curious (cc @arvindbr8), I used a combination of the excellent [typos](https://github.com/crate-ci/typos) tool along with the "Typos" IntelliJ IDE inspection. Both methods produce a non-trivial number of false positives that have to be manually checked however.

problems.title: Change in `GRPC_XDS_BOOTSTRAP` behaviour between `grpc-go` versions
Panic in gzip compressor, possibly due to incorrect concurrent use
problems.issue_body:
In a depend-a-bot PR, to upgrade to the latest v1.71.0 version of this library ([link](https://github.com/connectrpc/conformance/pull/981)), CI encountered a panic inside the gRPC runtime. Looking at the stack-trace, it seems impossible: it's hitting a nil pointer dereference on a field named `hl`, but the preceding stack frame sets `hl` to a non-nil pointer.

The only possible way I can think of this happening is if a compressor is incorrectly used concurrently -- like another goroutine resetting this compressor in between the write and subsequent read of this field. And that could happen if it is accidentally returned to a `sync.Pool` before we are actually finished with it.

The only commit I could see between 1.70.0 and 1.71.0 that might be a culprit is #7918. I am still analyzing the code and have also been re-running this with the race detector enabled, hoping it could highlight a smoking gun. But I do know `sync.Pool` behaves a little differently when race detection is enabled. So far, it has not found anything, but I was able to reproduce the issue with a _different_ panic (described further below).

Here's the full stack trace from the panic:
```

[signal SIGSEGV: segmentation violation code=0x1 addr=0x0 pc=0x599edc]

goroutine 570 [running]:

problems.title: Expose credentials.NewRequestInfoContext as a public (experimental) API
problems.issue_body:
### Use case(s) - what problem will this feature solve?
We have a Go library that reuses gRPC code generator and many of APIs, but uses an alternative transport layer (simple HTTP request/responses): http://go.chromium.org/luci/grpc/prpc The can historically be traced to lack of HTTP2 support on Google Appengine and it solves the same problem as solved by grpc-web proxy, except without a proxy.

We are morphing this library to be closer and closer to the real gRPC client, to do a full switch at some point.

As part of that, it would be nice to be able to use PerRPCCredentials. Unfortunately, many implementations of this interface call `credentials.RequestInfoFromContext(ctx)` to check the security level etc. And this is impossible to setup without access to internal https://pkg.go.dev/google.golang.org/grpc/internal/credentials#NewRequestInfoContext

### Proposed Solution
Please consider making this API public. This will also likely help with unit-testing PerRPCCredentials implementations.

problems.title: Grpc DNS resolver timing out for simple localhost lookup
problems.issue_body:
### What version of gRPC are you using?

1.71.1

### What version of Go are you using (`go version`)?

1.24.2 Darwin/arm64

### What operating system (Linux, Windows, …) and version?

Mac m1 Sequoia 15.5

### What did you do?

This is a follow up of https://github.com/grpc/grpc-go/issues/7429#issuecomment-2905313867

Essentially tried running: 

https://github.com/grpc/grpc-go/blob/master/examples/helloworld/greeter_client/main.go

But got:

```
rpc error: code = DeadlineExceeded desc= context deadline exceeded
```

This was with

```
localhost:4317
```

It worked fine with

```
127.0.01:4317
```

### What did you expect to see?

I expected both localhost and 127.0.01 to work.

### What did you see instead?

Only 127.0.0.1 worked

I had to remove the two DNS nameservers entries on my machine to get localhost to work. But this is not an appropriate fix because I needed root to do this and breaks other functionality that does need it.

I could have also  
used passthrough://localhost:4317, but for some libraries such as OTEL exporters that use grpc it is currently not possible to pass through the scheme.

I tried net.DefaultResolver.LookupIP(ctx, 'ip', 'localhost') and it resolved with no issues quickly.

The issue seems to be that grpc DNS resolver is trying to contact the name servers even for simple /etc/hosts entries that net.DefaultResolver has no issues working with.

problems.title: xds/googlec2p: cleanup in googlec2p resolver.
problems.issue_body:
The xdsclient package contains legacy support for a "fallback" bootstrap configuration, which was originally used by the googlec2p resolver via `SetFallbackBootstrapConfig`.
This global fallback mechanism has been superseded by the changes in PR #8648. The googlec2p resolver now uses this new, explicit approach to create a xdsClient in googlec2p resolver.

To simplify the API and prevent confusion, the legacy fallback logic should be removed.
- Deprecate and remove the exported `xdsclient.SetFallbackBootstrapConfig` function.
- Remove the internal `fallbackConfig` field from the `xdsclient.Pool` and all associated logic that applies it during client creation.

problems.title: Expose custom metadata from EDS response to custom LB policies
problems.issue_body:
Please see the FAQ in our main README.md before submitting your issue.

### Use case(s) - what problem will this feature solve?
Presently, when we create a custom load balancer, it cannot access the endpoint or locality weights because they are keyed by an internal struct, so we cannot read these from Attributes. Similarly, the "metadata" returned from EDS response is also not exposed. As a result, the custom load balancer cannot make decision based on weights or metadata

### Proposed Solution
Expose at least the weights (locality weight and endpoint weight) so they can be read by custom load balancer; there is a precedence in [ringash.go](https://github.com/grpc/grpc-go/blob/master/resolver/ringhash/attr.go#L57C6-L57C13) - clients can use `ringhash.HashKey(endpoint)` to get this info. We can have something similar for weights.

### Alternatives Considered
There are no clean or viable alternatives for this missing data.

### Additional Context
NA

problems.title: grpc Server Metrics rpcInfo gets overwritten by grpc client invocations
server: Set runtime/pprof goroutine labels on server streams
problems.issue_body:
### Use case(s) - what problem will this feature solve?

Since they're inherited by child goroutines, setting [runtime/pprof.Labels](https://pkg.go.dev/runtime/pprof#Labels) on goroutines provides instrumentation that can aid debugging and introspection considerably.

As of Go 1.26, by default these labels currently show up in three places:
 - trace labels in pprof CPU profiles
 - trace labels in runtime/pprof (and http/pprof) debug=0 goroutine
   profiles (which are pprof format)
 - debug=1 aggregated text-format goroutine profiles

For Go 1.27, golang/go#76349 adds goroutine labels to tracebacks and by
extension debug=2 pprof text-based profiles for Go 1.27+ modules. There's an earlier version of this instrumentation in Go 1.26 that's visible with `GODEBUG=tracebacklabel=1`, and that same `GODEBUG` will be usable with Go 1.27 when working with modules with `go` directives specifying older Go versions. (and will be kept to allow for opting-out in case there's PII in some of these labels).

Sometimes while debugging a panic in a child goroutine, it's not clear which code-path spawned the goroutine that paniced, providing a hint as to which gRPC method handler spawned that goroutine will provide useful context.

### Proposed Solution

Set a goroutine label on all new server streams. (I'll send a PR)

In my change, the naming of the goroutine label currently matches the Open Census gRPC method tag, and has some similarities to the current proposal for goroutine tag naming for goroutine labels in the `testing` package in golang/go#75047. In particular, it uses `grpc.server.method` as the label key.

### Alternatives Considered
The status quo has been sufficient for a long time, so doing nothing is a reasonable alternative.

An alternative naming scheme for labels would be to adopt the same naming
as the `stats/opentelemetry` package and label them as `grpc.method` with
`grpc.server=true` or something to make it clear that it's not a gRPC
client call.

### Additional Context

problems.title: xds: build the interceptor chain for routes with cluster_specifier_plugins as well
problems.issue_body:
We are currently building the interceptor chain only for routes that point to weighted_clusters. See: https://github.com/grpc/grpc-go/blob/c7ec4d9ae3281bc57a8adce59b572e56965fb728/internal/xds/resolver/xds_resolver.go#L374

And not doing so in the case where the route points to a cluster_specifier_plugin. See: https://github.com/grpc/grpc-go/blob/c7ec4d9ae3281bc57a8adce59b572e56965fb728/internal/xds/resolver/xds_resolver.go#L365

This needs to be fixed. Interceptor chain needs to be build for all cluster types. As part of this change, we also need to add a test to ensure that filters work properly for routes that match to cluster_specifier_plugins.

problems.title: rls: only reset backoff on recovery from TRANSIENT_FAILURE
problems.issue_body:
Fix control channel connectivity monitoring to track TRANSIENT_FAILURE state explicitly. Only reset backoff timers when transitioning from TRANSIENT_FAILURE to READY, not for benign state changes like READY → IDLE → READY.

RELEASE NOTES: N/A

Fixes  #8693

problems.title: timeout while waiting for headers
problems.issue_body:
There doesn't seem a way to specify, on a server, after a client connection has been accepted, a read timeout so as to close the client connection after a while when no client headers have been sent at all. See for instance the [ReadTimeout](https://blog.cloudflare.com/exposing-go-on-the-internet/#timeouts) from GoLang’s HTTP Server class where you can configure this.

The http1_header_read_timeout method only starts the timer when the first line of the http1 header is sent. So if I just netcat into my hyper http server and don't send anything, the connection is kept open forever.

problems.title: hyper client happens cacel error which should retry
No `Connection: close` on HTTP1 Connection Drain
problems.issue_body: When HTTP1 connection draining is activated, the Connection: close header is not attached to responses sent to active connections. This prevents active clients from realizing that the server is requesting that the connection be closed and prevents graceful draining of HTTP1 connections.

problems.title: Intermittent panic in is_complete_fast
problems.issue_body:
**Version**
1.5.1

**Platform**
`Darwin ghost.local 23.5.0 Darwin Kernel Version 23.5.0: Wed May  1 20:19:05 PDT 2024; root:xnu-10063.121.3~5/RELEASE_ARM64_T8112 arm64`

**Description**
Hyper client can panic when processing broken up 1xx HTTP1 responses.

When a server responds with `HTTP/1.1 100 Continue
Content-Type: text/plain
Server: BaseHTTP/0.6 Python/3.12.5
Date: Mon, 16 Dec 2024 03:08:27 GMT

This is a sample text/plain document.

This is not an HTML document.

`, it's possible for hyper to first read `HTTP/1.1 100 Continue
Content-Type: text/plain
Server: BaseHTTP/0.6 Python/3.12.5
Date: Mon, 16 Dec 2024 03:08:27 GMT

`, followed by `This is a sample text/plain document.

This is not an HTML document.

`.

This triggers a panic in [the code introduced in #3764](https://github.com/hyperium/hyper/pull/3764/files#diff-aa04de01d67bf1f61d03ef30830c744fa331c7918335393ace8c6137fa4d84d6R94), since the prev_length value stored after the first response is longer than the length of the second response.

This has been hit independently by both deno and Servo upon upgrading to hyper 1.5.1, since there are web-platform-tests that exercise 1xx responses: https://github.com/web-platform-tests/wpt/blob/master/fetch/security/1xx-response.any.js

problems.title: Client support for receiving 1xx informational responses
problems.issue_body:
This is to discuss adding support for accessing 1xx informational responses on the client side (see #2426 for server side). Currently, hyper parses all 1xx responses and ignores them, as RFC 7230 says it must at least be able to receive and ignore them.

The Client (and `client::conn`) interfaces return an `impl Future<Output = Result<Response>>`, which only allows awaiting it for a single response. We can't really add new methods to allow also awaiting for informational responses, unless we stuck to always return a specialized type that is both a `Future` and has extra methods. However, _using_ those together wouldn't be that clear to a user. You can't be sure if or how many informational responses would be received before the full response, so you would want to await "both" types.

Instead, we can use a callback format. We could define a type (`hyper::client::ext::Informational`? `On1xx`? names...), and the user would store that in the request extensions. We'd then check for that in the h1 proto code and if it exists, call the callback with the 1xx headers.

problems.title: fix(http1): is_timeout works on HeaderTimeout
problems.issue_body:
Changed `hyper::Error::is_timeout()` to also check if the kind of the error is a `HeaderTimeout`. I also added a test at the end of `server.rs` to check for this kind of timeout error.

Fixes #3780

problems.title: HTTP/1.1 trailers are case-sensitive
Dropping HTTP/2 response future doesn't cancel request body send and does not send RST_STREAM
H2 streams reserve 1 more byte than needed from the connection, leading to deadlock
Hyper returns a partial byte array when its runtime is closed mid-read
HTTP/1 client busy-loops at 100% CPU when peer half-closes with an open request body (regression in 1.10.0)
problems.issue_body:
_This issue (and the linked reproducer) was authored by Claude Opus 4.8, reviewed before posting._

### Version

hyper 1.10.0

### Platform

aarch64-apple-darwin

### Summary

On **hyper 1.10.0**, an HTTP/1 **client** connection busy-loops at 100% CPU when the peer half-closes (sends FIN) **without sending a response** while the client still has an open streaming request body. The task does no I/O while spinning — it just re-polls `flush` forever. 

### Code Sample

https://github.com/shikhar/hyper-1.10-h1-spin-repro

### Expected Behavior

With the request body open and the read side finished (peer half-closed), the connection task should park and be woken when the body produces its next frame — the behavior of hyper 1.9.0.

### Actual Behavior

The connection task spins at 100% CPU indefinitely (until the application happens to produce the next body frame). The transport's `poll_flush` is called hundreds of millions of times while `poll_write`/`poll_read` are called once or twice — i.e. no I/O progress, purely spinning in `proto::h1::dispatch::Dispatcher::poll_loop`.

Same repro over real loopback TCP, only the `hyper` pin changes:

| hyper | output |
|---|---|
| **1.10.0** | `poll_flush in 2s: 127164355  poll_write=1  poll_read=2` → **BUSY-LOOP** |
| **1.9.0** | `poll_flush in 2s: 2  poll_write=1  poll_read=2` → **parked (ok)** |


### Additional Context

- **Root cause:** the only behavioral change here is the rewrite of `Dispatcher::poll_loop` (`src/proto/h1/dispatch.rs`). 1.9.0 parked as soon as it didn't want to read again; 1.10.0 added a write-continuation gate:

  ```rust
  let write_ready = self.poll_write(cx)?.is_ready();
  let flush_ready = self.poll_flush(cx)?.is_ready();
  let wants_write_again = self.can_write_again() && (write_ready || flush_ready);
  // ...
  let wants_read_again = self.conn.wants_read_again();
  if !(wants_write_again || wants_read_again) {
      return Poll::Ready(Ok(()));   // only parks if BOTH are false
  }
  if !wants_read_again && wants_write_again {
      if write_ready { continue; }                       // <-- hot path
      if self.poll_write(cx)?.is_pending() { return Poll::Ready(Ok(())); }
  }
  ```

  with `fn can_write_again(&mut self) -> bool { self.body_rx.is_some() }`.

  `can_write_again()` is `true` for the entire lifetime of a streaming request body (it only checks that a body channel exists, not that a frame is available). After the peer half-closes with no response, the read side is finished (`wants_read_again() == false`) and `poll_write` returns `Ready` without doing any I/O (the write side is winding down — the transport's `poll_write` is never called again, hence `poll_write=1`). So `write_ready == true`, `flush_ready == true` (a real TCP `poll_flush` is a no-op that returns `Ready`), and `body_rx.is_some() == true` ⇒ `wants_write_again == true`; the loop never parks, `if write_ready { continue; }` is taken every iteration, the `for _ in 0..16` runs out, and `task::yield_now(cx)` resche…

problems.title: HTTP1: max_buf_size can be bypassed
problems.issue_body:
### Version

1.10.0

### Platform

any

### Summary

In HTTP/1, `Buffered::parse`, the check only runs when `parse_headers` returns `None` (incomplete headers).


### Code Sample

Repro (with `max_buf_size(8192)` as in the current code):

```rust
  tcp.write_all(b"GET / HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: x\r\nX: ")?;
  tcp.write_all(&[b'a'; 7000])?;
  thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(100)); // force read boundary
  tcp.write_all(&[b'a'; 5000])?;
  tcp.write_all(b"\r\n\r\n")?;
```

Total head ≈ 12 KiB, well above the 8192 limit.

### Expected Behavior

Expected: `431 Request Header Fields Too Large + TooLarge error`.

### Actual Behavior

The server happily responds `HTTP/1.1 200 OK` and the handler sees the oversized X header.


### Additional Context

_No response_

problems.issue_body:
## Overview
Add performance monitoring metrics and health check API endpoints for OceanBase, following the existing ES/Infinity health check implementation.

## Technical Details

### Location
- `api/utils/health_utils.py` (lines 71-91)
- `api/apps/system_app.py` (add new health check endpoint)

### Implementation Plan

1. **Health Check Function**
   ```python
   async def check_oceanbase_health():
       """Check OceanBase health status"""
       return {
           "status": "healthy",
           "details": {
               "connection": "connected",
               "latency_ms": 5.2,
               "storage_used": "1.2GB",
               "storage_total": "100GB",
               "query_per_second": 150
           }
       }
   ```

2. **Monitoring Metrics**
   - Connection status (connected/disconnected)
   - Query latency (ms)
   - Storage space usage
   - Query throughput (QPS)
   - Slow query statistics

3. **API Endpoint**
   - Add to `/system/health` response
   - Provide OceanBase-specific health status

### Reference Implementation
- `api/utils/health_utils.py:71-91` - ES/Infinity health check

### Estimated Effort
- Code size: ~80 lines
- Difficulty: ⭐⭐ Low
- Priority: 🟡 Medium

### Related Files
- `api/utils/health_utils.py` - Health check utilities
- `api/apps/system_app.py` - System API
- `rag/utils/ob_conn.py` - OceanBase connection

## Acceptance Criteria
- `/system/health` API returns OceanBase health status
- Monitoring metrics accurately reflect OceanBase running status
- Clear error messages when health checks fail
- Response time within 100ms

## Background
This is part of the RAGFlow + OceanBase Hackathon to enable users and operations teams to monitor OceanBase health status and performance metrics for troubleshooting and system maintenance.

problems.title: [Bug] MinIO client connection ignores 'secure' configuration and hardcodes SSL to False
problems.issue_body:
Based on the discussion provided, here are the two GitHub issues formatted for the RAGFlow repository.


**RAGFlow workspace code commit ID**
latest

**RAGFlow image version**
0.23.1

**Other environment information**
Docker

**Actual behavior**
The MinIO client connection in RAGFlow is currently hardcoded to `secure=False`, making it impossible to connect to MinIO instances requiring SSL/TLS, even if the `secure` parameter is set to `True` in `service_conf.yaml`.

In `rag/utils/minio_conn.py`:
```python
def __open__(self):
    self.conn = Minio(settings.MINIO["host"],
                      access_key=settings.MINIO["user"],
                      secret_key=settings.MINIO["password"],
                      secure=False  # This is hardcoded to False
                      )
```

**Expected behavior**
The MinIO client should respect the `secure` setting from the configuration. Additionally, it should provide a way to handle self-signed certificates (e.g., via an environment variable to disable certificate verification or provide a CA bundle).

**Steps to reproduce**
1. Set up a MinIO instance with SSL enabled (HTTPS).
2. Configure `service_conf.yaml` with `secure: True`.
3. Attempt to start RAGFlow or upload a document.
4. The connection will fail or attempt to connect via plain HTTP.

**Suggested Fix**
Modify `rag/utils/minio_conn.py` to read the configuration and handle SSL contexts:
```python
    secure = settings.MINIO.get("secure", False)
    # Logic to handle SSL and optionally disable verification for self-signed certs
```

---

### Is your feature request related to a problem?

```Markdown
Currently, the auto-metadata extraction feature is only configurable through the Web UI (Dataset > Configuration > Auto metadata > Settings).
There is no way to programmatically configure auto-metadata settings via HTTP API or Python SDK when creating or updating datasets.

This limitation prevents full automation of dataset creation workflows and makes it impossible to:
- Programmatically define metadata fields with descriptions and examples
- Enable/disable "Restrict to defined values" option via API
- Create multiple datasets with different auto-metadata configurations without manual UI intervention
- Integrate dataset creation with auto-metadata setup in CI/CD pipelines or infrastructure-as-code

2. **Python SDK methods:**

problems.title: [Feature Request]: Expose parent-child chunking configuration via HTTP API and Python SDK
problems.issue_body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem?**

Parent-child chunking was introduced in v0.23.0 (PRs #11598, #11629, #11810, #11997) and is configurable via the web UI on the dataset Configuration page ("Child chunks are used for retrieval" toggle + child chunk delimiter). However, this configuration is not exposed through the HTTP API or Python SDK.
The parser_config object for chunk_method: "naive" currently accepts: auto_keywords, auto_questions, chunk_token_num, delimiter, html4excel, layout_recognize, tag_kb_ids, task_page_size, raptor, graphrag — but has no parameter for enabling child chunking or setting the child chunk delimiter.
This means users who manage datasets programmatically (via API-driven ingestion pipelines) cannot enable parent-child chunking without manually toggling it in the UI for each dataset.

**Describe the feature you'd like**

Add parent-child chunking parameters to parser_config for the naive chunk method in both the HTTP API (POST /api/v1/datasets, PUT /api/v1/datasets/{dataset_id}) and the Python SDK (RAGFlow.create_dataset(), DataSet.update()).
Suggested shape (adapt to match internal field names):

"parser_config": {
  "chunk_token_num": 512,
  "delimiter": "\n",
  "child_chunking": {
    "enabled": true,
    "delimiter": "\n"
  }
}
**Additional context**

The backend implementation already exists — this is purely about wiring the existing config to the API surface. The UI writes this config to the dataset; the API should be able to do the same.

problems.issue_body:
### Self Checks

### RAGFlow workspace code commit ID

cfdcceb

### RAGFlow image version

infiniflow/ragflow:v0.22.1

### Other environment information

```Markdown

```

### Actual behavior

When calling the open API corresponding to the workflow, the execution of the workflow is slow, taking about 3 minutes to produce results. The configured timeout is 3 minutes. When 10 concurrent requests are made, 504 and 502 timeout errors occur. After modifying the following code, the performance improved by 50%, but the concurrency is still relatively low, and resource utilization is not high.

edit http server  # ragflow_server.py  154
# app.run(host=settings.HOST_IP, port=settings.HOST_PORT)

 # Use Hypercorn for production
  import hypercorn
  import multiprocessing
  from hypercorn.config import Config
  from hypercorn.asyncio import serve
  import asyncio
            
  config = Config()
  config.workers = (multiprocessing.cpu_count() * 2) + 1
  config.bind = [f"{settings.HOST_IP}:{settings.HOST_PORT}"]
  config.workers = 4  # Adjust based on your CPU cores
  config.keep_alive_timeout = 30
  config.timeout = 60

  config.max_requests = 1000
  config.max_requests_jitter = 50
            
  asyncio.run(serve(app, config))

### Expected behavior

Under normal conditions, the system should be able to handle 30 concurrent requests within the 3-minute timeout period **without returning 502/504 errors**.


### Steps to reproduce

```Markdown
When making concurrent requests to the workflow's open API, the workflow response time approaches the configured timeout limit.
```

problems.title: [Feature Request]: Moodle connector should support deleted-file sync
problems.issue_body:
### Self Checks

- [x] I have searched for existing issues [search for existing issues](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues), including closed ones.
- [x] I confirm that I am using English to submit this report ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Non-english title submitions will be closed directly ( 非英文标题的提交将会被直接关闭 ) ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Please do not modify this template :) and fill in all the required fields.

### Is your feature request related to a problem?

```Markdown
Yes. The Moodle connector does not let the sync runner clean up indexed documents that were deleted from the source.

Other connectors such as dropbox, seafile, webdav, rss, gitlab, gmail, zendesk, asana, and bitbucket already do this through a slim snapshot pass. The connector returns a light list of every document id that still exists in the source, and the runner removes any indexed doc whose id is not in that list.

Moodle is missing from this set. After a user deletes a resource, forum, page, book, assign, or quiz module in Moodle, the matching document stays in the dataset forever and shows up in retrieval results, even though the source is gone.
```

### Describe the feature you'd like

The Moodle connector should expose a slim snapshot pass so the sync runner can remove deleted modules from the index, the same way the other connectors do it.

When the user turns on "sync deleted files" for a Moodle data source, the runner should:

1. Walk every enrolled course and module in Moodle.
2. Collect a list of slim document ids for every module that could be indexed (resource, forum, page, book, assign, quiz).
3. Compare this list with the docs already in the index.
4. Delete any indexed doc whose id is not in the list.

The snapshot must not download files. It only needs the module type and id, which is enough to build the slim id.

The option for "sync deleted files" should also show up in the web UI for Moodle, the same way it shows up for the other connectors that already support this.


### Describe implementation you've considered

_No response_

### Documentation, adoption, use case

```Markdown
Same use case the other connectors solved already. A teacher or admin removes a course resource in Moodle, and the next sync run removes the matching chunks from the dataset. Retrieval no longer returns dead links for content that no longer exists at the source.

No new docs are needed. The sync deleted files toggle for Moodle behaves the same way it does for the other connectors that already expose it.
```

### Additional information

The fix follows the pattern already merged for these connectors:

- feat(rss): support deleted-file sync (#14493)
- feat(webdav): support deleted-file sync via slim snapshot (#14491)
- feat(zendesk): support deleted-file sync (#14487)
- feat(asana): support deleted-file sync (#14468)
- feat(seafile): support deleted-file sync via slim snaps…

```Markdown

```

### Actual behavior

## [Improvement Suggestion] Metadata Filtering is Performed In-Memory Instead of Leveraging Elasticsearch, Leading to Performance Bottlenecks

### Background

In the current implementation, the `meta_filter` function in `ragflow/common/metadata_utils.py` is responsible for filtering documents based on metadata. This function loads all metadata into memory and then applies user-defined filter conditions to each document using Python code, rather than translating user filters into Elasticsearch (ES) queries and running the filtering at the ES layer.

### Problem Description

- All filtering logic is performed by iterating over data in memory; no part of the filtering is offloaded to ES.
- User-defined metadata filters are not converted to ES query DSL, so ES's indexing and filtering capabilities are not utilized.
- As the number of documents grows, this approach leads to memory inefficiency and significant performance bottlenecks.
- The current design prevents Ragflow from scaling efficiently and leveraging distributed search engines such as ES for advanced filtering.

#### Code Reference

A sample of the filtering logic:
```python
def meta_filter(metas: dict, filters: list[dict], logic: str = "and"):
    # ...
    for input, docids in v2docs.items():
        if operator == "=":
            matched = input == value
        # ... many other operators handled locally
```

### Suggested Improvement

- If metadata is stored in ES, consider converting user-defined filter conditions to equivalent ES query DSL and sending them directly to ES for server-side filtering.
- In-memory filtering can remain as a fallback or for use cases with no ES backend or very small datasets.

### Expected Outcome

- Offloading complex metadata filtering to Elasticsearch would significantly improve performance and scalability in large-data scenarios.
- Users would be able to define filter conditions that are efficiently executed at the search engine layer, making Ragflow more robust for production deployments.

If needed, I can provide specific examples or further discuss potential design changes.

### Expected behavior

_No response_

### Steps to reproduce

```Markdown
ragflow/common/metadata_utils.py
def meta_filter(metas: dict, filters: list[dict], logic: str = "and"):
```

### Additional information
…

problems.title: [Bug]: OSConnection missing create_doc_meta_idx — Document metadata update fails on OpenSearch backend
problems.issue_body:
## [Bug]: OSConnection missing `create_doc_meta_idx` — Document metadata update fails on OpenSearch backend

### What happened

When using **AWS OpenSearch** (with Elasticsearch compatibility mode enabled) as the document engine (`DOC_ENGINE=opensearch`), updating document `meta_fields` via the API always fails with:

```
Failed to update metadata
```

Server-side logs show:

```
Error updating metadata for document <doc_id>: 'OSConnection' object has no attribute 'create_doc_meta_idx'
```

This affects both:
- `PATCH /api/v1/datasets/{dataset_id}/documents/{document_id}` with `{"meta_fields": {...}}`
- `POST /api/v1/datasets/{dataset_id}/metadata/update`

All other document operations (retrieval, parsing, name update, chunk management) work correctly on OpenSearch.

### What should have happened

Document metadata should be updatable via the API regardless of the document engine backend. The `OSConnection` class should implement `create_doc_meta_idx` (and any other metadata-related methods) just like `ESConnection` does.

### How to reproduce

1. Deploy RAGFlow v0.25.1 with `DOC_ENGINE=opensearch` and an AWS OpenSearch cluster
2. Upload and parse a document in a dataset
3. Try to update the document's metadata:

```bash
curl --request PATCH \
     --url http://<ragflow_host>/api/v1/datasets/<dataset_id>/documents/<document_id> \
     --header 'Authorization: Bearer <API_KEY>' \
     --header 'Content-Type: application/json' \
     --data '{"meta_fields": {"url": "https://example.com"}}'
```

4. Response: `{"code":102,"message":"Failed to update metadata"}`
5. Server log: `'OSConnection' object has no attribute 'create_doc_meta_idx'`

### Environment

- **RAGFlow version**: v0.25.1 (also reproduced on v0.24.0)
- **Document engine**: AWS OpenSearch (with Elasticsearch compatibility mode enabled)
- **DOC_ENGINE env**: `opensearch`
- **Deployment**: Kubernetes (EKS)

### Analysis

In `common/settings.py`, when `DOC_ENGINE=opensearch`, RAGFlow uses `rag.utils.opensearch_conn.OSConnection`. However, `OSConnection` does not implement `create_doc_meta_idx`, which is called during metadata updates. The `ESConnection` class (`rag.utils.es_conn`) has this method implemented.

We also tried setting `DOC_ENGINE=elasticsearch` to use `ESConnection` with our OpenSearch cluster (compatibility mode enabled), but the `elasticsearch` Python client library (v8.x) rejects the connection to OpenSearch at the client level (`Elasticsearch ... is unhealthy`).

### Suggested fix

Implement `create_doc_meta_idx` and other metadata-related methods in `rag/utils/opensearch_conn.py` (`OSConnection` class), mirroring the implementation in `rag/utils/es_conn.py` (`ESConnection` class).

problems.title: [Bug]: Cross-tenant chatbot access via valid API key (`/chatbots/<dialog_id>/completions`, `/chatbots/<dialog_id>/info`)
problems.issue_body:
### Self Checks

- [x] I have searched for existing issues [search for existing issues](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues), including closed ones.
- [x] I confirm that I am using English to submit this report ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Non-english title submitions will be closed directly ( 非英文标题的提交将会被直接关闭 ) ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Please do not modify this template :) and fill in all the required fields.

### RAGFlow workspace code commit ID

a1b2c3d4e5f67890123456789abcdef12345678

### RAGFlow image version

0.13.1

### Other environment information

```Markdown

```

### Actual behavior

The chatbot API endpoints:

- `POST /chatbots/<dialog_id>/completions`
- `GET /chatbots/<dialog_id>/info`

validate only that the presented bearer token exists in `APIToken`, but they do **not** verify that the requested `dialog_id` belongs to the same tenant as that token.

Current flow (simplified):

1. Route extracts bearer token and checks `APIToken.query(beta=token)`.
2. If token exists, request is accepted.
3. Downstream service resolves dialog globally by ID:
   - `DialogService.get_by_id(dialog_id)` in `conversation_service.py`
4. No tenant ownership check is enforced for `dialog_id`.

As a result, any user with a valid API key can attempt arbitrary `dialog_id` values and access or invoke chatbots outside their own tenant boundary if IDs are known/guessed/leaked.

## Why this is critical

- This is a **Broken Access Control / IDOR** vulnerability (OWASP Top 10 A01).
- It can cause **cross-tenant data exposure** and unauthorized model invocation.
- Attack complexity is low once a valid API key and a target `dialog_id` are available.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Prepare two tenants:
   - Tenant A with API key `TOKEN_A`
   - Tenant B with chatbot `dialog_id = DIALOG_B`
2. Send request from Tenant A to Tenant B chatbot endpoint:

```bash
curl -X POST "https://<host>/chatbots/DIALOG_B/completions" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer TOKEN_A" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"question":"hello","stream":false}'
```

3. Observe that request is processed (or reaches dialog resolution) without tenant ownership rejection.

The same issue applies to:

```bash
curl -X GET "https://<host>/chatbots/DIALOG_B/info" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer TOKEN_A"
```

## Affected code paths

- `api/apps/sdk/session.py`
  - `chatbot_completions(dialog_id)` token validation without tenant-to-dialog ownership enforcement.
  - `chatbot_info(dialog_id)` same ownership gap.
- `api/db/services/conversation_service.py`
  - `async_iframe_completion(...)` calls `DialogService.get_by_id(dialog_id)` without tenant scoping.

### Expected behavior

Requests to `/chatbots/<dialog_id>/completions` and `/chatbots/<dialog_id>/info` must be authorized only when:

- bearer token is valid, **and**
- `dialog_id` belongs to the same `tenant_id` as the token.

Otherwise return auth…

problems.title: [Bug]: Cross-tenant document download via `GET /v1/document/get/<doc_id>` (missing tenant authorization)
problems.issue_body:
### Self Checks

- [x] I have searched for existing issues [search for existing issues](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues), including closed ones.
- [x] I confirm that I am using English to submit this report ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Non-english title submitions will be closed directly ( 非英文标题的提交将会被直接关闭 ) ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Please do not modify this template :) and fill in all the required fields.

### RAGFlow workspace code commit ID

15dcdd7b5

### RAGFlow image version

nightly (main @ 15dcdd7b5)

### Other environment information

```Markdown
- Component: Python backend (Flask/Quart) — api/apps/document_app.py
- Affected endpoint: GET /v1/document/get/<doc_id>
- Auth decorator on route: @login_required only (no tenant scoping)
```

### Actual behavior

While reading through `api/apps/document_app.py` I noticed the `/get/<doc_id>` route is gated by `@login_required` and nothing else. It calls `DocumentService.get_by_id(doc_id)` (a global lookup, no tenant filter) and then streams the file straight from MinIO via `File2DocumentService.get_storage_address(...)` and `settings.STORAGE_IMPL.get(...)`. There's no check anywhere in between to confirm the document's KB actually belongs to the caller.

In practice this means any logged-in user — including a brand new self-serve signup — can download another tenant's document just by knowing the `doc_id`. It's the actual file content, not metadata.

What makes me fairly confident this is a real bug and not intentional: every other place in the codebase that resolves a document by id does enforce tenant scoping. The SDK route in `api/apps/sdk/doc.py` does `KnowledgebaseService.query(id=doc[0].kb_id, tenant_id=tenant_id)` right after `get_by_id`, and `api/apps/restful_apis/document_api.py` does the same at several call sites (around lines 174, 1156, 1796). The web-app route is the odd one out.

There's also already a helper for exactly this check — `DocumentService.accessible(doc_id, user_id)` in `api/db/services/document_service.py` — which joins through `UserTenant` so it also handles users who belong to multiple tenants / shared team KBs. So the fix can reuse existing code.

### Expected behavior

Hitting `GET /v1/document/get/<doc_id>` should only succeed when the caller actually has access to that document via their tenant/team membership. Otherwise it should return an authorization error rather than the file bytes.

### Steps to reproduce

```Markdown
1. Set up two accounts:
   - Tenant A: any logged-in user (a fresh signup is enough).
   - Tenant B: a separate tenant with at least one uploaded document. Note its doc_id (call it DOC_B).

2. From Tenant A's session, request Tenant B's document directly:

   curl -X GET "https://<host>/v1/document/get/<DOC_B>" \
     -H "Cookie: session=<TENANT_A_SESSION>" \
     --output leaked.bin

3. The response body contains Tenant B's file, …

problems.title: [Bug]: Private datasets remain accessible to tenant members through document APIs
problems.issue_body:
### Self Checks

- [x] I have searched for existing issues [search for existing issues](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues), including closed ones.
- [x] I confirm that I am using English to submit this report ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Non-english title submitions will be closed directly ( 非英文标题的提交将会被直接关闭 ) ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Please do not modify this template :) and fill in all the required fields.

### RAGFlow workspace code commit ID

bb10b83e6114cc271c3563194464b19297dc5c25

### RAGFlow image version

infiniflow/ragflow:v0.25.1

### Other environment information

```Markdown
- Browser: N/A
- OS: Any
```

### Actual behavior

Datasets configured with `permission = me` are intended to be private to the owner, but multiple document APIs authorize access using `KnowledgebaseService.accessible()`, which only checks whether the caller belongs to the dataset's tenant and does not enforce the dataset permission flag.

This creates an authorization bypass where a non-owner tenant member can access or modify a private dataset if they know its `dataset_id`.

Any tenant member with the `dataset_id` can pass authorization checks on several document endpoints because access is granted based on tenant membership rather than the dataset's permission setting.

This can expose private document inventories and may also allow unauthorized modification actions such as document deletion, metadata updates, or parse control operations.

### Expected behavior

Only the dataset owner should be able to access a dataset marked with `permission = me`. Tenant membership alone should not grant access to private datasets.

### Steps to reproduce

```Markdown
1. Create two users in the same tenant or workspace: User A and User B.
2. As User A, create a dataset with permission set to `me` so it is private.
3. Confirm User B cannot see this dataset through normal dataset listing.
4. As User B, obtain the private dataset's `dataset_id`.
5. Call a document API that checks `KnowledgebaseService.accessible()`, for example `GET /api/v1/datasets/<dataset_id>/documents`.
6. Observe that the request succeeds even though the dataset is marked private.
```

### Additional information

_No response_

problems.title: [Bug]: `/files/link-to-datasets` lacks authorization checks for files and datasets
problems.issue_body:
### Self Checks

- [x] I have searched for existing issues [search for existing issues](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues), including closed ones.
- [x] I confirm that I am using English to submit this report ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Non-english title submitions will be closed directly ( 非英文标题的提交将会被直接关闭 ) ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Please do not modify this template :) and fill in all the required fields.

### RAGFlow workspace code commit ID

59c35100c56ab8954f1c4066cac512eb51bbf194

### RAGFlow image version

infiniflow/ragflow:v0.25.1

### Other environment information

```Markdown
- Browser: N/A
- OS: Any
```

### Actual behavior

The endpoint accepts attacker-supplied `file_ids` and `kb_ids` after existence-only validation, then removes existing file-document links and recreates documents under the supplied datasets without enforcing ownership or team-permission checks.

### Expected behavior

The endpoint should reject requests unless the authenticated user has permission to access every referenced file and every target dataset.

### Steps to reproduce

```Markdown
1. Create User A and User B as two different authenticated users.
2. As User A, upload a file and note its `file_id`. Also create a dataset and note its `dataset_id`.
3. As User B, send a `POST` request to `/files/link-to-datasets` with User A's `file_id` and `dataset_id`.
4. Observe that the request succeeds as long as the IDs exist, even though User B should not be allowed to relink User A's resources.
```

### Additional information

The `POST /files/link-to-datasets` endpoint only validates that the provided `file_ids` and `kb_ids` exist, but it does not verify that the authenticated user is allowed to access those files or datasets before relinking them.

In `api/apps/restful_apis/file2document_api.py`, the handler accepts arbitrary IDs and schedules `_convert_files(...)` after existence-only checks. Inside `_convert_files(...)`, the server removes existing file-document relations and recreates documents under the attacker-supplied datasets.

This makes the issue important because it is not just an information leak. It is a state-changing authorization flaw that can let one authenticated user modify another user's file-to-dataset mappings if they know the relevant IDs.

problems.title: [RFC] Python 3.12 → 3.13 Version Bump
problems.issue_body:
# Python 3.12 → 3.13 Version Bump

## 1. Motivation

Python 3.13 (October 2024) brings significant improvements for RAGFlow's I/O-bound async workload:

- **Free-threading** (PEP 703): No GIL — notable throughput gains for document processing pipeline
- **Improved error messages** with color tracebacks
- **New deprecations** that RAGFlow should stay current with

RAGFlow currently pins `requires-python = ">=3.12,<3.15"` in `pyproject.toml`. This RFC proposes bumping the floor to 3.13 across all surfaces.

---

## 2. Scope

### 2.1 Files to Update

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `pyproject.toml:8` | `requires-python = ">=3.12,<3.15"` → `">=3.13,<3.15"` |
| `Dockerfile:81` | `uv python install 3.12` → `3.13` |
| `Dockerfile:150` | `uv sync --python 3.12 --frozen` → `3.13` |
| `.github/workflows/tests.yml:161` | `uv sync --python 3.12 --frozen` → `3.13` |
| `.github/workflows/tests.yml:225` | `uv sync --python 3.12 --frozen` → `3.13` |
| `CLAUDE.md:121` | `Python 3.10-3.12` → `Python 3.10-3.13` |
| `uv.lock` | Regenerated with `uv lock --python 3.13` |

### 2.2 Out of Scope

- Dependency version bumps (only Python version floor changes).
- Base Docker image changes (not pinned to a specific Python minor version).
- `docker-compose-base.yml` service images (managed separately).

### 2.3 Lockfile Regeneration (Required, Not Optional)

The `uv.lock` file **must be regenerated** as part of this change — there is no post-merge CI job that does this. The frozen lockfile is used directly by `uv sync --frozen` in:

- `Dockerfile:150`
- `tests.yml:161`
- `tests.yml:225`

---

## 3. Risk Assessment

### 3.1 Compatibility Risk: **HIGH**

Most packages are safe — they declare no `<3.13` upper bound. The primary risk is a hard blocker from `graspologic`.

### 3.2 Package Audit Summary

| Package | Status | Notes |
|---------|--------|-------|
| Most dependencies | ✅ Safe | No `<3.13` upper bound |
| `graspologic` | ❌ **BLOCKS 3.13** | `requires-python = ">=3.9,<3.13"` — hard blocker |
| `onnxruntime-gpu` | ⚠️ Verify | Need to confirm py313 binary wheel exists |
| `strenum` | ⚠️ Monitor | Deprecated, no `requires-python` declared |

Full audit table in [docs/proposals/python-313-upgrade.md](docs/proposals/python-313-upgrade.md).

### 3.3 Critical Blocker: graspologic

**`graspologic`** (`git+https://gitee.com/infiniflow/graspologic.git@38e680cab72...`) is directly imported in first-party graph RAG code:

```
rag/graphrag/general/entity_embedding.py:13: import graspologic as gc
rag/graphrag/general/leiden.py:11: from graspologic.partition import hierarchical_leiden
rag/graphrag/general/leiden.py:12: from graspologic.utils import largest_connected_component
```

Its `pyproject.toml` declares `requires-python = ">=3.9,<3.13"` — it will refuse to install on Python 3.13 under any circumstances.

### 3.4 Resolution Path (Recommended Order)

| Option | Approach | Effort |
|--------|----------|--------|
| **A. Fork & patch (recommended first move)** | Fork graspol…

problems.title: [Bug]: Security — IDOR on GET /api/v1/chats (and GET /searches) via unvalidated owner_ids enables cross-tenant dialog me
problems.issue_body:
### Self Checks

- [x] I have searched for existing issues [search for existing issues](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues), including closed ones.
- [x] I confirm that I am using English to submit this report ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Non-english title submitions will be closed directly ( 非英文标题的提交将会被直接关闭 ) ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Please do not modify this template :) and fill in all the required fields.

### RAGFlow workspace code commit ID

db9085a3ba809251361fbe24753d51e27f6d223f

### RAGFlow image version

Fill from UI → System, or N/A if running from source only.

### Other environment information

```Markdown
- **Severity**: Critical (authenticated cross-tenant confidentiality / IDOR).
- **Scope**: REST `chat_api` / `search_api` in this tree; not claimed as duplicate of a specific upstream issue (local code review; contrast with already-hardened `agent_api` in the same product).
```

### Actual behavior

Any authenticated user can pass arbitrary `owner_ids` query parameters on **`GET /chats`**. The handler forwards them directly into `DialogService.get_by_tenant_ids` without verifying that each ID is a tenant the caller is allowed to see.

The database filter is effectively:

`dialog.tenant_id IN (supplied owner_ids) OR dialog.tenant_id == current_user.id`

(see `get_by_tenant_ids` in `dialog_service.py`). There is **no join or membership check** tying `owner_ids` to the caller’s teams/tenants.

The post-query line in `list_chats` only restricts rows to `chat["tenant_id"] in owner_ids`; it does **not** prove the user may query those tenants. An attacker who knows (or guesses) another user’s tenant UUID (the `tenant_id` stored on dialogs, same as owner scope elsewhere) receives that tenant’s chat list in the JSON response.

The list path builds each item with `_build_chat_response`, which exposes rich dialog fields from the ORM row (including `llm_setting`, `prompt_config`, similarity / vector weights, `rerank_id`, resolved dataset names via `kb_ids`, etc.). That is a **cross-tenant configuration and knowledge-base linkage leak**, not merely chat titles.

**Same pattern** exists on **`GET /searches`**: unvalidated `owner_ids` → `SearchService.get_by_tenant_ids` with the same “filter to requested tenants only” list comprehension and no authorization gate. Impact is somewhat lower than chats (different projection), but tenant-scoped metadata is still exposed.

**Inconsistency**: `GET /agents` already validates `owner_ids` against an authorized set derived from joined tenants and rejects unknown IDs (`list_agents` in `agent_api.py`). Chats and searches never applied that pattern.

### Expected behavior

- `owner_ids` must be intersected with (or validated against) the **same authorized tenant set** used elsewhere (e.g. pattern in `list_agents`: build `authorized_owner_ids` from membership, then `requested - authorized` → error; pa…

problems.title: [Feature Request]: Implement Rerank in the NVIDIA Go driver
problems.issue_body:
### Self Checks

- [x] I have searched for existing issues [search for existing issues](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues), including closed ones.
- [x] I confirm that I am using English to submit this report ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Non-english title submitions will be closed directly ( 非英文标题的提交将会被直接关闭 ) ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Please do not modify this template :) and fill in all the required fields.

### Is your feature request related to a problem?

```Markdown
Yes. The NVIDIA Go driver in internal/entity/models/nvidia.go has a stub Rerank method that always
returns "no such method", even though the NVIDIA NIM platform hosts dedicated reranking models:

  - nvidia/nv-rerankqa-mistral-4b-v3
  - nvidia/llama-3.2-nv-rerankqa-1b-v2

accessible via:

  POST https://integrate.api.nvidia.com/v1/ranking

Note that NVIDIA NIM exposes reranking under /v1/ranking (not /v1/rerank). The request shape is
also different from other providers: the query is a {"text": "..."} object and each passage is a
{"text": "..."} object inside a "passages" array, not a plain string array. The response returns a
"rankings" array with {"index": N, "logit": F} entries that must be sorted by index before
returning scores in the original input order.

The conf/models/nvidia.json file has no "rerank" URL suffix and no rerank models listed, so even
if Rerank worked the picker would have nothing to select.

ListModels and CheckConnection are already implemented in this driver. Rerank is the natural next
step. NVIDIA NIM is one of the most commonly used GPU-accelerated inference platforms, so a
working reranker is high value for RAG pipelines running on NVIDIA hardware.
```

### Describe the feature you'd like

Implement `NvidiaModel.Rerank` in `internal/entity/models/nvidia.go` so NIM reranking models work
end to end, and add the rerank model entries and URL suffix to `conf/models/nvidia.json`.

The driver should:

- POST to `${BaseURL}/${URLSuffix.Rerank}` (resolves to
  `https://integrate.api.nvidia.com/v1/ranking` with default config).
- Send `Authorization: Bearer <api_key>`, the same header the chat path already uses.
- Build the NVIDIA-specific request body:
  ```json
  {
    "model": "<model_name>",
    "query": {"text": "<query_text>"},
    "passages": [
      {"text": "<passage_0>"},
      {"text": "<passage_1>"}
    ],
    "truncate": "END"
  }
  ```
- Parse the `rankings` array from the response, sort entries by `index`, and return a `[]float64`
  of `logit` scores in the original input order.
- Use a per-call context deadline so a slow rerank call cannot block forever.

The NVIDIA NIM ranking response shape to parse:

```json
{
  "rankings": [
    {"index": 0, "logit": 4.75},
    {"index": 1, "logit": 2.10}
  ]
}
```

`conf/models/nvidia.json` needs two updates:

1. Add the `"rerank"` URL suffix:

```json
"url_suffix": {
  "chat": "chat/completions",
…

### Other environment information

```Markdown
- Browser: N/A (backend/API issue)
- OS: N/A
```

### Actual behavior

When `tenant_rerank_id` is provided, the code resolves the rerank model via a global ID lookup (`get_model_config_by_id(...)`) with no tenant authorization check before building `LLMBundle(...)`.

This appears to allow model-selection by raw database ID rather than by tenant-scoped authorization.

### Expected behavior

Caller-supplied tenant model IDs should be rejected unless the model belongs to the authenticated tenant, or to the dataset owner tenant if cross-tenant team sharing is explicitly supported for rerank models.

Retrieval should use only tenant-authorized rerank models, similar to the safer `rerank_id -> get_model_config_by_type_and_name(...)` path.

### Steps to reproduce

```Markdown
1.
   Prepare two tenants, A and B. Tenant A must have a rerank model configured in `TenantLLM`. Obtain or infer A's numeric `tenant_rerank_id`.

2.
   Authenticate as tenant B and call a retrieval endpoint that accepts `tenant_rerank_id`, for example `POST /api/v1/retrieval` or `POST /api/v1/searchbots/retrieval_test`, using a dataset that tenant B is allowed to access.

3.
   Set `tenant_rerank_id` in the request body to tenant A's model id and submit the retrieval request.
```

### Additional information

Some retrieval endpoints accept `tenant_rerank_id` directly from the request and resolve it with `get_model_config_by_id(...)`. That helper performs a global lookup by `TenantLLM.id` and returns decoded model configuration without checking whether the model belongs to the authenticated tenant or the dataset owner tenant.

This is important because the affected endpoints otherwise do validate dataset access, so the rerank-model path becomes an authorization bypass for model selection. A caller who knows or can guess another tenant's `tenant_rerank_id` may be able to run retrieval using a rerank model config they do not own.

Confirmed call sites include:

- `api/apps/sdk/doc.py` (`POST /api/v1/retrieval`)
- `api/apps/sdk/session.py` (`POST /api/v1/searchbots/retrieval_test`)
- `api/apps/services/dataset_api_service.py` (shared retrieval service path)

The common sink is:

- `api/db/joint_services/tenant_model_service.py:get_model_config_by_id()`

which calls `TenantLLMService.get_by_id…

problems.title: [Bug]: User Credentials (Password Hash + Access Token) Leaked in Login API Response via `to_json()`/`to_dict()`
problems.issue_body:
### Self Checks

- [x] I have searched for existing issues [search for existing issues](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues), including closed ones.
- [x] I confirm that I am using English to submit this report ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Non-english title submitions will be closed directly ( 非英文标题的提交将会被直接关闭 ) ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Please do not modify this template :) and fill in all the required fields.

### RAGFlow workspace code commit ID

b8522ed67

### RAGFlow image version

b8522ed67

### Other environment information

```Markdown
- **Files affected:**
  - `api/apps/restful_apis/user_api.py` (lines 124, 386, 531, 840)
  - `api/db/db_models.py` (lines 158–163, 707–723)
- **Python:** 3.10–3.12
- **Severity:** Critical — every successful authentication response leaks the user's bcrypt password hash and raw `access_token` to the client

---
```

### Actual behavior

Every endpoint that returns user data calls `user.to_json()` or `current_user.to_dict()`, which resolves to `self.__dict__["__data__"]` — a raw dump of **every column** in the `User` database row. The `User` model includes sensitive fields `password` (bcrypt hash) and `access_token` (the raw UUID secret that the JWT wraps), both of which are sent verbatim in the JSON response body.

**Four endpoints are affected:**

### 1. `POST /users/login` (line 124) — password hash + stale access_token leaked

```python
response_data = user.to_json()
user.access_token = get_uuid()
# ...
return await construct_response(data=response_data, auth=user.get_id(), message=msg)
```

`to_json()` is called **before** the access_token is rotated, so the response contains the **previous** access_token (which was valid until this moment) alongside the password hash.

### 2. `GET /users/me` (line 386) — password hash + current active access_token leaked

```python
return get_json_result(data=current_user.to_dict())
```

This endpoint returns the **currently active** access_token on every call. Any authenticated request to this endpoint exposes the live secret.

### 3. OAuth callback (line 531) — password hash leaked for new OAuth registrations

```python
return await construct_response(
    data=user.to_json(),
    auth=user.get_id(),
    message=f"{nickname}, welcome aboard!",
)
```

### 4. Password reset (line 840) — newly set password hash leaked immediately

```python
return await construct_response(data=user.to_json(), auth=user.get_id(), message=msg)
```

The user's brand-new password hash is returned in the response the instant they reset their password.

---

### Expected behavior

1. The `password` field (bcrypt hash) must **never** appear in any API response. Exposing the hash enables offline brute-force attacks without triggering rate limits.
2. The `access_token` field must **never** appear in any API response. The raw `access_token` UUID is the secret signed into the JWT via `URLSaf…

problems.title: [Feature Request] Add tag management for Agents with filtering and sorting support
problems.issue_body:
## Feature Description

Currently, RAGFlow Agents do not support tagging or labeling functionality. As users create more Agents, managing and organizing them becomes increasingly difficult. Tag-based management would allow users to categorize and filter Agents efficiently.

## Expected Behavior

- Users can assign one or more tags to an Agent (e.g., "customer-support", "internal", "finance", "experimental")
- The Agent list view should support filtering by tag
- Agents can be sorted by tag, or grouped by tag in the UI
- Tags should be editable and removable from the Agent settings
- Tag metadata should be searchable across the Agent list

## Use Case

- Teams with many Agents (20+) need a way to organize them by function, team, or purpose
- Quick filtering to find Agents relevant to a specific workflow or project
- Batch operations on Agents sharing the same tag (e.g., bulk enable/disable)
- Simplifies Agent governance in large-scale deployments

## Additional Context

This is a common organizational feature in most agent or bot management platforms. Tag support would significantly improve usability for power users and enterprise deployments.

problems.title: [Feature Request]: Implement Embed (embeddings) in the Mistral Go driver
problems.issue_body:
### Self Checks

- [x] I have searched for existing issues [search for existing issues](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues), including closed ones.
- [x] I confirm that I am using English to submit this report ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
- [x] Non-english title submitions will be closed directly ( 非英文标题的提交将会被直接关闭 ) ([Language Policy](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/5910)).
