problems.title: `stubtest` doesn't expand `**kwargs: Unpack[...]`
problems.issue_body:
**Bug Report**

I am working on a library where some functions have many keyword arguments in common.  Therefore, to reduce code duplication, we're taking advantage of the Python typing feature of defining a `TypedDict` to give a name to that collection of keyword arguments and using `**kwargs: Unpack[...]` in the stubs file to document these functions.

However, when these functions are implemented in Rust (using PyO3) and have all the arguments listed separately, `stubtest` reports errors, even though (I believe) these functions are implemented correctly.

**To Reproduce**

I've created a [minimal example project](https://github.com/jbytheway/stubtest-unpack) to demonstrate the problem, but I'll quote the important parts of the implementation here:

The [stubs file](https://github.com/jbytheway/stubtest-unpack/blob/main/python/stubtest_unpack/_rust.pyi) uses two different approaches to document two functions, which I believe should be equivalent, from a typing perspective:

```python
class _Args(TypedDict):
    a: int
    b: int

def f1(**kwargs: Unpack[_Args]):
    ...

def f2(*, a: int, b: int):
    ...
```

The [Rust implementation](https://github.com/jbytheway/stubtest-unpack/blob/main/src/lib.rs) implements the two identically:

```rust
    #[pyfunction]
    #[pyo3(signature = (*, a, b))]
    fn f1(a: usize, b: usize) -> PyResult<String> {
        Ok((a + b).to_string())
    }

    #[pyfunction]
    #[pyo3(signature = (*, a, b))]
    fn f2(a: usize, b: usize) -> PyResult<String> {
        Ok((a + b).to_string())
    }
```

**Expected Behavior**

No `stubtest` errors.

**Actual Behavior**

`stubtest` [reports errors](https://github.com/jbytheway/stubtest-unpack/actions/runs/23088801164/job/67069805367) for `f1`, but not for `f2`:

```
error: stubtest_unpack._rust.f1 is inconsistent, stub does not have parameter "a"
Stub: in file /home/runner/work/stubtest-unpack/stubtest-unpack/.venv/lib/python3.14/site-packages/stubtest_unpack/_rust.pyi:9
def (**kwargs: Unpack[TypedDict('stubtest_unpack._rust._Args', {'a': builtins.int, 'b': builtins.int})]) -> Any
Runtime:
def (*, a, b)

error: stubtest_unpack._rust.f1 is inconsistent, stub does not have parameter "b"
Stub: in file /home/runner/work/stubtest-unpack/stubtest-unpack/.venv/lib/python3.14/site-packages/stubtest_unpack/_rust.pyi:9
def (**kwargs: Unpack[TypedDict('stubtest_unpack._rust._Args', {'a': builtins.int, 'b': builtins.int})]) -> Any
Runtime:
def (*, a, b)

error: stubtest_unpack._rust.f1 is inconsistent, runtime does not have **kwargs parameter "kwargs"
Stub: in file /home/runner/work/stubtest-unpack/stubtest-unpack/.venv/lib/python3.14/site-packages/stubtest_unpack/_rust.pyi:9
def (**kwargs: Unpack[TypedDict('stubtest_unpack._rust._Args', {'a': builtins.int, 'b': builtins.int})]) -> Any
Runtime:
def (*, a, b)

Found 3 errors (checked 1 module)
```

**Your Environment**

You can see the full [GitHub CI output](https://github.com/jbytheway/stubtest-unpack/actions/runs/23088801164/job/67…

problems.title: [1.20 regression] loss of type inference of chained-comparison operands
problems.issue_body:
<!--
If you're not sure whether what you're experiencing is a mypy bug, please see the "Question and Help" form instead.
Please consider:

- checking our common issues page: https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/common_issues.html
- searching our issue tracker: https://github.com/python/mypy/issues to see if it's already been reported
- asking on gitter chat: https://gitter.im/python/typing
-->

**Bug Report**

<!--
If you're reporting a problem with a specific library function, the typeshed tracker is better suited for this report: https://github.com/python/typeshed/issues

If the project you encountered the issue in is open source, please provide a link to the project.
-->

[My repo](https://gitlab.com/TTsangSC/pytest-autoprofile)[^disclaimer-not-a-plug] has a [line of code](https://gitlab.com/TTsangSC/pytest-autoprofile/-/blob/v0.19.1/.mock-ci/pytest_autoprofile_local_ci/commands.py?ref_type=tags#L54) which uses a chained comparison to check if two `str | None` operands are equal and are not `None` (see example below). Said code failed type-checking in [yesterday's pipeline](https://gitlab.com/TTsangSC/pytest-autoprofile/-/jobs/13742751853#L121) which pulled the latest `mypy` from PyPI. Apparently, since 1.20.0[^disclaimer-version-inconsistency] the `a` argument is no longer narrowed to a `str`.

[^disclaimer-not-a-plug]: Provided for context only since the issue template encourages so: `If the project you encountered the issue in is open source, please provide a link to the project.`

[^disclaimer-version-inconsistency]: Note that I failed to replicate the differing behaviors between 1.19 and 1.20 on `mypy` Playground. Instead, "mypy latest (1.20.0)" retained the behavior from 1.19, while "mypy master branch" replicated the narrowing failure I saw with 1.20.0 in the above pipeline and on my local machine.

**To Reproduce**

Gist URL: https://gist.github.com/mypy-play/dc825cee3a47c4b77265efb1e253ec02

```python
from typing import reveal_type


def some_func(a: str | None = None, b: str | None = None) -> None:
    if None is not a == b:
        reveal_type(a)
        reveal_type(b)
        print(a + b)  # Stand-in for some code using them as strings
    else:
        ...  # Some other processing happens here
```

**Expected Behavior**

<!--
How did you expect mypy to behave? It’s fine if you’re not sure your understanding is correct.
Write down what you thought would happen. If you expected no errors, delete this section.
-->

`a` should narrow to `str`, and also `b` by virtue of its equality with `a`.[^disclaimer-string-subtype]

[^disclaimer-string-subtype]: As noted in **Actual Behavior**, `b` is not narrowed in any recent version. Maybe `mypy` is trying to be more conservative in the narrowing, accommodating for the off-chance of some `str` subclass overriding `.__eq__()` to somehow compare to `True` with `None`... ?  May have to do with (the discussion around) #10708.

**Actual Behavior**

<!-- What went wrong? Paste mypy's output. -->

Be…

problems.title: [1.20 regression] narrowing failure with unrelated boolean
problems.issue_body:
**Bug Report**

Mypy 1.20 and master report an incorrect error that seems to be a failure of type narrowing in the following case:

```python
class FancyInteger(int):
    name = 'fancy'

    def __repr__(self):
        return f'{int(self)}!'

    def __str__(self):
        return f'{int(self)}!'


def stringify(value: object) -> str:
    if isinstance(value, (str, int)) or value is None:
        if value.__class__.__str__ == FancyInteger.__str__ and isinstance(value, FancyInteger):
            return f"{value.__class__.__name__}.{value.name}"
    return str(value)
```

[**playground link**](https://mypy-play.net/?mypy=latest&python=3.12&gist=a6f8ba95151fa5e0003273b812d8223e)

This passes in MyPy 1.19.1, and is ostensibly correct code -- this is a minimised version, but the original performs similar checks with `enum.StrEnum`, so not that esoteric.

If the `value.__class__.__str__ == FancyInteger.__str__` comparison is removed, or split to a new `if` statement, the error fails to reproduce.

**To Reproduce**

https://mypy-play.net/?mypy=latest&python=3.12&gist=a6f8ba95151fa5e0003273b812d8223e

**Expected Behavior**

No error

**Actual Behavior**

```shell
$ uvx mypy==1.20
bug.py:14: error: Item "str" of "str | int" has no attribute "name"  [union-attr]
bug.py:14: error: Item "int" of "str | int" has no attribute "name"  [union-attr]
Found 2 errors in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
$ uvx mypy==1.19.1
Success: no issues found in 1 source file
```

**Your Environment**

- Mypy version used: 1.20 & 1.19.1
- Mypy command-line flags: None
- Mypy configuration options from `mypy.ini` (and other config files): None
- Python version used: 3.12 & 3.14

problems.title: [1.20 regression] unreachable regression with generic Callable

**Bug Report**

Since mypy 1.20.0, a comparison between 2 generic callables may cause  "Statement is unreachable"

**To Reproduce**

```python
from typing import Callable

def identity[T](x: T) -> T:

def msg[T,S](cmp_property: Callable[[T], S]) -> None:

```

problems.title: Mypy shoould not suggest Foo[...] if annotation contains Foo(arg=...) etc.
[1.20 regression] when same object is narrowed multiple times
problems.issue_body:
**Bug Report**

There was a regression in narrowing with isinstance checks from 1.19 to 1.20. This seems to only happen when the same object is narrowed multiple times. For example, this works correct if removing the outer `is not None` guard and the `| None` from the argument. 

**To Reproduce**

[mypy playground link](https://mypy-play.net/?mypy=1.20.0&python=3.12&gist=cc164caa960acb22ff1f9a42b68eac14)

```python
from __future__ import annotations


def _score_test(
    exc: BaseException | None, expected_excs: set[type[BaseException]]
) -> None:
    if exc is not None:
        reveal_type(exc)
        reveal_type(expected_excs)  # Revealed type is "BaseException"
        reveal_type(tuple(expected_excs))  # Revealed type is "tuple[type[BaseException], ...]"
        if isinstance(exc, tuple(expected_excs)):
            reveal_type(exc)  # Revealed type is "object"
            return logexc(exc)  # Argument 1 to "logexc" has incompatible type "object"; expected "BaseException | None"

def logexc(
    exc: BaseException | None
) -> None:
    ...
```

problems.title: Drop support for Python 3.9
problems.issue_body:
Python 3.9 is reaching the end of its life tomorrow. We should also drop support from mypy at some point, though not necessarily immediately.

Concrete issue: https://github.com/python/mypy/blob/72131392483c19a6b8db812f4d4acfdb50a769dd/.github/workflows/test.yml#L84-L85 currently uses a macos-13 runner, which GitHub is working to deprecate. Therefore, this job may start failing in the next month or so. We could drop this one job and still keep best-effort 3.9 support for some time, or we could just use this as a reason to drop 3.9 for now. (Thanks @hugovk for pointing me to this.)

Please write here if support for Python 3.9 is still important to you.

problems.title: Undefined names used as decorators on top of `TypedDict` are not reported
[1.20 regression] Type narrowing is order sensitive in match-case statement with narrowed enum type
problems.issue_body:
## Bug Report

Under specific circumstances, changing the order of `case`-statements in a `match`-statement influences mypy's type narrowing, leading to flaky `assert_never` errors.

Specifically, this happens you narrow enum values to specific enum values using `Literal` and combine that narrowed type with other types in a union.

(I've not done an exhaustive search, but I've only seen this with `Literal[SomeEnum.SPECIFIC_VALUE]`.)

### This bug does not occur with mypy==1.19

I've tried to reproduce this bug with Mypy 1.19, but it only occurs with Mypy 1.20. (We ran into this after upgrading Mypy.)

## To Reproduce

In the script below, the only different between the `dummy_class_then_enum` and `enum_then_dummy_class` is the order of the `case`-statements within the `match` statement.

In both instances, all possible value types are handled, but `mypy` will throw an error on the `assert_never` in the `enum_then_dummy_class` function.


```python
import enum
from typing import Literal, assert_never


class DummyClass:
    """A dummy class."""


class MyEnum(enum.StrEnum):
    """A dummy enum class."""

    RELEVANT = enum.auto()
    IGNORED = enum.auto()


type MyUnionType = DummyClass | Literal[MyEnum.RELEVANT]


def dummy_class_then_enum(arg: MyUnionType) -> str:
    match arg:
        case DummyClass():
            return "dummy class"
        case MyEnum.RELEVANT:
            return "relevant"
        case _ as unreachable:
            assert_never(unreachable)


def enum_then_dummy_class(arg: MyUnionType) -> str:
    match arg:
        case MyEnum.RELEVANT:
            return "relevant"
        case DummyClass():
            return "dummy class"
        case _ as unreachable:
            assert_never(unreachable)



```

### Expected Behavior

Mypy reports no errors (or errors for both functions).

### Actual Behavior

We only get an error for the second function, not the first.

```
(mypy-demo) sebastiaan@python MypyDemo % mypy bug_demo.py
bug_demo.py:36: error: Argument 1 to "assert_never" has incompatible type "Literal[MyEnum.RELEVANT]"; expected "Never"  [arg-type]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```

## Your Environment

- Mypy version used: `1.20`
- Mypy command-line flags: None
- Mypy configuration options from `mypy.ini` (and other config files): None
- Python version used: `3.14.3`

problems.title: [1.20 regression] Inconsistent narroving for unions involving Any
problems.issue_body:
Consider this example:
```python
from typing import Any

class B:
    x: int

class C(B):
    y: int

x: B | int
y: C | Any

assert x == y
reveal_type(x)  # Revealed type is "test.B | int", OK

y.y  # Error here, previously this worked.
```
cc @hauntsaninja I didn't bisect, but there is high chance this may be caused by one of the recent narrowing changes.

problems.title: TypedDict does not support type refinement in subclasses
problems.issue_body:
### Are you reporting a bug, or opening a feature request?

Feature Request

### Please insert below the code you are checking with mypy.

```py
from typing_extensions import Literal, TypedDict
from typing import Optional

class Super:
    type: str

class Foo(Super):
    type: Literal['foo']

class SuperDict(TypedDict):
    type: str

class Bar(SuperDict):
    type: Literal['bar']

class SuperTuple(NamedTuple):
    type: str

class Baz(SuperTuple):
    type: Literal['baz']
```

### What is the actual behavior/output?

Even though `Literal['bar']` is compatible with type `str`, as shown in the other two examples, the following error is returned.

```
test.py:14: error: Cannot overwrite TypedDict field "type" while extending
```

### What is the behavior/output you expect?

PEP 589 states the following:

> Additional notes on TypedDict class inheritance:
> * Changing a field type of a parent TypedDict class in a subclass is not allowed
> * Multiple inheritance does not allow conflict types for the same name field

The current behavior of TypedDict is in-line with the specification, but that restriction prevents type refinement for subtypes, a restriction that is not shared by regular classes or NamedTuple subclasses. PEP 589 does not appear to mention why that additional restriction is given to typed dicts specifically, and this restricts how well TypedDict is able to express certain structures.

Could this limitation be lifted or is there a specific reason for it to apply?

problems.title: [1.20 regression] False positive for checking reachability on dict with single-variant enum keys
problems.issue_body:
**Bug Report**
After upgrading from 1.19.x to 1.20.x, I'm seeing a false positive with reachability checking for `dict`s with single-variant `Enum` keys.

**To Reproduce**

https://mypy-play.net/?mypy=latest&python=3.12&flags=warn-unreachable&gist=3ba2a6f89ea69ebf0ce78f0d91fb0e7f

```py
import enum

class Color(enum.Enum):
    RED = enum.auto()

def foo(colors: list[Color]) -> None:
    counts: dict[Color, int] = {}
    for color in colors:
        if color not in counts:
            counts[color] = 0  # error: Statement is unreachable  [unreachable]
        counts[color] += 1
```

If I [add a second `Enum` variant](https://mypy-play.net/?mypy=1.20.2&python=3.12&flags=warn-unreachable&gist=3ab07115fc955b11e27bf639c337cd8c), the error goes away.

Interestingly, the error also goes away if I [remove the explicit type annotation](https://mypy-play.net/?mypy=1.20.2&python=3.12&flags=warn-unreachable&gist=8fad802fc75f8f5db0dae53f1a297352).

**Expected Behavior**

[With v1.19.1](https://mypy-play.net/?mypy=1.19.1&python=3.12&flags=warn-unreachable&gist=3ba2a6f89ea69ebf0ce78f0d91fb0e7f), I get no errors.

problems.issue_body:
mypy_primer in typeshed has started flakily showing the introduction or removal of an error on pandas.

This typically looks like:
```
pandas (https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas)
+ pandas/core/computation/ops.py:328: error: Need type annotation for "_binary_ops_dict" (hint: "_binary_ops_dict: dict[<type>, <type>] = ...")  [var-annotated]
```

You can see this in a number of typeshed PRs https://github.com/python/typeshed/pulls?q=is%3Apr+_binary_ops_dict

Here's an example error from April 7 https://github.com/python/typeshed/pull/15622#issuecomment-4197149226. Typeshed updated to 1.20 on April 1.

The interesting thing is I suspect this will not repro with stock mypy (e.g. we haven't seen it in mypy's mypy_primer)

I think what is going in is that you also need these typeshed PRs to `operator.py`. I reverted these PRs for us because they are bad for our users (for other reasons): https://github.com/python/mypy/pull/21232#issuecomment-4374615030

problems.title: Support exhaustiveness checking for 'in' statements (enums)
problems.issue_body:
**Feature**

Allow the use of the `in` statement when doing exhaustiveness checking.

```python
from enum import Enum
from typing import assert_never


class MyEnum(Enum):
    A = 1
    B = 2
    C = 3 


def my_function(a: MyEnum) -> bool:
    if a == MyEnum.A:
        return True
    elif a in (MyEnum.B, MyEnum.C):
        return False
    assert_never(a)


my_function(MyEnum.A)

```

Currently, this will raise the following exception:
```
error: Argument 1 to "assert_never" has incompatible type
"Literal[MyEnum.B, MyEnum.C]"; expected "NoReturn"  [arg-type]
        assert_never(a)
```

where the following would not:

```python
def my_function(a: MyEnum) -> bool:
    if a == MyEnum.A:
        return True
    elif a == MyEnum.B:
        return False
    elif a == MyEnum.C:
        return False
    assert_never(a)
```

**Pitch**

If you wish to do the same thing for multiple enums in that particular function and also want exhaustiveness checking, you need to type each case out individually and then return the same thing, which is not great (DRY etc...).

problems.title: Support enum type narrowing with `in`
problems.issue_body:
The syntax `some_enum_value in [list_of_values]` should narrow the enum to that list of values.

This is a list extension to https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/10915 "support narrowing enum values using == and !="
Unlike the == case, there is no equivalent `is` syntax for lists that will narrow the type.

This example shows the motivation for this feature, as well as the syntax that is expected to work.

```
from typing import Literal, Union, cast
from enum import Enum


class SomeType(Enum):
    a = 1
    b = 2
    c = 3


LimitType = Union[Literal[SomeType.a, SomeType.b]]
AllowedLimitType = [SomeType.a, SomeType.b]


def inner_check(limit: LimitType) -> None:
    pass


def outer_check(some: SomeType) -> None:
    if some in [SomeType.a, SomeType.b]:
        inner_check(some)  # Incompatible type

    if some in AllowedLimitType:
        inner_check(some)  # Incompatible type

    if some == SomeType.a or some == SomeType.b:
        inner_check(some)  # Incompatible type
        inner_check(cast(LimitType, some))

    if some is SomeType.a or some is SomeType.b:
        inner_check(some)

    if some == SomeType.a:
        inner_check(SomeType.a)

    # This would be ideal
    # if some in LimitType:
    #   inner_check(some)

def main() -> None:
    outer_check(SomeType.a)


main()
```

problems.title: Incompatible return value when using union bound for generic function (false positive)
problems.issue_body:
**Bug Report**

Using union as a bound for generic function type parameter `T`.
Function takes one argument of type `type[T]` and returns `T`.
mypy cannot infer equivalence between instance of `type[T]` and return type `T`.

The error is not reproduced if:
 * single concrete type is used for bound (`func[T: int]`)
 * no bound used (`func[T]`)

**To Reproduce**

```python
def func[T: bool | int | float | str](ftype: type[T], value: str | None) -> T:
    if value is None:
        return ftype()
    return ftype(value)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    print(func(bool, 'True'))
    print(func(bool, None))
    print(func(int, '1'))
    print(func(int, None))
    print(func(float, '1.0'))
    print(func(float, None))
    print(func(str, 'test'))
    print(func(str, None))
```

https://mypy-play.net/?mypy=latest&python=3.12&gist=3bc312ee3a5620cbd789bfa1c93152ec

**Expected Behavior**

No errors

**Actual Behavior**

```sh
main.py:3: error: Incompatible return value type (got "int | float | str", expected "T")  [return-value]
main.py:4: error: Incompatible return value type (got "int | float | str", expected "T")  [return-value]
```

**Your Environment**

- Mypy version used: 1.19.1
- Python version used: 3.12

problems.title: `fail_after` deadline is set on initialization not context entry
problems.issue_body:
This is a duplicate of https://github.com/agronholm/anyio/issues/514 — they are following the convention established here in trio.

When using a `fail_after` context, the deadline is set at "initialization" time rather than `__enter__`. This behavior can result in unexpected bugs if the context is declared before it is entered. This is not particularly intuitive for a Python context manager — I'd expect the timer to start when the context is entered. I do not think changing it would be complex, but it could be considered breaking.

```python
import trio


async def main():
    ctx = trio.fail_after(5)
    await trio.sleep(5)
    with ctx:
        for i in range(1, 6):
            print(i)
            await trio.sleep(1)


trio.run(main)
```

problems.title: Problems with `trio.Lock()` across multiple tasks
problems.issue_body:
Some time ago - never mind how long precisely - having a little coordination between tasks and nothing particular to interest me in semaphores, I saw our `RuntimeError("can't release a Lock you don't own")`.

I approve pretty strongly of keeping things local by default; it prevents all sorts of bugs and `trio.Semaphore` is right there if you need something without this limitation.  But two further observations prompted this issue:

1. `trio.Semaphore()` is considerably more powerful and flexible in general.  By the principle of least power, it might be nice to have `trio.Lock(*, allow_outside_release: bool = False)` or something along those lines.
2. If the owning task finishes (returns or raises) _without releasing the lock_, then you're stuck forever.  The toy program below demonstrates.  Perhaps we should raise an error or at least emit a warning when a task which owns a lock finishes?

```python
import trio

@trio.run
async def main():
    lock = trio.Lock()
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        nursery.start_soon(lock.acquire)
    # The lock is now held, and can only be released by the acquiring task.
    # However, that task is now finished, so we can never release the lock!
    lock.release()
```

problems.title: Add exception message for easier debugging with `Cancelled`
problems.issue_body:
Occasionally we end up digging through logs trying to work out why something was cancelled, and eventually it occurred to me that Trio could help with this.  I don't have a complete design proposal, but it would be great to handle each of three cases:

- a deadline was reached.
- a sibling task in the `Nursery` raised an exception, including the name of the erroring task
- someone called `CancelScope.cancel()`, including the name of the task in which this was called
  - add a new `reason: str | None = None` param, so that users can explicitly annotate why they cancelled something

I think we could represent this as a string argument to `Cancelled` like any other exception message; the only tricky part will be threading it through all the cancellation-delivery machinery 🙂 

Comments and design input most welcome, of course!

problems.title: Internal error from nursery mis-nesting (due to bad use of `AsyncExitStack`)
problems.issue_body:
[contextlib.AsyncExitStack](https://docs.python.org/3/library/contextlib.html#contextlib.AsyncExitStack) is quite dangerous, in that it makes it very easy to break the nesting structure of context managers.  If you manage to do that to a nursery, bad things result:

<details>

```python
from contextlib import AsyncExitStack, asynccontextmanager
import trio

async def main():
    async with AsyncExitStack() as stack, trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        # The asynccontextmanager is going to create a nursery that outlives this nursery!
        nursery.start_soon(
            stack.enter_async_context,
            asynccontextmanager_that_creates_a_nursery_internally(),
        )

@asynccontextmanager
async def asynccontextmanager_that_creates_a_nursery_internally():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        nursery.start_soon(
            print_sleep_print, "task_in_asynccontextmanager_nursery", 2.0
        )
        yield

async def print_sleep_print(name: str, sleep_time: float):
    print(f"{name} is about to sleep for {sleep_time} seconds")
    await trio.sleep(sleep_time)
    print(f"{name} finished sleeping for {sleep_time} seconds")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    trio.run(main)
```
```python-traceback

task_in_asynccontextmanager_nursery is about to sleep for 2.0 seconds
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".../python3.11/site-packages/trio/_core/_run.py", line 2872, in unrolled_run

problems.title: Define `trio.Event.__bool__()` to reduce bugs
problems.issue_body:
I've now hit enough bugs where someone wrote `if event:` rather than `if event.is_set():` that I think `Event.__bool__()` should raise an error of some kind, likely `NotImplementedError("Trio events cannot be treated as bools; consider using 'event.is_set()'")`.

I could be argued into `def __bool__(self): return self.is_set()` but disprefer this - "there should be one obvious way to do it".  Thoughts?

I don't think there's as urgent a need on other classes, but we could consider e.g. Condition and Semaphore too...

problems.title: `except*` can break `@trio.as_safe_channel` cleanup
problems.issue_body:
If we `break` out of a loop over some receive-channel created with `@trio.as_safe_channel`, the async generator will be cleaned up in the background, and indeed this works:

```python
import trio

async def main():
    async with inner_generator() as recv_chan:
        async for _ in recv_chan:
            break  # Early exit

@trio.as_safe_channel
async def inner_generator():
    try:
        for x in range(10):
            await trio.lowlevel.checkpoint()
            yield x
    except BaseException:  # breaks if `except*`!
        raise
    await trio.lowlevel.checkpoint()

trio.run(main)
```

..._unless you use `except*`._  

Add one little `*` to the program above, and we instead crash with a `BaseExceptionGroup(..., [GeneratorExit()])` - and if you have an outer generator wrapping around the inner one, you'll instead see our `BaseExceptionGroup: Encountered exception during cleanup of generator object, as well as exception in the contextmanager body - unable to unwrap` error.

Inserting `if eg.subgroup(GeneratorExit): raise GeneratorExit from eg` before the inner `try:` block [here](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/5bfba27b23f9a1f1bac86880b341bcfab54e43f2/src/trio/_channel.py#L553-L555) fixes this issue and I _think_ is the right approach, but I'd love to hear your takes.

problems.title: `as_safe_channel` could wrap exception groups less
problems.issue_body:
Just realized the "Encountered exception during cleanup of generator object ..." exception group... contains the exception group with multiple exceptions!? That looks weird when printed!

_Originally posted by @A5rocks in https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/3331#issuecomment-3304681339_

problems.title: Replace `trio.testing.RaisesGroup` with `pytest.RaisesGroup` and `trio.testing.Matcher` with `pytest.RaisesExc`
problems.issue_body:
pytest 8.4 released `RaisesGroup`, and it's already better than `trio.testing.RaisesGroup` with more informative error messages.
I think we can get away with simply aliasing `trio.testing.RaisesGroup` to `pytest.RaisesGroup` and `trio.testing.Matcher` to `pytest.RaisesExc`, maybe with a short period where we raise a DeprecationWarning if somebody accesses them without `pytest` available.

We could also start raising warnings straight away for accessing them, given that there's not much reason for keeping them around.

problems.title: exceptions raised before calling `task_status.started` loses cause & context
problems.issue_body:
This snippet eats cause&context

https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/9c909fa10d7a9d9e1cfd019725f826848d05bf81/src/trio/_core/_run.py#L1387-L1389

a la https://flake8-async.readthedocs.io/en/latest/rules.html#async123

we currently only run the ruff reimplementation of flake8-async, which hasn't added that rule. So we should maybe consider running flake8-async as well, disabling any rules that ruff handles [better], or are noisy.

It should make use of `trio._util.raise_saving_context`.

repro:
```python
import trio


async def task(task_status: trio.TaskStatus) -> None:
    e = ValueError("foo")
    e.__cause__ = SyntaxError("bar")
    e.__context__ = TypeError("baz")
    raise e

async def main() -> None:
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        await nursery.start(task)

try:
    trio.run(main)
except ExceptionGroup as excgroup:
    # should be SyntaxError, is None
    print(excgroup.exceptions[0].__cause__)
    # should be TypeError, is the exceptiongroup
    print(repr(excgroup.exceptions[0].__context__))
```

problems.title: Layout/MultilineMethodCallIndentation false positive for method chains inside hash pairs in method arguments
problems.issue_body:
In RuboCop 1.84.2, a false positive was introduced for the Layout/MultilineMethodCallIndentation rule. When a method chain containing hash pairs is passed as an argument to another method, nested method chains within those hash values are incorrectly flagged as misaligned, despite being correctly indented.

              .where(id: Xyz.select(:id)

  end
end

No offenses found

problems.title: Style/IfInsideElse autocorrect moves condition into the wrong place
problems.issue_body:
If an `else` block starts with a comment, `Style/IfInsideElse` should not move that comment down into the following `true` branch of the following condition.

--------

## Steps to reproduce the problem

Run the `Style/IfInsideElse` cop (with autoformatter) on the following:

```ruby
if cond_a
  body_a
else
  # Not A!
  
  if cond_b
    body_b
  else
    body_c
  end
end
```

## Expected behavior

No `Style/IfInsideElse` should be triggered, and the autoformatter should leave the code intact.

## Actual behavior

The code is autocorrected to the following

```ruby
if cond_a
  body_a
elsif cond_b
  # Not A!

  body_b
else
  body_c
end
```

Here, the comment `# Not A!` erroneously is inside the `cond_b` true condition.

```

^

blah.rb:6:3: C: [Correctable] Style/IfInsideElse: Convert if nested inside else to elsif.

  ^^

problems.title: Template files are not correctly autocorrected
problems.issue_body:
There is an issue where the template support feature does not work correctly with RuboCop's autocorrect feature, and I’m not sure how to modify `lib/rubocop/runner.rb` to fix it, so could you help me?

The problem is as follows:

For example, suppose `example.erb` contains three `<% ... %>` blocks, and each of them has one offense.

```erb
<% ... %>
<% ... %>
<% ... %>
```

When running `rubocop --autocorrect example.erb`, RuboCop detects all three offenses and shows them as `[Corrected]` in the output, but in reality only the last reported offense is actually autocorrected in the file. Because of this problem, for example, with this template file, you would need to run `rubocop --autocorrect example.erb` a total of three times before all corrections are actually applied.

Inside `RuboCop::Runner`, what happens is that autocorrect is applied three times for `example.erb`. However, each of these is treated as if it were a different file. As a result, the `File.write` from the second autocorrect overwrites the changes from the first, and the `File.write` from the third overwrites the second, so only the last correction remains.

The reason this happens is that, to support templates, the existing implementation represents each embedded Ruby snippet in the same template file as a combination of file path + offset. `RuboCop::Runner` then treats these as different files.

I believe this issue can be resolved if we can modify the code so that these snippets are recognized as the same file and autocorrections for the same file are properly grouped and applied.

Also, although this is unrelated to the autocorrect issue, it seems that the caching mechanism doesn’t work properly for files analyzed using the template API. I haven’t investigated this issue in detail yet, so I can’t say anything definitive at this point.

---

relevant links:

- https://github.com/rubocop/rubocop/pull/10839
- https://github.com/r7kamura/rubocop-slim/issues/12
- https://github.com/r7kamura/rubocop-haml/issues/18

problems.title: Style/EmptyClassDefinition casts too wide of a net
problems.issue_body:
## Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

[Style/EmptyClassDefinition](https://docs.rubocop.org/rubocop/latest/cops_style.html#styleemptyclassdefinition) currently requires one to select between:

  ```ruby
  # class_keyword style
  class MyModel < ApplicationRecord  # <--- Should be good
  end
  ```
and:
  ```ruby
  class MyService
    # class_new style
    Error = Class.new(StandardError)  # <--- Should also be good
  end
  ```

Must these be mutually exclusive options? They have different contexts.

## Describe the solution you'd like

Maybe we need a distinction between top-level classes and sublclasses? Could be a separate cop even. But in my above example, I want both to be good. I think what I want to be able to have an empty top-level class, but not an empty subclass.

So, could have Style/EmptyClassDefinition only consider top-level classes. Then could have Style/EmptySubClassDefinition consider only subclasses? (Might prefer renaming Style/EmptyClassDefinition -> Style/EmptyTopLevelClassDefinition as well, for symmetry?)

## Describe alternatives you've considered

I've currently disabled Style/EmptyClassDefinition. Essentially all I really care about here is the ability to define "Error" classes using the `class_new` style, tbh.

## Additional context

I'm currently on Rubocop 1.85.0. Was testing the new changes to Style/EmptyClassDefinition.

---

By the way, I think the [documentation for Style/EmptyClassDefinition](https://docs.rubocop.org/rubocop/latest/cops_style.html#styleemptyclassdefinition) fell out of date in v1.85.0. It still says:

> The supported styles are:
> class_definition (default) - prefer standard class definition over Class.new
> class_new - prefer Class.new over class definition

But I think the top option is meant to be `class_keyword` now.

Also, Configurable Attributes table at the bottom lists 3 options (not 2) under Configurable values. Remove `class_definition` here?

problems.title: Run in parallel when generating TODO file
problems.issue_body:
(Re-/)generating the TODO file is currently quite slow in large repos, and I suspect it runs in a single thread/process.

Would it be possible to run in parallel when generating the TODO file? I think I read once, somewhere, that it wouldn’t be possible, but I forgot the arguments behind that statement.

problems.title: RuboCop::Cop::Documentation.url_for returns nil for builtin Cops with no instance methods defined
problems.issue_body:

problems.title: Ruby syntax error missing reported as a zero-length range at EOF
problems.issue_body:
This happens when using Solargraph + RuboCop diagnostics: `Lint/Syntax` for a missing `end` is reported as a zero-length range at EOF (`start == end`), which makes inline highlighting unreliable in some editors (like [here](https://github.com/zed-extensions/ruby/issues/259)).

--------

## Steps to reproduce the problem

1. Create `test.rb` with:
```
if true
  puts 'x'
```
2. Use Solargraph (with RuboCop diagnostics enabled) and inspect `textDocument/publishDiagnostics` for this file.

Diagnostic payload received:
```
[
  {
    "range": {
      "start": { "line": 2, "character": 0 },
      "end": { "line": 2, "character": 0 }
    },
    "severity": 1,
    "source": "rubocop",
    "code": "Lint/Syntax",
    "message": " unexpected token $end\n(Using Ruby 2.7 parser; configure using `TargetRubyVersion` parameter, under `AllCops`)"
  }
]
```
## Expected behavior

For `missing end`, the diagnostic range should be non-empty (or otherwise point to a visible span), so editors can reliably show inline error highlighting.

## Actual behavior

In Solargraph + RuboCop diagnostics, `Lint/Syntax` for `unexpected token $end` is reported as a zero-length range at EOF (`start == end`).

## RuboCop version

 1.85.0 (using Parser 3.3.10.2, rubocop-ast 1.49.0, analyzing as Ruby 2.7, running on ruby 3.4.8) [x86_64-linux]

problems.title: AllCops/Exclude silently ignored when project root is inside a dot-prefixed ancestor directory
problems.issue_body:
### Before submitting

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and this hasn't been reported yet.
- [x] I am running the latest stable version of RuboCop.

### Issue type

Other

### Cop name

_No response_

### Steps to reproduce the problem

When rubocop is run from a project root that has a dot-prefixed ancestor directory anywhere in its absolute path (e.g. a git worktree checked out under ~/.cache/, .claude/worktrees/…, or .config/…), AllCops/Exclude patterns are silently ignored. Every ruby-ish file under the tree is inspected, regardless of the Exclude configuration.
   
#  Steps to reproduce                                                                                                                                                                                                          
```                                                                   
  mkdir -p /tmp/.hidden/myproj/lib/tasks /tmp/.hidden/myproj/spec
  cd /tmp/.hidden/myproj                                                                                                                                                                                                      
   
  cat > .rubocop.yml <<'YAML'                                                                                                                                                                                                 
  AllCops:                                                         
    NewCops: enable                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    Exclude:                                                       
      - 'lib/**/*.rake'
      - 'spec/*_helper.rb'                                                                                                                                                                                                    
  YAML
                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  cat > lib/tasks/demo.rake <<'RB'                                                                                                                                                                                            
  namespace :demo do
    task :hello do                                                                                                                                                                                                            
      puts "hi"                                                    
    end
  end
  RB

  cat > spec/rails_helper.rb <<'RB'                                                                                                                                                                                 …

problems.title: Style/Copyright cop autocorrecting without hashtag/comment
problems.issue_body:
### Before submitting

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and this hasn't been reported yet.
- [x] I am running the latest stable version of RuboCop.

### Issue type

Incorrect autocorrect

### Cop name

Style/Copyright

### Steps to reproduce the problem

1. Create a file `example.rb` with a single line of `puts 'Hello world'`
2. Add the .rubocop.yml configuration file, as below. (Try both configs.)
3. Run `rubocop -A example.rb`.
4. Review terminal output and code file.

### Expected behavior

The first configuration file should insert the AutocorrectNotice text above the line of code, with an octothorpe and a space (# ) prefacing it and not result in further warnings.

The second configuration file should insert the AutocorrectNotice text above the line of code and not result in further warnings.

### Actual behavior

The first configuration file will insert the AutocorrectNotice text above the line of code, without a # prefacing it, resulting in invalid Ruby code. This results in other cops reporting issues, if enabled, and code that doesn't run.

The second configuration file will result in a warning, saying `Style/Copyright: AutocorrectNotice '# Copyright (c) 2026 My Name' must match Notice /^# Copyright (\(c\) )?2[0-9]{3} .+/ (from file: ...`.

### RuboCop configuration

```yaml
There are two configs worth inspecting - the first omits the # from the config (and relies upon the autocorrect to place it), while the second includes the # in the config and fails to autocorrect with a warning due to not matching the regex.

AllCops:
  DisabledByDefault: true

Style/Copyright:
  Enabled: true
  Notice: '^Copyright (\(c\) )?2[0-9]{3} .+'
  AutocorrectNotice: 'Copyright (c) 2026 My Name'

--------

AllCops:
  DisabledByDefault: true

Style/Copyright:
  Enabled: true
  Notice: '^# Copyright (\(c\) )?2[0-9]{3} .+'
  AutocorrectNotice: '# Copyright (c) 2026 My Name'
```

### RuboCop version

```shell
$ rubocop -V
1.86.1 (using Parser 3.3.11.1, rubocop-ast 1.49.1, analyzing as Ruby 2.7, running on ruby 3.4.6) [arm64-darwin25]
```

problems.title: Layout/ClosingParenthesisIndentation and Layout/CommentIndentation allegedly do not support IndentationWidth parameter
problems.issue_body:
### Before submitting

- [x] I am running the latest stable version of RuboCop.

### Issue type

Other

### Cop name

Layout/ClosingParenthesisIndentation,Layout/CommentIndentation

### Steps to reproduce the problem

Test file (uses tabs, not spaces!)

```rb
class Test
	expect(something).to eql(
		foo: 123
	)
	# This is a comment
end
```

### Expected behavior

Rubocop either does not show any errors here (with Layout/ClosingParenthesisIndentation and Layout/CommentIndentation commented out in the config), or I can configure these two cops with indentation width.

### Actual behavior

Rubocop gives these errors:

Layout/ClosingParenthesisIndentation: Indent `)` to column 0 (not 1)
Layout/CommentIndentation: Incorrect indentation detected (column 1 instead of 2).

When I comment in the whole configuration, it does not give errors in the file, but when running the rubocop CLI:

```
Warning: Layout/ClosingParenthesisIndentation does not support IndentationWidth parameter.

Supported parameters are:

  - Enabled

Warning: Layout/CommentIndentation does not support IndentationWidth parameter.

Supported parameters are:

  - Enabled
  - AllowForAlignment
```

However, the IndentationWidth seems to do exactly what I wanted and fixes the problems!

### RuboCop configuration

```yaml
# Layout/ClosingParenthesisIndentation:
#   IndentationWidth: 1

# Layout/CommentIndentation:
#   IndentationWidth: 1

Layout/IndentationStyle:
  EnforcedStyle: tabs

Layout/FirstArgumentIndentation:
  IndentationWidth: 1
```

problems.title: [Safe Autocorrect Bug] Style/RedundantParentheses swallows chained methods into inline comments
problems.issue_body:
### Before submitting

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and this hasn't been reported yet.
- [x] I am running the latest stable version of RuboCop.

### Issue type

Incorrect autocorrect

### Cop name

Style/RedundantParentheses

### Steps to reproduce the problem

When Style/RedundantParentheses autocorrects a multiline expression, it deletes the closing parenthesis and the preceding newline character. If the inner expression ends with an inline comment, this text-deletion causes any trailing chained methods to be pulled up into the comment, breaking the code or swallowing linter directives.

Steps to Reproduce
1. Create a file named example.rb with the following code:
```ruby
member_ids = (
  [1, 2, 3].map { |i| i } # rubocop:disable Rails/Pluck
).uniq
```
2. Run RuboCop with autocorrect for this specific cop:
```sh
bundle exec rubocop example.rb --only Style/RedundantParentheses -A
```





### Expected behavior


The autocorrect should either preserve the execution of .uniq by handling the comment safely, or it should safely bail out and refuse to autocorrect if an inline comment is detected at the end of the begin node's line.

Example of safe correction:
```ruby
member_ids = [1, 2, 3].map { |i| i } # rubocop:disable Rails/Pluck
  .uniq
```

### Actual behavior

The newline and closing parenthesis are blindly deleted, causing .uniq to become part of the inline comment. The method is no longer executed, and the RuboCop disable directive becomes invalid.
```ruby
member_ids = [1, 2, 3].map { |i| i } # rubocop:disable Rails/Pluck.uniq
```

### RuboCop configuration

```yaml

```

### RuboCop version

```shell
1.84.2
```

problems.title: Fix Lint/ParenthesesAsGroupedExpression false positive with yield
problems.issue_body:
### Before submitting

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and this hasn't been reported yet.
- [x] I am running the latest stable version of RuboCop.

### Issue type

False positive

### Cop name

Lint/ParenthesesAsGroupedExpression

### Steps to reproduce the problem

1. Create a `example.rb` file.
2. Add this line: `def rewrite = read.then { |content| write yield(content) if block_given? }`
3. Run `rubocop example.rb`.

### Expected behavior

I would not expect this to cause an error. This was working fine prior to RuboCop 1.86.2.

### Actual behavior

Here's what you see first:

``` 
[Correctable] Lint/ParenthesesAsGroupedExpression: yield(content) interpreted as grouped expression.
```

When running auto-correct, this gets worse, as you end up with this code which is syntactically incorrect:

``` ruby
def rewrite = read.then { |content| writeyield(content) if block_given? }
```

Notice that the `write` and `yield` message are completely collapsed as a `writeyield` message.

### RuboCop configuration

Here's my full [configuration](https://github.com/bkuhlmann/caliber/blob/main/config/all.yml) if it helps.

### Steps to reproduce the problem

Create a `.rubocop.yml` file with the provided configuration.

Create `example.rb` with the following content:
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

i = 0
while i < 3 do i += 1
end

while true
  break 123
end
```

Run `rubocop -A example.rb`

### Expected behavior

I expected it to not introduce a syntax error into working code.

### Actual behavior

```
$ cat example.rb
# frozen_string_literal: true

i = 0
while i < 3 do i += 1
end

while true
  break 123
end
$ ruby example.rb
$ rubocop -A example.rb
Inspecting 1 file
F

Offenses:

example.rb:4:13: F: Lint/Syntax: unexpected token tIDENTIFIER
(Using Ruby 3.3 parser; configure using TargetRubyVersion parameter, under AllCops)
while i < 3 i += 1
            ^
example.rb:4:13: C: [Corrected] Style/WhileUntilDo: Do not use do with multi-line while.
while i < 3 do i += 1
            ^^
example.rb:5:1: F: Lint/Syntax: unexpected token kEND
(Using Ruby 3.3 parser; configure using TargetRubyVersion parameter, under AllCops)
end
^^^
example.rb:7:1: C: [Corrected] Style/InfiniteLoop: Use Kernel#loop for infinite loops.
while true
^^^^^

1 file inspected, 4 offenses detected, 2 offenses corrected
$ ruby example.rb
example.rb: --> example.rb

expected a predicate expression for the `while` statement

> 3  i = 0
> 4  while i < 3 i += 1
> 5  end

example.rb:4: syntax error, unexpected local variable or method, expecting `do' for condition or ';' or '\n' (SyntaxError)
while i < 3 i += 1
            ^
$ cat example.rb
# frozen_string_literal: true

i = 0
while i < 3 i += 1
end

loop do
  break 123
end
```

### RuboCop configuration

```yaml
AllCops:
  DisabledByDefault: true
  TargetRubyVersion: 3.3
  
Style/WhileUntilDo:
  Enabled: true

Style/InfiniteLoop:
  Enabled: true
```

### RuboCop version

```shell
1.87.0 (using Parser 3.3.11.1, Prism 1.9.0, rubocop-ast 1.49.1, analyzing as Ruby 3.3, running on ruby 3.3.8) [x86_64-linux-gnu]
```

problems.title: Cop idea: Rails/Env
problems.issue_body:
## Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

A problem that large Rails applications have is consistent behaviour across environments. Many application maintainers choose to solve this by using feature flags instead of environment flags for particular code behaviour.

## Describe the solution you'd like

Instead of relying on `env.production?` or some environment in an application, we should push the dependency into an environmental config and rely on a feature flag mechanism. like `if Feature.active? ...` by detecting it with a Rubocop rule

## Additional context
This is a custom Rubocop rule that we have ran in production for many years and is part of our initiative to open source and contribute some of our rules upstream

Fans of the [12 factor app](https://12factor.net/config) design philosophy will find this particularly useful.

problems.title: Enable non-default locations of checked files
problems.issue_body:
When using engines, the path for controllers, models, etc. are different from the core application which forces gem users to enable "Include" for all cops with this configurable option.

It would be great if there was a possibility to have some configurable global parameters, for instance:

```
Rails:
  -controllers_path: '**/app/controllers'
  -models_path: '**/app/{models,decorators}'
```

problems.title: Missing case for Rails/EnvLocal
problems.issue_body:
The `Rails/EnvLocal` cop will detect cases like

```rb
Rails.env.development? || Rails.env.test?
```

and

```rb
Rails.env.development? || Rails.env.test? || foo?
```

but it will currently not detect this case:

```rb
foo? || Rails.env.development? || Rails.env.test?
```

Would it be possible to change the cop to catch such a case?

problems.title: Rails/TransactionExitStatement false positive with until loop
problems.issue_body:
Similar to https://github.com/rubocop/rubocop-rails/issues/658 but using a `until` loop rather than `loop`:

```ruby
ApplicationRecord.transaction do
  until stop_looping?
    break if condition

    do_something
  end
end
```
has offence: 

```
Inspecting 1 file
C

Offenses:

loop.rb:3:5: C: Rails/TransactionExitStatement: Exit statement break is not allowed. Use raise (rollback) or next (commit).
    break if condition
    ^^^^^

1 file inspected, 1 offense detected
```

--------

## Expected behavior

No offense - the `break` affects the `until` not the `transaction`.

## Actual behavior

Offense found. 

## Steps to reproduce the problem

Code as above

## RuboCop version

```
$ bundle exec rubocop -V
1.77.0 (using Parser 3.3.8.0, rubocop-ast 1.45.1, analyzing as Ruby 2.7, running on ruby 3.3.7) [arm64-darwin24]
  - rubocop-performance 1.24.0
  - rubocop-rails 2.32.0
  - rubocop-rspec 3.3.0
```

problems.title: More Rails/Output false-positives when using Phlex p() tag helper
problems.issue_body:
Similar to #1141, there's a couple more false-positives when trying to use the `p` tag helper.

When using a `p` helper without a block (this is a bit of an edge-case, and could be replaced with a `div()`):

```
app/aspects/chats/components/skeleton_message.rb:42:13: C: Rails/Output: Do not write to stdout. Use Rails's logger if you want to log.
            p(class: "h-4 bg-gray-200 rounded-full dark:bg-neutral-700", style: "width: 60%;")
```

And when you provide a block variable:

```
app/components/ui/blank_state.rb:13:11: C: Rails/Output: Do not write to stdout. Use Rails's logger if you want to log.
          p(class: "...", &@content) if @content
```

Perhaps the cop could be made to be configurable, such that the values in `RESTRICT_ON_SEND` (currently a const) could be customized, and exclude the `p`.

--------

## Expected behavior

Rubocop Rails/Ouput should not offend when using the `p()` helper in Phlex.

## Actual behavior

#1141 updated it to not flag a `p` helper provided with a block literal. There are a few more instances

## Steps to reproduce the problem

Have a phlex component with this code:

```ruby
def view_template
  content = "hello"
  p(&content)
  p(class: "placeholder")
end
```

Rails/Output will flag both those `p` methods as errors.

## RuboCop version

```
1.75.4 (using Parser 3.3.8.0, Prism 1.4.0, rubocop-ast 1.44.1, analyzing as Ruby 3.4, running on ruby 3.4.4) [x86_64-linux]
  - rubocop-rails 2.31.0
```

problems.title: Rails/FindByOrAssignmentMemoization autocorrect behavior leads to unintuitive test failures
problems.issue_body:
In our codebase, we have some code that like this:
```
def user =  @user ||= User.find_by(id: params.require("user_id"))
```
This code currently fails the `Rails/FindByOrAssignmentMemoization` rubocop rule.
--------

## Expected behavior

I expect that running `rubocop -A` will lead to changes that make sense, i.e the following, per the
[rails style guide](https://rails.rubystyle.guide/#find-by-memoization)

```
  def user
      return @user if defined?(@user)

      @user = User.find_by(id: params.require("user_id"))
    end
```

## Actual behavior

Instead, the result is:
```
    if defined?(@user)
      attr_reader :user
    end

    @user = User.find_by(id: params.require("user_id"))

```

This leads to a really difficult to understand issue when running `bundle exec rspec` for any tests on circleCI (but rspec works locally):

```
An error occurred while loading ./spec/services/recover_care_pathway_answers_spec.rb.
Failure/Error: require File.expand_path("../config/environment", __dir__)

problems.title: Rails/FindByOrAssignmentMemoization is not a safe autocorrect
problems.issue_body:
## Expected behavior

I expect Rubocop auto fixes to only auto fix things that are guaranteed not to change the behavior of the code.  `Rails/FindByOrAssignmentMemoization` auto fix can significantly change the code behavior in cases where instance variables are explicitly defined as nil. This is more common after the introduction of object shapes in Ruby 3.2.

*I see now in the docs that this auto fix is marked as unsafe, so I guess there is another issue I had that allows unsafe auto correction after upgrading rubocop, but I still think this rule makes too many assumptions.

Specifically:

```ruby
class Parent
  def initialize(name:)
    @name = name
    @user = nil
  end

  def user
    @user ||= User.find_by(name: @name)
  end
end
```

Gets auto corrected to:


```ruby
class Parent
  def initialize(name:)
    @name = name
    @user = nil
  end

  def user
    return @user if defined?(@user)

    @user = User.find_by(name: @name)
  end
end
```

Which changes the behavior. Now, user will always be nil.

Blog post about object shapes:
https://railsatscale.com/2023-10-24-memoization-pattern-and-object-shapes/

problems.title: Incorrect autocorrect on `RedirectBackOrTo` when fallback location is a hash argument
problems.issue_body:
In cases where the `fallback_location:` for `redirect_back` is a hash, the autocorrect in `Rails/RedirectBackOrTo` is missing the parentheses needed to differentiate between a hash argument and a block argument.

--------

## Steps to reproduce the problem

Here's an example method from our app

```ruby
def change_failed(errors)
  flash[:errors] = errors
  redirect_back fallback_location: { action: 'index' }
end
```

## Expected behavior

The autocorrection need parens:

```ruby
def change_failed(errors)
  flash[:errors] = errors
  redirect_back_or_to({ action: 'index' })
end
```

## Actual behavior

```
app/controllers/concerns/non_maneuver_score_controller.rb:47:5: W: [Corrected] Rails/RedirectBackOrTo: Use redirect_back_or_to instead of redirect_back with :fallback_location keyword argument.
    redirect_back fallback_location: { action: 'index' }
    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^
app/controllers/concerns/non_maneuver_score_controller.rb:47:33: F: Lint/Syntax: unexpected ':', expecting end-of-input
(Using Ruby 3.4 parser; configure using TargetRubyVersion parameter, under AllCops)
    redirect_back_or_to { action: 'index' }
```

```
$ bundle exec rubocop -V
1.81.7 (using Parser 3.3.10.0, Prism 1.6.0, rubocop-ast 1.48.0, analyzing as Ruby 3.4, running on ruby 3.4.3) [x86_64-linux]

problems.title: DatabaseTypeResolvable does not respect shared default configuration
problems.issue_body:
Rubocop cannot detect the correct adapter configuration reliably if the `database.yml` makes use of `shared:` default configuration.

## Steps to reproduce the problem

Use a `database.yml` that looks like the following:

```yaml
shared:
  adapter: postgresql

development:
# no adapter configuration here
```

## Expected behavior

Similar to how Rails would merge configuration from `shared`, Rubocop should use the shared config if it contains an adapter configuration.

## Actual behavior

Rubocop ignores `shared`, which leads to some cops (e.g. Rails/BulkChangeTable) not applying the configuration properly.

This affects all Rubocop versions.

problems.title: `Rails/StrongParametersExpect` correction fails when parameter is a Hash/Array and the code is calling `.delete` or `.ea
problems.issue_body:
Much like #1624.

```ruby
params[:key].delete(:inner)
```

is corrected to

```ruby
params.expect(:key).delete(:inner)
```

which fails with `ActionController::ParameterMissing: param is missing or the value is empty or invalid: key`

Also happens with `.each`:

```ruby
params[:key].each do |_index, subparams|
  # ...
end
```

is corrected to

```ruby
params.expect(:key).each do |_index, subparams|
  # ...
end
```
which also fails in the same way.

```
1.86.2 (using Parser 3.3.11.1, Prism 1.9.0, rubocop-ast 1.49.1, analyzing as Ruby 4.0, running on ruby 4.0.1) [x86_64-linux]
  - rubocop-rails 2.35.1
  - rubocop-minitest 0.39.1
  - rubocop-performance 1.26.1
  - rubocop-capybara 2.23.0
```

problems.title: reload() leaves the app dead with no recovery when startInternal() fails
ThreadInterruptShutdownHook hangs reload indefinitely when user's main() ignores interrupts
[Bug] gRPC proxy ignores configured listen host and binds to all interfaces
problems.issue_body:
## Summary
The gRPC live-reload proxy advertises and documents `live.reload.proxy.grpc.host`/
`LIVE_RELOAD_PROXY_GRPC_HOST` as the host to bind the proxy to, defaulting to `localhost`.

In practice, the gRPC proxy startup path ignores that host and starts the server with only a port:

[GrpcDevServerStart.java] (../jvm-live-reload/core/webserver-grpc/src/main/java/me/seroperson/reload
/live/webserver/grpc/GrpcDevServerStart.java:68).

The result is that a user can configure `LIVE_RELOAD_PROXY_GRPC_HOST=127.0.0.1`, see the banner claim `grpc://127.0.0.1:9000`, but the process actually listens on `*:9000`.

## Reproduction Steps
1. Publish local artifacts:

```sh
PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH" \
JDK_JAVA_OPTIONS="--add-opens=java.base/java.util=ALL-UNNAMED --add-opens=java.base/java.lang=ALL-UNNAMED" \
just
```

2. Start the sbt gRPC fixture with loopback-only proxy host:

```sh
cd /jvm-live-reload/sbt/src/test/resources/grpc-scala3

PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH" \
LIVE_RELOAD_PROXY_GRPC_HOST=127.0.0.1 \
JDK_JAVA_OPTIONS="--add-opens=java.base/java.util=ALL-UNNAMED --add-opens=java.base/java.lang=ALL-UNNAMED" \
sbt -Dproject.version="$(../jvm-live-reload/version.txt)" run
```

3. Inspect the listening socket:
```sh
ss -ltnp '( sport = :9000 or sport = :8080 )'
```

4. Connect to the proxy through the machine’s non-loopback IPv4 address:
```sh
timeout 3 bash -lc 'ip=$(ip -4 addr show scope global | awk '\''/inet / {sub("/.*", "", $2); print $2; exit}'\''); echo "testing $ip:9000"; : < /dev/tcp/$ip/9000; echo CONNECTED'
```
5. Make a real gRPC call to the non-loopback address.

## Actual Result

The banner says the proxy is only on loopback:

```text
Serving at:    grpc://127.0.0.1:9000
```

But the kernel socket table shows all-interface bind:

```text
LISTEN 0 4096 *:9000 *:* users:(("java",pid=1713316,fd=189))
```

TCP connection to the non-loopback address succeeds:

```text
testing 37.60.255.86:9000
CONNECTED
```

A real gRPC request to the non-loopback address succeeds:

```text
37.60.255.86 -> Scala3-Hi
```
Server logs confirm the external gRPC call triggered the live-reload path:

```text
Proxying method: greeter.Greeter/Greet
Reloading an application
Starting App
Server started on port 8080
Finished reloading
```

## Expected Result

When `LIVE_RELOAD_PROXY_GRPC_HOST=127.0.0.1`, the gRPC proxy should bind only to `127.0.0.1:9000`. Connections to non-loopback interfaces should fail.
## Evidence
Relevant docs declare the host setting:

[README.md](https://github.com/seroperson/jvm-live-reload/blob/main/README.md)

Relevant setting exists and defaults to `localhost`:

[DevServerSettings.java]      (https://github.com/seroperson/jvm-live-reload/core/build-link/src/main/java/me/seroperson/reload/live/settings/DevServerSettings.java:99)

The startup path ignores the host and binds by port only:

[GrpcDevServerStart.java]        (https://github.com/seroperson/jvm-live-reload/blob/main/core/webserver-grpc/src/main/java/me/seroperson/reload/live/webser…

problems.title: Startup health hook hangs forever after application `main()` crashes before binding the server
problems.issue_body:
## Summary
If the application `main()` throws before the HTTP server starts, `jvm-live-reload` logs the exception in the app thread but the request that triggered startup never receives an error.

`HealthCheckStartupHook` keeps polling `/health` forever, even though the app thread is already dead and the target port will never become healthy.

## Reproduction Steps
1. Publish local artifacts:
```sh
export JDK_JAVA_OPTIONS="--add-opens=java.base/java.util=ALL-UNNAMED --add-opens=java.base/java.lang=ALL-UNNAMED"
./gradlew publishToMavenLocal
```

2. Create a temporary Gradle app using the plugin with:
```kotlin
liveReload {
    settings = mapOf(
        "live.reload.http.port" to "18180",
        "live.reload.proxy.http.port" to "19180",
        "live.reload.debug" to "true"
    )
}
```

3. Use an app that crashes before opening the target server:
```java
public class App {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    System.out.println("APP_MAIN_ABOUT_TO_CRASH");
    throw new RuntimeException("boom before binding HTTP server");
  }
}
```

4. Start:
```sh
./gradlew liveReloadRun --info --continuous
```

5. Trigger first startup through the proxy:
```sh
curl --max-time 5 -i http://localhost:19180/anything
```

## Actual Result
The app crash is logged:
```text
🔃 Reloading an application
🚀 Starting App
APP_MAIN_ABOUT_TO_CRASH
Error in application main thread
Caused by: java.lang.RuntimeException: boom before binding HTTP server
```

But the client receives no response and times out:
```text
curl: (28) Operation timed out after 5004 milliseconds with 0 bytes received
```

The proxy remains listening while the target app never bound its port:
```text
LISTEN 127.0.0.1:19180 users:(("java",pid=2799074,...))
```

## Expected Result
When the application main thread dies during startup, the startup hook should abort and the proxy should return a clear HTTP error instead of hanging indefinitely.

## Evidence
`BaseDevServerStart.startInternal()` starts the app thread, logs `InvocationTargetException`, but does not propagate failure to the startup hook:
```java
appThread.start();
runHooks(appThread, classLoader, startupHooks);
```

`HealthCheckStartupHook` loops forever on connection failure:
```java
while (true) {
  var healthResponse = isHealthy(...);
  if (healthResponse == 1) return;
  else if (healthResponse == 0) Thread.sleep(50L);
  else if (healthResponse == -1) Thread.sleep(50L);
}
```

## Root Cause
Startup readiness polling only observes the health endpoint. It does not observe application thread liveness or capture exceptions thrown by `main()`.

## Security/Business Impact
A simple startup bug can wedge the dev proxy indefinitely. Developers get hanging HTTP requests instead of actionable feedback, and must kill the build JVM to recover.

## Suggested Fix
Track app thread startup failure and stop polling when the app thread exits before health succeeds.

Possible remediation:
```java
if (!th.isAlive()) {
  throw new UnrecoverableException("Appli…

problems.title: The use of text/template disables dead code elimination in all users of cobra
problems.issue_body:
Any use of `reflect.Value.MethodByName()` or `reflect.Type.MethodByName()` disables the DCE. The compiler assumes that these functions will look any method up, and cannot remove methods even if they are never called.

`text/template` is a very notable offender in this respect. It calls `MethodByName()` with random strings extracted from template texts.

`Command::UsageFunc()` and `Command::HelpFunc()` use `text/template`, hence they disable the DCE for all users of cobra. Since cobra is the go-to module to implement CLIs, this essentially disables DCE in all command line tools written in go.

A low-level library that is used by almost everyone should not pessimise the code generation by disabling the DCE. I'd avoid `text/template` altogether.

problems.title: Completion helper
problems.issue_body:
Following #2220

Right now the format for completion is not obvious. I discovered in ov tool

```go

_ = rootCmd.RegisterFlagCompletionFunc("tab-width", func(_ *cobra.Command, _ []string, _ string) ([]string, cobra.ShellCompDirective) {
	return []string{"3\ttab width", "2\ttab width", "4\ttab width", "8\ttab width"}, cobra.ShellCompDirectiveNoFileComp
})
```

https://github.com/noborus/ov/blob/4f4e9e5c266ec4815c5a166718e318992ea47751/main.go#L431-433

By not .obvious, I mean the `tab` is logic but not something you will think of, when you easily find out in the documentation.

I might be overthinking and what is needed is simply about adding documentation about the format in the methods

So, let's talk 😁

problems.title: Feature request: Set a default completion function
problems.issue_body:
In my experience, not many flags actually require completion of files.

Some have dynamic completions, but a great deal of them take free-form string parameters where no completion can be offered.
Unfortunately, the default completion for a flag with no completion function in Cobra is file completion.
This forces me to register a `cobra.NoFileCompletions` for each flag of that category.

As an example, completion for `docker run` has 56 flags where no meaningful FlagCompletionFunc exists.
In a first attempt, I registered 56 handlers with `cmd.RegisterFlagCompletionFunc("flag-name", cobra.NoFileCompletions)` just to silence their file completion.
Following a hint of @thaJeztah, I replaced them with a programmatically installed default handler, see [here](https://github.com/docker/cli/commit/06260e68f3686a0368284a8ce9b910e056bb523b#diff-50fe347eb4bdafebc190aa42b2caa34b089a538cd444b8a151a763372e93c0cf).

I would very much welcome a solution in Cobra, where I can set a default handler on command level, something like

```go
cmd.CompletionOptions.DefaultCompHandler = cobra.NoFileCompletions
```

With this feature, I'd only have to define the completion functions for flags that actually have meaningful completions.

/cc @thaJeztah

problems.title: Completions modify os.Args
problems.issue_body:
I'm trying to workaround https://github.com/spf13/cobra/issues/1877 by inspecting `os.Args` by hand. In some cases cobra accidentally inserts `--` into `os.Args`.

Minimal example:
```go
package main

import (
	"os"
	"strings"

	"github.com/spf13/cobra"
)

func main() {
	cmd := &cobra.Command{
		TraverseChildren: true,
		ValidArgsFunction: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string, toComplete string) ([]cobra.Completion, cobra.ShellCompDirective) {
			cobra.CompErrorln(strings.Join(os.Args[1:], ", "))
			return nil, cobra.ShellCompDirectiveDefault
		},
	}
	if err := cmd.Execute(); err != nil {
		panic(err)
	}
}

```

Expected behaviour:
```console
$ go run . __complete x
[Debug] [Error] __complete, x
:0
Completion ended with directive: ShellCompDirectiveDefault
```

Actual behaviour:
```console
$ go run . __complete x
[Debug] [Error] __complete, --
:0
Completion ended with directive: ShellCompDirectiveDefault
```

problems.title: Provide a public API like RequestBody.Empty
problems.issue_body:
I'm using version 5.0.0-alpha14, and found that we have a public `commonEmptyRequestBody` in `-UtilCommon.kt`.

```kotlin
val commonEmptyHeaders: Headers = Headers.headersOf()
val commonEmptyRequestBody: RequestBody = EMPTY_BYTE_ARRAY.toRequestBody()
val commonEmptyResponse: ResponseBody = EMPTY_BYTE_ARRAY.toResponseBody()
```

I think we can have them `internal` and public them like this:
```kotlin
// with @JvmField

Headers.Empty
RequestBody.Empty
ResponseBody.Empty
````
This type might fit Kotlin more. Also we can easier access them in Java like `Headers.Companion.Empty`

problems.title: JPMS support
problems.issue_body: The JPMS has been part of the java platform since Java 9, and will be in an LTS release when Java 11 is released in September. Very slowly, projects are adopting the module system, but to do so the libraries that they depend on need to also support modules. It would be really helpful if okhttp had a `module-info.class` added somehow so we can make proper use of okhttp in a modularised build.

problems.title: HttpLoggingInterceptor logging at level.BODY does not allow streaming requests
Multiple regressions in the handling of forms & form validation
problems.issue_body:
### Issue Content

We have found at least 2 regression in the way forms are handled while trying to migrate to v0.115.11 from version v0.112.4. After investigation we were able to identify 2 commits that introduce 2 separate bugs that break the handling of default values in both `x-form-urlencoded` and `mutlipart` froms. Partial fixes have been proposed that also address a CVE, but the PR has been hanging since october 24.

# Breaking in the handling of default value in `x-url-encoded` forms

This bug has been introduced in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12134.

MRE:
```python
app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/")
def root(
    name: Annotated[Optional[str], Form(embed=True)] = None,
):
    print(name)
    return name
```

with the following request:
```
❯ http post -v --form localhost:8888 name=""
POST / HTTP/1.1
Accept: */*
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br, zstd
Connection: keep-alive
Content-Length: 5
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Host: localhost:8888
User-Agent: xh/0.24.0

name=

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Length: 2
Content-Type: application/json
Date: Sun, 23 Mar 2025 21:01:49 GMT
Server: uvicorn

""
```
The expected result is that `None` is printed, but instead `""` is printed.

this is the offending bit from the linked PR:
```
diff --git a/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py b/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py
index 6083b731..db7eedba 100644
--- a/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py
+++ b/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py
@@ -789,9 +789,9 @@ async def _extract_form_body(
             value = serialize_sequence_value(field=field, value=results)
         if value is not None:
             values[field.name] = value
-    for key, value in received_body.items():
-        if key not in values:
-            values[key] = value
+    # for key, value in received_body.items():
+    #     if key not in values:
+    #         values[key] = value
     return values
```
This patch solves the x-form-urlencoded  case. So we indeed have two different regressions.
This _get_multi_dict_value  gets called for all fields in the schema: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/4633b1bca933e68dac5c3bcce797ff5963debe2a/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L765-L765
we see here the special case for empty fields: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/4633b1bca933e68dac5c3bcce797ff5963debe2a/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L695-L700
This bit here just blindly re-adds all fields from the body:    https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/4633b1bca933e68dac5c3bcce797ff5963debe2a/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L794-L796

This would be partially fixed by https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12502/files, but also requires that ignored fields are not re-added to the form.


# Breaking in the handling of default value in `multipart` forms

The breaking of multipart form originates from https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12117 where the check for empty field was dropped https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12117/files#diff-aef3dac481b68359f4edd6974fa3a047cfde595254a4567…

problems.title: Using pydantic Json Type as Form data type doesn't work
problems.issue_body:

However, while `working` returns the parsed json_list as expected, `broken` fails with ```json {"detail":[{"loc":["body","json_list"],"msg":"JSON object must be str, bytes or bytearray","type":"type_error.json"}]} ``` ### Operating System Linux ### Operating System Details _No response_ ### FastAPI Version 0.95.0 ### Python Version Python 3.10.8 ### Additional Context sample request that fails with `broken` and works with `working`: ```python import requests import json headers = {"Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded"} response = requests.post( "http://0.0.0.0:8000/broken", data={"json_list": json.dumps(["abc", "def"])}, headers=headers, ) ```

problems.title: FastAPI does not respect __wrapped__ attribute, causing NameError for forward references in decorated functions.
problems.issue_body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
# This needs to be in two files because the nature of the bug involves different globalns
# entries for a decorated function versus the module of the original function.
# If you put the code here into two files (func.py and main.py) in a directory, then the
# bug can be reproduced by running:
# PYTHONPATH=.:$PYTHONPATH python ./main.py
# This should cause an exception `NameError: name 'MyModel' is not defined`

# func.py
from pydantic import BaseModel


def foo(obj: "MyModel") -> "MyModel":
    return obj


class MyModel(BaseModel):
    x: int

# main.py
import functools, typing

from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
from func import foo

app = FastAPI()


def deco(func):
    @functools.wraps(func)
    def passthrough(*args, **kwargs):
        return func(*args, **kwargs)
    return passthrough

decorated = deco(foo)

assert issubclass(typing.get_type_hints(decorated)["obj"], BaseModel)
assert decorated.__annotations__ == foo.__annotations__
assert isinstance(decorated.__annotations__["obj"], str)

method = app.post('/endpoint')(decorated)
```


### Description

Using a decorator employing `functools.wraps` or a similar causes a `NameError` in `fastapi/dependencies/utils.py` in `get_typed_annotation`. The implementation there differs from the implementation in `typing`, which dereferences the `__wrapped__` attribute to find the namespace the original function was defined in. This allows forward references to be handled correctly:
https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/576dd901170af30fc50b0a7f07a388b38fd724a9/Lib/typing.py#L2314-L2315

In the "Example Code" section, my example includes assertions showing that `typing.get_type_hints` handles this situation correctly. I believe this can be fixed either by using the same implementation as typing uses, or by calling `typing.get_type_hints`, and only doing special FastAPI-specific logic if that fails.

### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

I first saw this on Ubuntu Linux with python 3.8, then later reproduced it on my Macbook in python 3.9. The example code was tested on the Mac.

I do not …

problems.title: Using PEP 649 annotations with status code `204 No Content` and return type `None` raises `AssertionError`
problems.issue_body:
### Privileged issue

- [X] I'm @tiangolo or he asked me directly to create an issue here.

### Issue Content

# Reproduce

1. create FastAPI app
2. create route with status code `204 No Content`
3. add return type `None` to said route
4. add future annotations ([PEP 649](https://peps.python.org/pep-0649/))

```py
from __future__ import annotations

import http
import logging

from fastapi import FastAPI


app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/", status_code=http.HTTPStatus.NO_CONTENT)
async def root() -> None:
    logging.info("endpoint called.")
```

The file above should boot up fine when running `uvicorn filename:app --reload`
