problems.title: Fix incorrect return type annotation for Locale.format_day
problems.issue_body:
`Locale.format_day` is annotated as returning `bool`, but it actually returns a formatted string (e.g. `"Monday, January 22"`).

This incorrect annotation causes type checkers to flag valid usage as errors and can mislead IDE autocompletion.

problems.title: feature: add swipe tool
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #75

## Problem

TouchPilot has `scroll`, but it does not expose a general swipe primitive for horizontal pages, drawers, carousels, custom surfaces, and gesture-only controls.

## Proposed behavior

Add a `swipe` Android tool.

Supported modes should include:

- direction-based swipe: `left`, `right`, `up`, `down`,
- optional coordinate swipe: `start`, `end`, `duration_ms`,
- optional target/container selector after the resolver exists.

## User workflow

A user can ask TouchPilot to swipe left/right/up/down on a screen or specific area.

## Local-first impact

Swipe is a core Android control primitive and improves local automation on gesture-heavy screens.

## Safety and security considerations

Coordinate gestures are easy to misuse. Keep duration/distance bounded, validate coordinates, and log only safe gesture metadata.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add `swipe` to the Android tool catalog.
- [ ] Support direction-based swipe first.
- [ ] Validate optional coordinate inputs if included.
- [ ] Return useful failures for invalid direction or out-of-range coordinates.
- [ ] Add tests for direction validation and execution request construction.
- [ ] Add manual emulator validation for horizontal and vertical swipe cases.

## Alternatives considered

Use `scroll` for all swipe-like behavior. Rejected because scroll and swipe have different semantics and verification needs.

problems.title: feature: add get_foreground_app tool
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #75

## Problem

Tool verification needs to know which app/window is currently foreground, but TouchPilot does not expose a dedicated foreground-app inspection tool.

This makes `open_app`, `press_back`, `press_home`, and future multi-step loops harder to verify.

## Proposed behavior

Add a `get_foreground_app` Android tool that returns current foreground package/window information where Android allows it.

The result should include, when available:

- package name,
- app label,
- window title,
- activity/class if accessible,
- whether TouchPilot AccessibilityService is connected.

## User workflow

A user or local agent can ask TouchPilot where it is before or after an action, and verification can use the result to confirm transitions.

## Local-first impact

Foreground state is local device context and is necessary for reliable local control.

## Safety and security considerations

Foreground app metadata can reveal private app usage. Keep logs concise, avoid sensitive text, and do not require broad new permissions unless clearly justified.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add `get_foreground_app` to the Android tool catalog.
- [ ] Return package/window/app label where practical.
- [ ] Do not add unnecessary permissions.
- [ ] Use result in or prepare for post-action verification.
- [ ] Add unit tests for result shape and fallback behavior.
- [ ] Add live checklist coverage.

## Alternatives considered

Infer foreground app from raw screen dumps only. Rejected because verification needs a stable structured signal.

problems.title: feature: add clear_text tool
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #75

## Problem

TouchPilot can type text, but it does not have a reliable primitive for clearing or replacing existing field content.

This blocks common workflows such as editing search terms, replacing settings values, or retrying failed form entry.

## Proposed behavior

Add a `clear_text` Android tool that clears the focused or resolved input field.

Expected behavior:

- use a resolved input target when selector args are provided,
- otherwise operate on the currently focused editable field,
- return a clear failure when no editable target is available,
- avoid logging existing field contents.

## User workflow

A user can ask TouchPilot to clear a field before typing a new value.

## Local-first impact

Reliable local form control needs clear/replace behavior before Milestone 5 multi-step tasks.

## Safety and security considerations

Clearing text can delete user-entered data. The tool should use the normal policy/risk path and must not log previous field contents.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add `clear_text` to the Android tool catalog.
- [ ] Support focused-field and resolved-target modes.
- [ ] Return actionable errors for no editable target or ambiguous target.
- [ ] Do not log raw field contents.
- [ ] Add unit tests and live-test checklist coverage.

## Alternatives considered

Use repeated delete key events through a generic key tool. Rejected for now because a focused clear primitive is easier to verify and constrain.

problems.title: feature: add find_element tool
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

Current tool calls need reliable target resolution, but agents often need to inspect whether a specific element exists before choosing an action. Reusing raw observe output for this makes routing brittle and pushes too much parsing into the reasoning layer.

## Proposed behavior

Add a `find_element` Android tool that searches the current screen context and returns structured candidate nodes.

Expected arguments:

- `text`: optional visible text query.
- `content_description`: optional accessibility description query.
- `node_id`: optional stable node identifier.
- `class_name`: optional Android class filter.
- `match`: optional match mode such as `exact`, `contains`, or `semantic` when supported.
- `limit`: optional maximum candidates.

## User workflow

The agent can call `find_element({text=Settings})`, inspect the returned candidates, then call a target-aware action such as `tap` or future bounds-based action with the selected target.

## Local-first impact

This gives the local router and small model a structured lookup primitive instead of forcing them to parse full UI trees.

## Safety and security considerations

Results must preserve existing sensitive-text redaction and sensitivity flags. The tool should not return hidden/private fields beyond the existing accessibility observation boundary.

## Acceptance criteria

- Tool is registered in the Android tool catalog.
- Returns structured candidates with stable id, display-safe text, content description, class, bounds, enabled/clickable flags, and sensitivity marker when available.
- Supports bounded candidate limits.
- Includes tests for matching modes and sensitive-text redaction.
- Live-test checklist covers exact text, partial text, content description, and no-match cases.

problems.title: feature: add wait_for_idle tool
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

Many Android actions trigger short UI transitions, loading states, keyboard changes, or accessibility-tree churn. Current follow-up actions can run too early unless each caller hand-rolls delays or text waits.

## Proposed behavior

Add a `wait_for_idle` Android tool that waits until the observed screen context remains stable for a small window, or until a bounded timeout expires.

Expected arguments:

- `stable_ms`: optional duration the screen must remain stable.
- `timeout_ms`: optional maximum wait.
- `include_bounds`: optional flag if bounds changes should count as instability.

## User workflow

After `tap`, `swipe`, `open_app`, or `dismiss_keyboard`, the agent can call `wait_for_idle` before selecting the next target.

## Local-first impact

This improves reliability through deterministic synchronization rather than extra model reasoning.

## Safety and security considerations

The tool should be low-risk observation only. It should not expose raw sensitive text beyond the existing redacted screen-context path, and it should use strict timeout bounds to avoid stuck agent loops.

## Acceptance criteria

- Tool is registered in the Android tool catalog.
- Uses screen-context snapshots or accessibility-tree signatures to detect stability.
- Has bounded defaults and actionable timeout messages.
- Includes unit tests for idle/still-changing/timeout behavior where practical.
- Live-test checklist covers waiting after launch, tap, scroll/swipe, and keyboard dismissal.

problems.title: feature: add next-step context and clarification path
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

A multi-step local agent must use what happened in the previous step. It also needs a safe path when the next action is uncertain or multiple targets match. Without that, the loop will either guess or stop with vague errors.

## Proposed behavior

Add a next-step context object that carries recent screen summary, selected tool result, verification result, failure reason, and candidate targets into the next local decision. Add a clarification decision path when the local router cannot safely choose one action.

Clarification cases should include:

- multiple matching targets
- missing required target
- ambiguous user request
- low confidence local decision
- action would require user choice before continuing

## User workflow

When TouchPilot is unsure, it asks a direct question such as “Which Settings item should I open?” instead of executing a risky or random action.

## Local-first impact

This improves small local model reliability by giving it structured context and a non-execution fallback.

## Safety and security considerations

Clarification must not expose raw sensitive screen text. If policy blocks the action, the loop should stop or ask for approval only through the existing approval path, not through generic clarification.

## Acceptance criteria

- Adds a reusable next-step context structure.
- Carries tool result and verification result into the next decision.
- Adds clarification decision type and stop/continue behavior.
- Includes tests for ambiguous target and no-valid-action cases.
- Keeps blocked-policy behavior distinct from clarification behavior.

problems.title: feature: show tool call cards in chat
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

Tool calls are central to TouchPilot, but chat users need a readable view of what tool was selected, why it ran, whether approval was needed, and what happened. Raw logs are too dense for normal use.

## Proposed behavior

Add compact tool call cards inside the chat step timeline. Cards should show tool name, redacted arguments, approval/policy status, result status, short message, and verification status when available.

## User workflow

During a task, the user can quickly scan each action TouchPilot took and see whether it succeeded.

## Local-first impact

Visible local tool calls make the local agent loop auditable without cloud traces.

## Safety and security considerations

All arguments and messages must use the existing redactor. High-risk or blocked policy outcomes should be clearly marked.

## Acceptance criteria

- Tool calls render as compact cards in chat.
- Cards include tool name, redacted args, result, and verification state.
- Approval-required and blocked states are easy to distinguish.
- Long args/messages wrap cleanly on mobile.
- No raw sensitive values are displayed.

problems.title: feature: add agent step model
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

Milestone 5 needs a shared representation for each local agent step before the loop and UI can evolve cleanly. Today, agent events and tool results exist, but the observe-decide-act loop does not have a compact step model that can be reused by the runner, tests, chat timeline, and run detail UI.

## Proposed behavior

Add a structured agent step model for local runs. The model should represent step type, sequence number, status, input summary, output summary, tool call details when present, verification result when present, timestamps, and stop/clarification metadata.

Expected step types include:

- `observe`
- `decide`
- `act`
- `verify`
- `clarify`
- `stop`

## User workflow

A user starts a local task and every internal step can later be rendered in chat or inspected in a run detail view.

## Local-first impact

This makes the local agent loop observable without relying on cloud traces or external debugging tools.

## Safety and security considerations

Step fields must use existing redaction paths for tool args, screen text, and tool output. Sensitive data should not be persisted or rendered raw.

## Acceptance criteria

- Adds a reusable step data model for local agent runs.
- Covers observe, decide, act, verify, clarify, and stop steps.
- Includes status and stop reason fields.
- Keeps sensitive text redacted in display-facing fields.
- Includes focused unit tests for model construction/serialization where applicable.

problems.title: feature: add bounded local agent loop
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

TouchPilot needs to move from one-shot tool routing to short multi-step local tasks, but the loop must be bounded so it cannot run indefinitely or keep acting after repeated failures.

## Proposed behavior

Add a local observe-decide-act loop with strict run limits. The loop should observe the screen, choose the next local action, execute through the existing policy/tool path, verify the result, and either continue or stop.

Initial stop conditions should include:

- success verified
- max steps reached
- repeated tool failure
- policy blocked
- clarification needed
- no valid next action
- user cancelled

## User workflow

A user asks for a small local task such as opening an app or navigating to a settings subscreen. TouchPilot runs a few safe steps and stops with a clear reason.

## Local-first impact

The loop should use deterministic local routing and the existing local reasoning path. It must not require cloud/API provider configuration.

## Safety and security considerations

Every action must still pass through `DefaultActionPolicy` and existing approval behavior. The loop must stop on blocked or uncertain actions instead of bypassing policy.

## Acceptance criteria

- Adds a bounded local agent loop entry point.
- Supports configurable max steps with safe default.
- Emits structured step records.
- Stops with clear machine-readable and user-facing reasons.
- Includes unit tests for success, max-step, blocked, and failure stop paths.

problems.title: feature: add dismiss_keyboard tool
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

The soft keyboard can cover controls and change the accessibility tree after typing. Agents currently have to guess whether pressing Back is meant to dismiss the keyboard or navigate away, which makes edit flows less reliable.

## Proposed behavior

Add a `dismiss_keyboard` Android tool that hides the soft keyboard when it is visible and reports whether the screen changed.

Expected arguments:

- `timeout_ms`: optional bounded wait for the keyboard to disappear.

## User workflow

After `focus_input` and `type_text`, the agent can call `dismiss_keyboard`, then `wait_for_idle`, then tap the next visible control without relying on a generic Back action.

## Local-first impact

This gives local command routing a precise editing primitive and reduces ambiguous use of `press_back`.

## Safety and security considerations

The tool should only hide input UI. It should not submit text, clear text, or navigate app state beyond keyboard dismissal. If implemented through Back fallback, the result should verify that the keyboard disappeared rather than silently navigating away.

## Acceptance criteria

- Tool is registered in the Android tool catalog.
- Detects keyboard visibility when possible.
- Hides the keyboard without submitting text.
- Returns clear success, already-hidden, and failure outcomes.
- Includes unit tests for result formatting/argument validation and live-test checklist coverage on focused input fields.

problems.title: feature: add agent run detail screen
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

The chat timeline should stay compact, but maintainers and advanced users need a deeper view of a local agent run for debugging and live validation.

## Proposed behavior

Add an agent run detail screen that can be opened from a completed run or logs. It should show the full ordered step list, tool calls, redacted arguments, verification details, stop reason, and trace/export affordance if already supported.

## User workflow

A user or maintainer taps a run summary and inspects the full run details without digging through raw log text.

## Local-first impact

This supports on-device debugging and trust in the local observe-decide-act loop.

## Safety and security considerations

The detail screen must use display-safe/redacted fields and should not expose raw accessibility snapshots unless the user explicitly exports a redacted debug trace.

## Acceptance criteria

- Run detail screen is reachable from chat run summary or logs.
- Shows ordered steps with statuses and timestamps.
- Shows redacted tool args/results and verification details.
- Provides a clear empty/error state if run data is unavailable.
- Does not display raw sensitive screen text.

problems.title: feature: add running agent state controls
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

Users need clear control while a local multi-step task is running. Without visible run state and cancellation, the app can feel stuck or unsafe during longer tasks.

## Proposed behavior

Add running-state UI for local agent runs. The chat screen should show whether the run is idle, running, waiting for approval, waiting for clarification, completed, failed, blocked, or cancelled. Add a cancel/stop control for active runs.

## User workflow

The user starts a task, sees that TouchPilot is running, and can stop it before more actions execute.

## Local-first impact

This keeps local automation user-directed and inspectable.

## Safety and security considerations

Cancel must stop future actions cleanly. It should not interrupt an already-dispatched Android action in a way that leaves internal state inconsistent.

## Acceptance criteria

- Chat screen shows clear run state.
- Active runs have a stop/cancel action.
- Completed, failed, blocked, and cancelled states are visually distinct.
- Cancelled runs emit a stop step/reason.
- Includes UI-level tests or focused state tests where practical.

problems.title: feature: show agent step timeline in chat
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

When a local task runs multiple steps, users need to see what TouchPilot is doing. A hidden loop makes the app feel unpredictable and makes debugging difficult.

## Proposed behavior

Render local agent steps as a compact timeline inside the chat-oriented agent screen. The timeline should show observe, decide, act, verify, clarify, and stop steps as they happen.

## User workflow

The user sends a task, then sees TouchPilot progress through each step without leaving chat.

## Local-first impact

A visible local timeline makes local reasoning inspectable and reinforces that the task is running on-device.

## Safety and security considerations

Timeline text must use redacted display fields. Sensitive args, screen text, or trace data must not be rendered raw.

## Acceptance criteria

- Chat screen renders agent step timeline items.
- Step status is visible: pending, running, success, failed, blocked, clarification needed.
- Timeline updates incrementally during a run.
- Empty and completed states look polished on mobile.
- Uses existing app Material styling conventions.

problems.title: feature: show clarification prompt in chat
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

When the local agent is uncertain, the user should see a clear question and respond in the same chat flow. A plain failure message is not enough for multi-step tasks.

## Proposed behavior

Render clarification requests as assistant chat messages. Where the backend provides options, show them as tappable choices; otherwise allow a normal text reply.

## User workflow

TouchPilot asks “Which item should I tap?” and the user can choose an option or type an answer. The run can then continue with the clarified context.

## Local-first impact

Clarification lets a small local model avoid unsafe guessing while still completing useful tasks.

## Safety and security considerations

Clarification options must be redacted and display-safe. User answers should go through the same policy path before actions resume.

## Acceptance criteria

- Chat renders clarification prompt messages.
- Optional answer choices are shown as tappable controls.
- Text reply path works when choices are absent.
- Clarification response is connected back to the active run state.
- Sensitive option text is redacted.

problems.title: feature: add wait_for_app tool
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

TouchPilot can open an app, but follow-up actions still need a reliable way to wait until the expected app is actually foreground. Without this, agent loops and tool chains can race Android launch transitions and act on the previous screen.

## Proposed behavior

Add a `wait_for_app` Android tool that waits until a target package name or launcher label becomes the foreground app, then returns a structured success/failure result.

Expected arguments:

- `package`: optional Android package name.
- `label`: optional launcher/app label.
- `timeout_ms`: optional bounded timeout with a safe default.

At least one of `package` or `label` should be required.

## User workflow

A local agent can call `open_app({target=Calculator})`, then `wait_for_app({label=Calculator})`, then observe or interact only after the app switch is confirmed.

## Local-first impact

This keeps app-switch synchronization local and deterministic, reducing the need for a larger model to guess whether Android has finished transitioning.

## Safety and security considerations

The tool should be observation/synchronization only. It must not launch apps itself, grant permissions, or bypass policy. Returned app identifiers should go through the normal logging/redaction path where applicable.

## Acceptance criteria

- Tool is registered in the Android tool catalog.
- Supports package-name and label matching.
- Uses bounded timeout and clear failure messages.
- Includes unit tests for argument validation and success/failure result formatting.
- Live-test checklist is updated for open-app plus wait-for-app flow.

problems.title: feature: add task completion summary card
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

At the end of a local task, users need a concise summary of whether TouchPilot completed the task, stopped safely, failed, or needs more input. Without a final summary, the step timeline can be hard to interpret.

## Proposed behavior

Add a completion summary card at the end of each local agent run. It should show final status, stop reason, step count, last verified state, and a short next action if applicable.

## User workflow

After a task finishes, the user sees “Completed”, “Stopped”, “Blocked”, or “Needs clarification” with enough context to understand what happened.

## Local-first impact

This makes local execution outcomes transparent without requiring users to inspect debug logs.

## Safety and security considerations

Failure and blocked summaries should avoid exposing raw tool args or screen text. Policy-blocked runs should explain the safety reason clearly.

## Acceptance criteria

- Summary card renders after every run stop.
- Shows final status, stop reason, step count, and last verification outcome.
- Distinguishes completed, failed, blocked, cancelled, and clarification-needed states.
- Uses redacted display fields only.
- Looks correct on narrow mobile screens.

problems.title: feature: add open_settings_panel tool
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #75

## Problem

Opening Android Settings by label works, but navigating to specific settings panels by tapping is brittle.

Many common workflows need direct access to Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Accessibility, app details, notification settings, or system settings screens.

## Proposed behavior

Add an `open_settings_panel` Android tool using Android Settings intents where available.

Supported panels can start small, for example:

- `wifi`
- `bluetooth`
- `accessibility`
- `app_info`
- `notifications`
- `system_settings`

The tool should return unsupported-panel errors instead of guessing.

## User workflow

A user can ask TouchPilot to open a specific Android settings panel directly, reducing brittle tap chains.

## Local-first impact

Direct local intents make common Android workflows more reliable without external services.

## Safety and security considerations

Settings panels can affect sensitive device state. Opening a panel is safer than toggling settings, but it should still be logged and may remain medium risk.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add `open_settings_panel` to the Android tool catalog.
- [ ] Support an initial allowlist of safe panel names.
- [ ] Use Android Settings intents where available.
- [ ] Return clear failure for unsupported panel names.
- [ ] Do not toggle settings automatically.
- [ ] Add unit tests for panel mapping and live checklist coverage.

## Alternatives considered

Use `open_app` plus `tap` chains. Rejected because common settings navigation should be deterministic and less brittle.

problems.title: feature: add observe_screen_context tool
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #75

## Problem

TouchPilot exposes `observe_screen` as a raw Accessibility tree dump, but Milestone 3 added structured `ScreenContext` for local screen understanding.

Agents and debug tooling need a structured observation tool that returns the normalized context directly.

## Proposed behavior

Add an `observe_screen_context` Android tool that returns a redacted serialized `ScreenContext`.

The output should include:

- app/package/window metadata,
- visible nodes,
- roles,
- bounds,
- click/input/scroll flags,
- sensitive text redacted or marked safely.

## User workflow

The local agent can inspect structured screen context before choosing reliable tool actions.

## Local-first impact

Structured local context is the bridge between Milestone 3 understanding and Milestone 4 reliable control.

## Safety and security considerations

`ScreenContext` can contain sensitive visible text. Output must use display-safe/redacted text by default and avoid leaking raw sensitive values in logs or traces.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add `observe_screen_context` to the Android tool catalog.
- [ ] Serialize `ScreenContext` in a stable JSON shape.
- [ ] Redact sensitive text by default.
- [ ] Keep `observe_screen` available for raw debug compatibility.
- [ ] Add tests for serialization, redaction, and empty context.
- [ ] Document when agents should prefer structured context over raw dumps.

## Alternatives considered

Only keep `observe_screen`. Rejected because raw text dumps are harder to rank, verify, and evolve safely.

problems.title: feature: add long_press tool
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #75

## Problem

Some Android workflows require long-press, such as opening launcher context menus, selecting text/items, or triggering app-specific overflow actions.

TouchPilot currently only supports normal tap.

## Proposed behavior

Add a `long_press` Android tool using the Milestone 4 target selector/resolver path.

Expected arguments should mirror `tap` where practical:

- `text`
- `node_id`
- `bounds`
- `view_id`

The tool should resolve one target and dispatch a long-press gesture on it.

## User workflow

A user can ask TouchPilot to long-press a visible item, such as an app icon or list item.

## Local-first impact

Long-press expands deterministic Android control without adding cloud dependencies.

## Safety and security considerations

Long-press can open destructive context menus or selection states. Ambiguous targets must fail safely, and the action should remain medium risk unless policy decides otherwise.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add `long_press` to the Android tool catalog.
- [ ] Resolve targets through the selector/resolver path.
- [ ] Dispatch a bounded long-press gesture.
- [ ] Return clear failure for missing or ambiguous targets.
- [ ] Add unit tests and manual emulator checklist coverage.

## Alternatives considered

Overload `tap` with a duration argument. Rejected for now because a separate tool keeps risk, logging, and agent command parsing clearer.

problems.title: Feature: Handle simple chat greetings before tool routing
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

When a user enters a simple greeting such as:

```text
Hello
```

TouchPilot currently treats it like an automation task and sends it into the command-routing flow.

For a chat-first UX, TouchPilot should answer simple conversational messages directly instead of observing the screen, selecting a tool, or producing an action timeline.

## Proposed behavior

If the user inputs:

```text
Hello
```

TouchPilot should respond:

```text
Hello, I am TouchPilot, how can I help you?
```

This should be returned as a final conversational response, not as an Android tool action.

## User workflow

1. User opens the Chat tab.
2. User types `Hello`.
3. TouchPilot appends the user message.
4. TouchPilot appends the assistant response:
   `Hello, I am TouchPilot, how can I help you?`
5. TouchPilot does not run `observe_screen`.
6. TouchPilot does not execute any Android tool.
7. TouchPilot does not show an action timeline for this greeting-only path.

For real automation requests, the existing provider, parser, skill, safety, approval, and tool execution flow should continue to work.

## Local-first impact

This should work without cloud fallback.

The local runtime should support a lightweight conversational `final` response path before tool routing. The behavior should be driven by TouchPilot agent/prompt policy, similar to the OpenClaw/PicoClaw pattern:

- identity/personality instructions
- agent behavior instructions
- tool capability instructions
- runtime/user-turn context
- provider/runtime execution

A future implementation may use an asset such as:

```text
app/src/main/assets/prompts/touchpilot_agent.md
```

or a dedicated Kotlin prompt module if that better fits the current Android app structure.

## Safety and security considerations

Greeting-only messages must not trigger Android control.

Specifically:

- Do not call `observe_screen`.
- Do not execute tools.
- Do not request approvals.
- Do not log screen state for this path.
- Keep automation tasks behind the existing parser, skill allowlist, safety policy, approval flow, and tool execution logs.

## Alternatives considered

One simple option is to hardcode `Hello` handling directly in `MainActivity`, but that would scatter agent behavior into UI code.

A better approach is to keep this behavior near the agent/provider boundary so TouchPilot can grow toward a maintainable prompt-driven local assistant model.

problems.title: Feature: Add real on-device command routing model
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

TouchPilot has a local router and an agent command-provider boundary, but it does not yet run a real on-device model. The project direction is local-first Android AI, so TouchPilot needs a real local inference path for command routing before expanding toward larger local LLMs.

The first local model runtime should be small, practical, and focused on structured command routing rather than general chat.

## Proposed Behavior

Add a real on-device model runtime for local command routing.

The runtime should take a user task plus relevant context and return a structured command compatible with the existing JSON command loop.

Example output:

```json
{
  "tool": "open_app",
  "args": {
    "target": "Settings"
  }
}
```

or:

```json
{
  "final": "I cannot do that safely."
}
```

## Scope

- Start with LiteRT for a small command-routing model.
- Define model asset loading and storage.
- Add local model status UI.
- Add runtime health/status checks.
- Add a local inference interface behind the existing command-provider boundary.
- Keep deterministic local router as fallback.
- Keep cloud provider optional and experimental.
- Ensure local model outputs go through:
  - JSON parsing
  - tool validation
  - skill allowlists
  - safety policy
  - approval flow
  - logs/traces
- Document future ExecuTorch and llama.cpp paths.
- Add tests or fixtures for local command output parsing.

## User Workflow

A user should be able to open TouchPilot and run simple tasks locally without configuring an API key.

Example tasks:

- open settings
- go back
- scroll down
- tap Wi-Fi
- observe the current screen

TouchPilot should show local model status and make it clear whether it is using:

- deterministic local router
- local model runtime
- cloud fallback

If the local model is unavailable, TouchPilot should fall back to the deterministic local router and explain that fallback in the UI.

## Local-First Impact

This is a core local-first milestone. It moves TouchPilot from deterministic local routing toward learned on-device command selection while preserving the existing safety/tool execution pipeline.

The goal is not full local chat yet. The goal is a small local command-routing model that can safely produce structured tool calls.

## Safety and Security Considerations

Local model output must be treated as untrusted.

Every local model command must pass through:

- schema parsing
- tool validation
- skill allowlists
- safety policy
- approval flow
- logging and trace redaction

The model must not directly execute Android actions. It only proposes commands.

The implementation should avoid:

- bypassing approval prompts
- bypassing skill allowlists
- logging sensitive screen text
- relying on model judgment for sensitive actions
- silently falling back to cloud without user visibility

## Alternatives Considered

- Keep deterministic local router only.
- Integrate ExecuTorch first.
- Integrate llama.cpp first.
- Use cloud provider until local LLM runti…

problems.title: Feature: Answer simple help requests before tool routing
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

TouchPilot can now answer simple greetings before tool routing, but users still need a lightweight way to discover what the local-first assistant can do.

If a user types `help`, TouchPilot should answer conversationally instead of treating it like an Android automation task.

## Proposed behavior

If the user inputs:

```text
help
```

TouchPilot should respond:

```text
I can help you control Android apps, open settings, tap visible text, scroll, go back or home, and use approved skills. What would you like to do?
```

Similar phrases should also work:

- `what can you do`
- `how can you help`

This should be returned as a final conversational response, not as an Android tool action.

## User workflow

1. User opens the Chat tab.
2. User types `help`.
3. TouchPilot appends the user message.
4. TouchPilot appends the assistant help response.
5. TouchPilot does not run `observe_screen`.
6. TouchPilot does not execute any Android tool.
7. TouchPilot does not show an action timeline for this help-only path.

Automation requests such as `open Settings`, `tap OK`, `scroll`, `go back`, and `go home` should continue through the existing provider, parser, skill, safety, approval, and tool execution flow.

## Local-first impact

This is a small Milestone 1 polish feature for the local-first chat experience.

The behavior should work offline and should not require cloud fallback. It can build on the existing `ConversationalGate` path introduced for greeting-only messages.

## Safety and security considerations

Help-only messages must not trigger Android control.

Specifically:

- Do not call `observe_screen`.
- Do not execute tools.
- Do not request approvals.
- Do not log screen state for this path.
- Keep real automation tasks behind the existing parser, skill allowlist, safety policy, approval flow, and tool execution logs.

## Acceptance criteria

- Typing `help` returns the expected help response.
- Typing `what can you do` returns the expected help response.
- Typing `how can you help` returns the expected help response.
- No tool call is made for help-only input.
- No action timeline is shown for help-only input.
- Existing automation commands still route normally.
- Unit tests cover positive help phrases and a mixed phrase such as `help me open Settings`, which should not be short-circuited.

problems.title: refactor: define local reasoning core boundary
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

Milestone 2 needs a clean local reasoning core boundary. The app should clearly separate UI state, local command production, tool routing, policy checks, and Android execution.

Without this boundary, local model work, deterministic routing, skills, and safety policy will become tightly coupled and hard to test.

## Proposed behavior

Define and implement the local reasoning core boundary:

```text
user request + context
  -> local reasoning core
  -> agent events and/or structured tool request
  -> tool router + policy
  -> Android tool execution
```

This should be an architecture cleanup with minimal behavior change.

## User workflow

Existing flows such as `Hello`, `Help`, `Open Settings`, and `Go back` should continue working, but internally they should pass through a clearer runtime boundary.

## Local-first impact

This keeps the core product independent from cloud providers and makes local command routing, local model reasoning, skills, and policy pluggable behind one local runtime boundary.

## Safety and security considerations

The reasoning core must not execute tools directly. Tool execution must continue to go through validation, skill allowlists, policy, approvals, and logs.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Define the local reasoning core interface or module boundary.
- [ ] UI does not directly own command production or Android tool execution.
- [ ] Local router output can pass through the reasoning core boundary.
- [ ] Tool execution remains behind tool router and policy checks.
- [ ] Existing core flows still work.
- [ ] Add focused unit tests for the boundary where practical.

problems.title: test: add offline validation checklist and tests
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

Milestone 2 needs offline validation to prove the hybrid local core works without cloud models or hosted routing.

The milestone should not be considered complete until representative local-only flows pass.

## Proposed behavior

Add an offline validation checklist and tests for Milestone 2 flows:

- `Hello`
- `Help`
- `Open Settings`
- `Go back`
- one ambiguous request routed through the local-model-needed path

Validation should confirm intent gate behavior, event emission, deterministic fallback, and policy/tool routing where applicable.

## User workflow

A developer or tester should be able to run the app offline and validate the key Milestone 2 flows without configuring a cloud provider, API key, or backend service.

## Local-first impact

This proves the product direction: TouchPilot's core agent behavior works locally.

## Safety and security considerations

Offline validation should include at least one blocked or approval-related event path if practical, to confirm local policy remains active.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add a Milestone 2 offline validation checklist.
- [ ] Validate `Hello` locally.
- [ ] Validate `Help` locally.
- [ ] Validate `Open Settings` locally.
- [ ] Validate `Go back` locally.
- [ ] Validate one ambiguous local-model-needed request.
- [ ] Confirm each flow emits expected agent events.
- [ ] Confirm no cloud provider/API key/backend is required.
- [ ] Add automated tests where practical and document any manual live-test steps.

problems.title: feature: add intent gate for local routing
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

Milestone 2 needs an intent gate so TouchPilot does not ask the local model to solve every request. Small local models are limited, so exact commands, known skills, unsafe requests, and clarification cases should be routed deterministically first.

## Proposed behavior

Add an intent gate that classifies incoming user requests into:

- exact deterministic command
- known skill request
- unsafe or blocked request
- local-model-needed request
- clarification needed

The intent gate should run before local model reasoning.

## User workflow

Examples:

- `Hello` -> deterministic final answer
- `Help` -> deterministic/help skill response
- `Go back` -> exact command
- `Open Settings` -> exact command or known skill
- `Send this payment` -> unsafe or approval-required path
- `Do the thing I usually do here` -> clarification or local-model-needed path

## Local-first impact

The intent gate makes the local AI architecture practical. It uses deterministic logic where correctness matters and reserves local model reasoning for ambiguity.

## Safety and security considerations

Unsafe requests should be detected before tool execution. The intent gate should never approve risky actions by itself; it should only route to policy, clarification, or blocked outcomes.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add an intent gate component.
- [ ] Route exact commands without invoking a local model.
- [ ] Route known skill candidates separately from exact commands.
- [ ] Route unsafe or unclear requests to policy/clarification paths.
- [ ] Route ambiguous requests to local-model-needed path.
- [ ] Unit tests cover representative routing decisions.

problems.title: feature: define OCR fallback boundary for weak screens
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #38

## Problem

Some Android screens expose weak or incomplete Accessibility data. TouchPilot needs a planned fallback boundary for OCR, without making OCR or local VLM required for Milestone 3 completion.

The project should know when Accessibility context is weak and how future OCR/VLM fallback will plug in.

## Proposed behavior

Add weak-tree detection and define the OCR fallback boundary:

```text
Accessibility observation
  -> screen context builder
  -> context quality check
  -> strong context: summarize/suggest directly
  -> weak context: mark OCR fallback needed
```

Milestone 3 can implement the interface/detection first. OCR execution can be added when the local OCR runtime is selected.

## User workflow

When TouchPilot cannot understand enough from Accessibility data, it should respond honestly:

```text
I can see this screen, but the app exposes limited readable UI data. OCR support is needed for better understanding here.
```

## Local-first impact

This keeps the primary path local and deterministic while preparing for future local OCR/VLM support.

## Safety and security considerations

- OCR output should be treated as untrusted and noisy.
- OCR text may include sensitive content and must go through redaction before logging.
- Weak context should not cause unsafe guessing or blind tapping.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add context quality or weak-tree detection logic.
- [ ] Define an OCR fallback interface/boundary or placeholder result type.
- [ ] Mark local VLM as future fallback, not a Milestone 3 dependency.
- [ ] Weak context produces conservative summary/suggestion behavior.
- [ ] Tests cover strong context, weak context, and empty context.
- [ ] Documentation or comments explain that OCR/VLM fallback remains local-only.

## References

- Parent issue: #38
- `docs/LOCAL_INFERENCE.md`
- `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`

problems.title: feature: define screen context model
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #38

## Problem

Milestone 3 needs a stable screen context model before TouchPilot can summarize screens, suggest actions, or feed useful local context into the reasoning core and future local models.

Current Android observation data is useful for debugging and tool execution, but it needs a normalized product-facing shape that is safe, testable, and independent from raw Accessibility implementation details.

## Proposed behavior

Define a structured screen context model that represents the current Android screen with enough information for local reasoning:

- current app label and package name when available
- screen/window title when available
- visible text
- clickable nodes
- input fields
- scrollable containers or scroll state
- node bounds
- enabled/focused/checked/clickable state
- stable node identifiers when available
- redaction-aware text fields

The model should be usable by:

- deterministic screen summaries
- suggested actions
- local model input contracts
- trace/log export
- future OCR/VLM fallback layers

## User workflow

When TouchPilot observes a screen, the runtime should be able to convert raw screen data into a stable context object before any summary or action suggestion is generated.

Example flow:

```text
Accessibility tree
  -> ScreenContext
  -> summary / suggested actions / local model input
```

## Local-first impact

A stable screen context model keeps screen understanding local and reusable. It lets TouchPilot improve local models later without changing the Android control layer or UI.

## Safety and security considerations

- Screen context may include sensitive text.
- Sensitive text fields should be marked or redacted before logs/traces.
- Raw Accessibility data should not be exposed directly to user-visible summaries without filtering.
- The model should support policy decisions later, such as sensitive app or sensitive field detection.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add a stable screen context data model.
- [ ] Model includes app/package, visible text, clickable nodes, input fields, bounds, and state flags where available.
- [ ] Model distinguishes raw text from redaction-safe display/log text where practical.
- [ ] Model is independent from UI components.
- [ ] Model can be used as input to local reasoning/model contracts.
- [ ] Unit tests cover construction of representative screen context objects.
- [ ] Documentation or code comments explain the role of the model boundary.

## References

- Parent issue: #38
- `docs/ROADMAP.md` Milestone 3
- `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`
- `docs/TOOL_SPEC.md`

problems.title: feature: build screen context from accessibility tree
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #38

## Problem

After defining the screen context model, TouchPilot needs a reliable builder that converts Android Accessibility observations into that model.

Raw Accessibility trees are noisy and inconsistent. The builder should normalize data into a smaller, useful context for local reasoning and suggested actions.

## Proposed behavior

Implement an Accessibility-to-screen-context builder:

```text
Accessibility observation
  -> normalize nodes
  -> extract visible text
  -> extract clickable targets
  -> extract input fields
  -> extract bounds/state
  -> produce ScreenContext
```

The builder should keep raw observation details available for debugging, but the reasoning core should use the normalized context.

## User workflow

When the user asks `What can you do here?`, TouchPilot should first build screen context from the active Android screen and then generate a local summary or suggestions from that context.

## Local-first impact

This is the primary screen-understanding path. It avoids requiring screenshot vision models for normal Android screens.

## Safety and security considerations

- Builder output should remain compatible with log redaction.
- Sensitive fields such as passwords, tokens, messages, and account data should be marked or redacted where possible.
- The builder should not create executable actions directly; it should only produce context.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Build screen context from current Accessibility observation data.
- [ ] Extract visible text nodes.
- [ ] Extract clickable targets with bounds and stable identifiers when available.
- [ ] Extract input fields and basic state.
- [ ] Extract scrollable state where available.
- [ ] Keep raw observation and normalized context separate.
- [ ] Add tests with fake/sample Accessibility data.
- [ ] Existing observe/tool behavior remains compatible.

## References

- Parent issue: #38
- Depends on: screen context model issue
- `docs/TOOL_SPEC.md`
- `docs/LIVE_TESTING.md`

problems.title: feature: hybrid local AI core (Milestone 2)
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

Milestone 1 proved the local-first foundation, but TouchPilot still behaves mostly like a deterministic command router with a local model runtime path. Milestone 2 should turn the foundation into a real 100% local hybrid AI core.

The goal is not to make one small local model do everything. The goal is to combine deterministic routing, local model reasoning, skills, safety policy, and agent events into one clean runtime boundary.

## Proposed behavior

Implement the Hybrid Local AI Core:

```text
user message
    │
    ▼
┌────────────┐   ┌────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐
│ Intent     │ → │ Reasoning  │ → │ Agent Event  │
│ Gate       │   │ Core       │   │ Stream       │
└────────────┘   └─────┬──────┘   └──────┬───────┘
                       │                 │
      exact commands ◄─┼─► local model   ▼
      skill match      │          tool running / success /
      safety precheck  │          failure / approval / final
                       ▼
              deterministic fallback
```

Milestone 2 is complete when TouchPilot can route simple requests deterministically, use a local model path for ambiguous requests, and expose all work as local agent events.

## User workflow

A user sends a request such as:

- `Hello`
- `Help`
- `Open Settings`
- `Go back`
- `Open Wi-Fi settings`
- `What can you do on this screen?`

TouchPilot should:

1. Build local request context.
2. Run an intent gate.
3. Choose one of:
   - exact deterministic command
   - known skill match
   - local model reasoning path
   - clarification / blocked response
4. Emit structured agent events for the UI.
5. Route any tool request through validation, skill allowlists, and policy.
6. Return a final response or visible tool result.

## Required features and fixes

- Define a local reasoning core boundary separate from UI and Android tool execution.
- Add an intent gate for exact commands, known skills, unsafe requests, and model-needed requests.
- Remove cloud/provider fallback from the normal product path.
- Define local model input/output contracts for:
  - intent classification
  - tool selection
  - argument extraction
  - target ranking
  - final answer
- Build shared agent event types for:
  - user message
  - assistant message
  - tool requested
  - tool running
  - tool succeeded
  - tool failed
  - approval required
  - blocked by policy
  - final answer
- Keep deterministic router as fallback and test baseline.
- Ensure local model outputs are treated as untrusted and still pass through tool validation, skill allowlists, policy, and logging.
- Add offline-only validation for the main Milestone 2 flows.
- Add focused tests for routing decisions and event emission.

## Local-first impact

This milestone removes cloud/provider thinking from the core product path and makes TouchPilot's intelligence local by design:

```text
local deterministic router
  + local model reasoning
  + local skills
  + local policy
  + local logs
  = hybrid local Android AI core
``…

problems.title: feature: add local screen summary and suggested actions
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #38

## Problem

TouchPilot needs a visible local AI feature from screen context: it should be able to summarize the current screen and suggest useful actions without cloud inference.

This should start deterministic and context-driven. A local model hook can be added later, but Milestone 3 should not depend on a VLM or cloud model.

## Proposed behavior

Add local screen summary and suggested action generation from `ScreenContext`:

```text
ScreenContext
  -> screen summary
  -> suggested actions
  -> agent/UI event output
```

Example output:

```text
I see the Settings screen. Visible actions include Network & internet, Connected devices, Apps, Notifications, and Battery. I can open one of these sections, scroll, or go back.
```

Suggested actions should map to existing safe tool concepts where possible:

- tap visible item
- type into visible input
- scroll
- go back
- open app-specific visible section

## User workflow

User asks:

- `What screen am I on?`
- `What can you do here?`
- `What buttons are visible?`
- `Suggest actions for this screen`

TouchPilot replies locally using the current screen context.

## Local-first impact

This makes TouchPilot feel like a real local AI assistant while still using deterministic, inspectable local logic first.

## Safety and security considerations

- Summaries should avoid exposing sensitive details from password fields, private messages, tokens, or account screens.
- Suggested actions are not execution approval. Any selected action must still go through validation and policy.
- Suggestions should be conservative on sensitive screens.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add local screen summary generation from screen context.
- [ ] Add suggested action generation from visible controls.
- [ ] Suggested actions reference existing tool concepts without executing automatically.
- [ ] UI or agent event path can display the summary and suggestions.
- [ ] Sensitive text is redacted or avoided in summaries where practical.
- [ ] Tests cover Settings-like screen, launcher-like screen, input-field screen, and empty/weak context.
- [ ] No cloud model/backend is required.

## References

- Parent issue: #38
- Depends on: screen context model and builder issues
- `docs/ROADMAP.md` Milestone 3

problems.title: test: add offline screen understanding validation
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #38

## Problem

Milestone 3 should not be considered complete until screen understanding works offline on representative screens and produces expected summaries, suggestions, and redacted logs.

## Proposed behavior

Add Milestone 3 offline validation covering screen context and understanding:

- TouchPilot screen
- Android Settings screen
- Launcher screen
- input-field screen
- weak/limited Accessibility screen if available

Validation should confirm:

- screen context is built locally
- summary is generated locally
- suggested actions are generated locally
- no cloud/backend is required
- sensitive text remains redacted in logs/traces

## User workflow

A tester should be able to run TouchPilot on emulator or real device, open common screens, ask what TouchPilot sees, and verify that the app responds with local summaries and possible actions.

## Local-first impact

This proves Milestone 3's core claim: TouchPilot can understand Android screens locally without relying on cloud vision/chat models.

## Safety and security considerations

- Include at least one text-entry or sensitive-looking screen in validation to confirm redaction behavior.
- Suggested actions should not execute automatically during validation unless explicitly selected.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add a Milestone 3 offline validation checklist.
- [ ] Validate TouchPilot screen context.
- [ ] Validate Android Settings screen context.
- [ ] Validate launcher screen context.
- [ ] Validate an input-field screen.
- [ ] Validate weak/limited Accessibility behavior where practical.
- [ ] Confirm summaries and suggestions require no cloud/backend.
- [ ] Confirm logs/traces redact sensitive text where applicable.
- [ ] Add automated tests where practical and document manual live-test steps.

## References

- Parent issue: #38
- `docs/LIVE_TESTING.md`
- `docs/ROADMAP.md` Milestone 3

problems.title: Bug: Tools, MCP, and Logs tabs share a single `outputText` field, leaking tool results into unrelated section cards
problems.issue_body:
### What happened?

The MCP tab's "MCP result" card and the Logs tab's "Latest result" card display the output of the *most recent operation from any of the three tabs*, not the most recent operation from that tab.

The cause is a single `private var outputText: String` on `MainActivity` ([MainActivity.kt:733](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L733)):

```kotlin
private var outputText: String = "No result yet."
```

This same field is written from three places:

- `executeAndRender` on the **Tools** tab ([MainActivity.kt:737](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L737))
- Every **MCP** button click handler — List Tools, Call Tool, success and failure branches ([MainActivity.kt:640](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L640), [L664](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L664), [L677](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L677), [L688](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L688))
- The **Logs** tab's "Export Debug Trace" button ([MainActivity.kt:707](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L707))

…and read from three different cards:

- Tools — `timelineCard("Latest result", outputText)` ([MainActivity.kt:616](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L616))
- MCP — `timelineCard("MCP result", outputText)` ([MainActivity.kt:699](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L699))
- Logs — `timelineCard("Latest result", outputText)` ([MainActivity.kt:711](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L711))

Because every `showSection(...)` call wipes the view tree and re-runs the render function, the card always shows the **current global** `outputText`, regardless of which tab produced it.

Concrete leak directions observed on the running build:

1. **Tools → MCP / Logs**: Tapping `Observe Current Screen` on the Tools tab stores the snapshot result in `outputText`. Switching to the MCP tab shows `MCP result: observe_screen({}) -> false: TouchPilot Control is not enabled.`, and Logs shows the same string under `Latest result`. The user has no way to tell that the message originated in Tools and is unrelated to MCP.
2. **MCP → Tools / Logs**: Tapping `Call Tool` against an unreachable endpoint produces `MCP call failed: failed to connect to /10.0.2.2 (port 8000) ...`. Switching to Tools shows that same MCP-flavoured error under `Latest result`, suggesting the most recent tool execution failed when no tool was executed.
3. **Logs → Tools / MCP**: Tapping `Export Debug Trace` writes the export path into `outputText`. Switching to MCP shows `Debug trace exported: /storage/.../touchpilot-trace-...txt` under `MCP result`, which is nonsensical in MCP context.

The `ToolExecutionLog` shown lower on the Logs tab is correctly siloed — only the "Latest result" card leaks — which makes the bug particularly confusing: the user sees one card claiming `observe_screen(...)` was just run, while the audit-log card below correctly sho…

problems.title: Bug: `SensitiveTextRedactor` mangles `Authorization: Bearer` headers and its greedy token regex eats surrounding structu
problems.issue_body:
### What happened?

[`SensitiveTextRedactor.redact(text: String)`](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/security/SensitiveTextRedactor.kt#L15-L27) contains **two distinct bugs** that fire together whenever an `Authorization: Bearer <token>` substring is redacted as part of a larger log line — which is the exact case for tool-execution log strings written by `executeAndRender` and surfaced on the Tools tab and in `Export Debug Trace`.

### Bug A — `Authorization` header structure is destroyed

The second assignment pattern uses `(?i)(authorization:\s*bearer\s+)[^\s,;]+` and shares the same replacement lambda as the first pattern:

```kotlin
redacted = pattern.replace(redacted) { match ->
    val prefix = match.value.substringBeforeAny(listOf(":", "="))
    "$prefix=[REDACTED]"
}
```

For input `Authorization: Bearer abc123xyz`:

- `match.value` = `Authorization: Bearer abc123xyz`
- `substringBeforeAny(listOf(":", "="))` returns `Authorization` (split at the first `:`)
- replacement = `Authorization=[REDACTED]`

The output is `Authorization=[REDACTED]`. The `Bearer` keyword is dropped, and the HTTP-header `:` is rewritten to an assignment `=`. The first capture group (`(authorization:\s*bearer\s+)`) is captured but never used — the regex is written as if a `"$1[REDACTED]"`-style replacement were intended, and that is presumably the original intent.

### Bug B — the greedy `[^\s,;]+` token consumes trailing `}`, `)`, `]`, `>` etc.

`[^\s,;]+` is greedy and only excludes whitespace, comma, and semicolon. Closing brackets, parentheses, angle brackets, quotes, and any other structural character get eaten as part of the "token" and replaced along with the secret.

Concretely, the tool-execution log string produced by `MainActivity.executeAndRender` for a `type_text` call with `text=Authorization: Bearer abc123xyz` is:

```
type_text({text=Authorization: Bearer abc123xyz}) -> false: typeIntoFocusedField
```

After redaction, the actual output is:

```
type_text({text=Authorization=[REDACTED] -> false: typeIntoFocusedField
```

The `})` that closes the args dict and the tool-call parenthesis is **gone** — the regex matched `Authorization: Bearer abc123xyz})` (eating the closing `})`) and replaced the whole match with `Authorization=[REDACTED]`, throwing the brackets away.

Same class of structural damage happens with:

- `password=secret123}` inside an args dict — `password=[REDACTED]`, closing `}` deleted
- `api_key=sk-abc.def\nsomething` — the regex would also eat through anything not whitespace/comma/semicolon, although newlines do happen to count as whitespace so this particular case stops at end-of-line
- any JSON-ish args printout where the secret is the last value before `}` or `)`

The mangling is visible in the Tools tab's "Latest result" card after the [bug2 fix](touchpilot/bug2.md) (or in the unified card on `main`), in approval cards rendered by `buildApprovalMessage`, and in trace files written by `exportDebugTrace`.

The audit-log section on…

problems.title: Bug: `DefaultActionPolicy.blockedWorkflow` substring-matches against the active screen text, blocking unrelated benign a
problems.issue_body:
### What happened?

[`DefaultActionPolicy.evaluate`](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/security/ActionPolicy.kt#L54-L67) builds a `haystack` string that concatenates the tool name, the argument values, **and the entire `request.activeScreen` snapshot**, then runs [`blockedWorkflow`](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/security/ActionPolicy.kt#L109-L131) which checks whether any of these substrings appear anywhere in the haystack:

```
payment, "pay ", password, passcode, account recovery, recover account,
factory reset, erase all, delete account, purchase, buy now, bank,
wire transfer, transfer money
```

Because `activeScreen` is the raw accessibility-tree text of whatever app the user is currently looking at, the policy returns `PolicyDecision.Block` **for every non-LOW-risk tool** the moment the foreground screen contains any of those tokens — even when the tool, its arguments, and the user's actual intent have nothing to do with the supposedly-blocked workflow.

The check runs before the message-send detector, before the MCP approval branch, and before the regular HIGH/MEDIUM approval branch (lines 67-91), so once a keyword appears on screen the agent loses its ability to take **any** medium- or high-risk action: `tap`, `open_app`, `type_text`, `scroll`, `press_back`, `press_home`, and any other MEDIUM/HIGH tool added in the future.

### Concrete examples that fire today

Every one of the following is a benign action whose arguments and tool name contain none of the blocked keywords, but the policy blocks it because the foreground screen contains an unrelated keyword:

| Tool | Args | Foreground screen text contains | Returned decision |
|------|------|--------------------------------|-------------------|
| `tap` | `text=Settings` | `"Banking app home screen"` | `Block("banking or financial actions are blocked")` |
| `open_app` | `target=Calculator` | `"Passwords saved earlier in this session"` | `Block("password workflows are blocked")` |
| `scroll` | `direction=forward` | `"Order history including past purchase totals"` | `Block("purchases are blocked")` |
| `press_back` | (none) | `"Help article: how to delete account permanently"` | `Block("destructive account changes are blocked")` |

Real-world consequences in normal Android usage:

- Browsing a news article that mentions "factory reset" anywhere on screen — every tap is blocked.
- Sitting on the Android system Settings → Passwords & accounts screen — every action is blocked, even pressing Home to leave the screen.
- Notification shade pulls in any banking, payment, or shopping notification text — every action is blocked until the notification is dismissed.
- A skill explicitly designed to navigate a banking app's read-only screens — entirely unable to act.

Crucially, the check provides **no actual security**: an attacker (or buggy model output) can perform the exact action the keyword is meant to discourage as long as the *foreground app currently on screen* does not happen to dis…

problems.title: feature: add Android target selector model
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #75

## Problem

TouchPilot tools currently pass target data as loose arguments such as `text`, `node_id`, or `bounds`. That makes tool behavior harder to validate, rank, debug, and extend.

## Proposed behavior

Add a stable target selector model for Android UI targets.

The selector should support:

- `text`
- `contentDescription`
- `nodeId`
- `bounds`
- `viewIdResourceName`
- optional role/package/window metadata
- confidence/source metadata for resolver output

## User workflow

When the agent or local router chooses a UI target, TouchPilot should represent the target in one structured shape before execution.

## Local-first impact

A local agent needs deterministic, inspectable target data. This selector model becomes the contract between screen understanding, routing, and Android tool execution.

## Safety and security considerations

Selectors must be redacted in logs when they contain sensitive text. The model should make ambiguity explicit rather than encouraging blind taps.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add a structured selector model for Android targets.
- [ ] Support current `tap` selectors: text, node id, and bounds.
- [ ] Include view id/content description fields for future resolver work.
- [ ] Add unit tests for selector construction and redaction-sensitive fields where practical.
- [ ] Do not change runtime tool behavior in this PR unless necessary.

## Alternatives considered

Keep loose string maps. Rejected because Milestone 4 needs stronger validation, ranking, and verification contracts.

problems.title: feature: add focus_input tool
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #75

## Problem

`type_text` currently depends on whichever field is already focused. That is unreliable for forms, search boxes, and settings screens where the intended field is visible but not focused.

## Proposed behavior

Add a `focus_input` Android tool that focuses a resolved editable target without typing text.

Expected arguments should support the Milestone 4 selector model, such as:

- `text`
- `node_id`
- `bounds`
- `view_id`

The tool should use the target resolver and only focus editable/input nodes.

## User workflow

A user or local agent can first focus a named input field, then call `type_text` with confidence that text will go to the intended place.

## Local-first impact

This strengthens deterministic local form control without needing a cloud model.

## Safety and security considerations

Focusing is lower risk than typing, but it can still prepare a sensitive workflow. The action should remain logged and should not bypass policy for the later text entry.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add `focus_input` to the Android tool catalog.
- [ ] Resolve target through the Milestone 4 selector/resolver path.
- [ ] Reject non-editable targets with a clear error.
- [ ] Preserve policy, approval, and log behavior.
- [ ] Add unit tests for successful focus, missing target, non-input target, and ambiguous target.

## Alternatives considered

Only add target handling inside `type_text`. That is still useful, but a separate focus primitive gives the agent a safer, inspectable step for multi-action form workflows.

problems.title: fix: harden scroll with target-aware container resolution
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #75

## Problem

The current `scroll` tool scrolls forward or backward on the active screen, but it does not resolve a specific scrollable container.

This can fail or scroll the wrong area on screens with nested lists, sheets, or panes.

## Proposed behavior

Make scroll target-aware where possible.

The hardened scroll flow should:

- resolve scrollable containers from `ScreenContext`,
- support current direction-only behavior as a fallback,
- return a clear failure when no scrollable target exists,
- record the selected scroll target in redacted debug data,
- avoid blind repeated scrolling when the screen does not change.

## User workflow

When a user asks TouchPilot to scroll a page or list, the app should scroll the relevant container or explain that no scrollable area is available.

## Local-first impact

Reliable local control depends on scroll behaving consistently across Android screens.

## Safety and security considerations

Do not use unbounded scrolling. Retry and verification must remain bounded and observable.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Resolver can identify scrollable nodes.
- [ ] `scroll` can use a resolved scrollable target where possible.
- [ ] Direction-only scroll remains compatible.
- [ ] No-scrollable-target failure is explicit.
- [ ] Unit tests cover single container, multiple containers, no container, and unchanged-screen behavior where practical.

## Alternatives considered

Keep global scroll only. Rejected because nested scroll surfaces are common on Android.

problems.title: bug: Setting cookies with expiry in lambda are broken
problems.issue_body:
### Provide environment information

  System:
    OS: macOS 14.7.2
    CPU: (12) arm64 Apple M3 Pro
    Memory: 231.94 MB / 36.00 GB
    Shell: 5.9 - /bin/zsh
  Binaries:
    Node: 22.12.0 - ~/.proto/shims/node
    Yarn: 4.1.0 - ~/.proto/shims/yarn
    npm: 10.4.0 - ~/.proto/shims/npm
    pnpm: Checking for package overrides
9.15.0 - ~/.proto/shims/pnpm
    bun: 1.0.32 - ~/.bun/bin/bun
  Browsers:
    Chrome: 133.0.6943.53
    Safari: 18.2
  npmPackages:
    typescript: ^5.7.3 => 5.7.3 

### Describe the bug

When setting a cookie which has an expiry eg `Sun, 15 Jul 2012 00:00:01 GMT` the lambda adapter breaks the cookie into 2 resulting in the cookie not being set with any params after the expiry including the expiry 

### To reproduce

Apply a set cookie with an expiry date and deploy to lambda, the reproduction needs to be deployed to lambda. 

### Additional information

The issue was introduced here https://github.com/trpc/trpc/pull/5783/files#diff-a1587748fc98224d3b2ef8f04104b72b5b029f913587da98272dc53fa3df3d5bR48 and is still in next https://github.com/trpc/trpc/blob/caed91ee782f78ada6c8c0c82e2d9d2ff474f555/packages/server/src/adapters/aws-lambda/getPlanner.ts#L48

It's unsafe to just split the cookies on comma without considering the expiry field. The set cookie header should be parsed and then split into the individual cookies

### 👨‍👧‍👦 Contributing

- [ ] 🙋‍♂️ Yes, I'd be down to file a PR fixing this bug!

problems.issue_body:
### Provide environment information

```
System:
    OS: macOS 15.3.1
    CPU: (8) arm64 Apple M1
    Memory: 2.35 GB / 16.00 GB
    Shell: 5.9 - /bin/zsh
  Binaries:
    Node: 22.14.0 - ~/.nvm/versions/node/v22.14.0/bin/node
    npm: 10.9.2 - ~/.nvm/versions/node/v22.14.0/bin/npm
    pnpm: 10.4.1 - ~/.nvm/versions/node/v22.14.0/bin/pnpm
    Watchman: 2021.09.27.00 - /opt/homebrew/bin/watchman
  Browsers:
    Chrome: 133.0.6943.127
    Safari: 18.3
  npmPackages:
    @tanstack/react-query: 5.66.0 => 5.66.0 
    @trpc/client: 11.0.0-rc.804 => 11.0.0-rc.804+ab12d16b0 
    @trpc/next: 11.0.0-rc.804 => 11.0.0-rc.804+ab12d16b0 
    @trpc/react-query: 11.0.0-rc.804 => 11.0.0-rc.804+ab12d16b0 
    @trpc/server: 11.0.0-rc.804 => 11.0.0-rc.804+ab12d16b0 
    next: 15.1.7 => 15.1.7 
    react: 19.0.0 => 19.0.0 
    typescript: 5.7.3 => 5.7.3 
```

### Describe the bug

Doing a migration from v10 to v11 in T3 based app. **It worth to mention that the same code works with no issues on v10.**
Full description: https://discord.com/channels/867764511159091230/1336292204045668352
TL;DR
I faced a weird issue:"Error serializing `.trpcState.json.queries[26].promise` returned from `getStaticProps`" using fetch + prefetch in getStaticProps.
I noticed that that happens only in case of dependant queries. helper.dyhydrate() returns promise like data mixed with real responses. That data is not serialized so NextJS throw en error.
Wrapping **helper.dyhydrate()** into JSON.stringify make builds pass, but I guess that just a way to mute errors and hide underlying problems.
Another way - refactor code from dependant queries to just one but that's not seem to be right way to do things.
In general I have no urgent need for a temporary solution, and I can wait for a fix in version 11 using version 10 which doesn't have this problem.


### Link to reproduction

https://github.com/MadaShindeInai/trpcreactqueryissuerepro/tree/main

### To reproduce

`git clone git@github.com:MadaShindeInai/trpcreactqueryissuerepro.git`
`cd trpcreactqueryissuerepro`
`pnpm i`
`pnpm build`

### Additional information

Discord question: https://discord.com/channels/867764511159091230/1020132255567454310/threads/1336292204045668352
Twitter question:
https://x.com/MadaShindeInai/status/1893626390664114340

### 👨‍👧‍👦 Contributing

- [ ] 🙋‍♂️ Yes, I'd be down to file a PR fixing this bug!

problems.title: feat: Add support for aws-lambda on httpBatchStreamLink
problems.issue_body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

`httpBatchStreamLink` was implemented on this https://github.com/trpc/trpc/pull/4347

Currently, the built-in AWS Lambda Adapter for tRPC didn't provide support for AWS Lambda Response Streaming, so I'm opening this issue just to keep track of the future support of this functionality.

### Describe the solution you'd like to see

The ideal solution should be something like this:

```js
import { CreateAWSLambdaContextOptions, awsLambdaRequestHandler } from '@trpc/server/adapters/aws-lambda-stream';
const appRouter = /* ... */;
// created for each request
const createContext = ({
  event,
  context,
}: CreateAWSLambdaContextOptions<APIGatewayProxyEventV2>) => ({}) // no context
type Context = trpc.inferAsyncReturnType<typeof createContext>;
export const handler = awsLambdaRequestStreamHandler({
  router: appRouter,
  createContext,
})
```

### Describe alternate solutions

I'm the maintainer of [Serverless Adapter](https://github.com/H4ad/serverless-adapter), a library to connect any serverless environment with any NodeJS Framework. 

I already added support for AWS Lambda Response Stream ([docs](https://serverless-adapter.viniciusl.com.br/docs/main/handlers/aws#aws-lambda-response-streaming)), and I also [support tRPC](https://serverless-adapter.viniciusl.com.br/docs/main/frameworks/trpc) (I still need to update my library to add support to the latest version) but I think it will work without any issue this new feature using my library.

If you want to add support for response streaming on the internal built-in, I suggest for who are interested in implementing, read the following files:
- [AwsStreamHandler](https://github.com/H4ad/serverless-adapter/blob/main/src/handlers/aws/aws-stream.handler.ts#L254-L316)
- [ResponseStream](https://github.com/H4ad/serverless-adapter/blob/main/src/network/response-stream.ts)
- [TrpcFramework](https://github.com/H4ad/serverless-adapter/blob/main/src/frameworks/trpc/trpc.framework.ts#L170-L186)

Of course, the final implementation can be different but this is just my suggestion.



### Additional information

_No response_

### 👨‍👧‍👦 Contributing

- [ ] 🙋‍♂️ Yes, I'd be down to file a PR implementing this feature!

problems.title: feat: limit batch size on serverside
feat: add `localLink` / `ssrLink` that runs the router in the same process as the client
problems.issue_body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Hi there!

It would be great to have a `LocalLink` that allows me to do:
This would directly run the router in the same process as the client instead of transferring requests to a server.

### But why??

This is motivated mainly due to testing and fast prototyping:
1. This allows to test the client with the server code (with a small caveat, see below)
2. I can start writing a fully functional client that saves data in local storage instead of a database, and when I want to add a database, I don't need to rewrite the procedures, giving me significantly faster prototyping speed.

### Describe the solution you'd like to see

```ts
import { createRouter } from '../path/to/server/trpc';
const client = createTRPCProxyClient<ReturnType<typeof createRouter>>({
  links: [
    localLink({ router: createRouter({ database }) }),
  ],
});```

For the feature itself it doesn't matter that the router is created by a function call, but this solves a problem I mentioned above: What if part of the code only runs on the server, e.g. database access? Well, just inject that dependency, and, in case you are running on the client, use a browser-based database.

### Describe alternate solutions

Not sure, to be honest.

### Additional information

_No response_
