problems.title: bug: custom classes violated during `httpBatchStreamLink` encoding
problems.issue_body:
### Provide environment information

I don't feel comfortable oversharing my environment, but I'm using `trpc@11.1.2`

### Describe the bug

I've got a route returning a single instance of a custom class as the response, i.e. root value, not nested in a vanilla object.

This is most definitely related to #6464, but now for a custom class. The root of the problem is that the [fix here](https://github.com/trpc/trpc/blob/4ea1d25de8ba93f6c1108952adc13a7af4ccbbb4/packages/server/src/unstable-core-do-not-import/stream/jsonl.ts#L25-L27):
```typescript
function isPlainObject(value: unknown): value is Record<string, unknown> {
  return Object.prototype.toString.call(value) === '[object Object]';
}
```
now returns a _"false positive"_, unless one were to define the `Symbol.toStringTag` ofc.

Shouldn't `trpc` do something smart and spot that the payload should be `serialized` instead of `encoded`? Especially since this isn't a problem with the basic [`httpBatchLink`](https://trpc.io/docs/client/links/httpBatchLink)

I'd love to fix this myself, but what would be the route to take?

### Link to reproduction

https://stackblitz.com/edit/github-nljmfutn?file=src%2Fpages%2Findex.tsx

### To reproduce

..Open da blitz?

Otherwise, create a custom class and "hook it up" to the transformer, set up a `httpBatchStreamLink` with said transformer, try to return an instance of the custom class as the sole response to a query and check the responses `instanceof` or `x.constructor.name`.

### Additional information

_No response_

### 👨‍👧‍👦 Contributing

- [x] 🙋‍♂️ Yes, I'd be down to file a PR fixing this bug!

problems.title: feat: migrate to Zod v4 ways of retrieving input/output types
problems.issue_body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

The `_input` and `_output` attributes that are being used to retrieve the types of Zod schemas are [now deprecated](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/blob/v4.0.15/packages/zod/src/v4/classic/schemas.ts#L26). There is at least [one bug](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod/issues/5073) where `_output` is incorrect (while the non-deprecated replacement (`z.output<...>` or `...['_zod']['output']`) is correct), causing the input in the resolver to have an incorrect type.

As of v3.24.0, Zod supports Standard Schema, which could also be used to extract the types.

### Describe the solution you'd like to see

Type inference should use one of the newer and more correct ways of retrieving input/output types, where available.

### Describe alternate solutions

Just wait for the bug to be fixed. That will probably work well enough in practice, but involves depending on something deprecated and potentially incorrect in other ways.

### Additional information

I'm willing to look into implementing a fix, though I would need guidance on what the desired solution should be, especially regarding compatibility with different versions of Zod.

### 👨‍👧‍👦 Contributing

- [x] 🙋‍♂️ Yes, I'd be down to file a PR implementing this feature!

```

### Describe the bug

When testing subscriptions on Vercel, I was surprised to experience timeouts of my lambdas, even though I've set  `sse.maxDurationMs` to a duration shorter than my lambda's max duration.

After running more tests, I figured the subscription was correctly terminated but only when it yields a value:
https://stackblitz.com/edit/github-rmw4rvvn-lqfxkrsa?file=src%2Fpages%2Fapi%2Ftrpc%2F%5Btrpc%5D.ts

If I do not yield anything from the subscription handler, then it's never terminated and the signal is never aborted:
https://stackblitz.com/edit/github-rmw4rvvn-ertp6cer?file=src%2Fpages%2Fapi%2Ftrpc%2F%5Btrpc%5D.ts

Btw, I also noticed that when the subscription is terminated by `maxDurationMs`, it takes some time for the client to notice and reconnect. It would be great if there was a built-in way to proactively reconnect from the client in order to minimise the latency during reconnection. Maybe that could be a default behaviour when `maxDurationMs` is set? Please let me know if I'm missing something here.

### Link to reproduction

https://stackblitz.com/edit/github-rmw4rvvn-ertp6cer?file=src%2Fpages%2Fapi%2Ftrpc%2F%5Btrpc%5D.ts

### To reproduce

Just look at the console log. Notice that we never log "🚫 subscription ended" but continue loggign `🔄 loop ${count}` even though the 2s `maxDurationMs` is passed.

problems.issue_body:
### Provide environment information


  Binaries:
    Node: 24.13.0 - ~/fnm_multishells/98932_1770386641777/bin/node
    pnpm: 10.17.1 - ~/fnm_multishells/98932_1770386641777/bin/pnpm

    @trpc/client: catalog:trpc => 11.9.0
    @trpc/server: catalog:trpc => 11.9.0
    next: catalog:next => 16.1.5
    react: catalog:react => 19.2.3
    typescript: catalog: => 5.9.3

### Describe the bug

The (specific) bug reported in https://github.com/trpc/trpc/issues/5825 is still not fixed in TRPC v11.
Even though the batch size can be handled in context as recommended:

```
{
  createContext: (opts) => {
    if (opts.info.calls.length > 5) {
      throw new TRPCError({
        code: 'BAD_REQUEST',
        message: `Batch size limit exceeded. Max batch size is ${MAX_BATCH_SIZE}.`,
      })
    }
  }
}
```

invalid trpc procedure paths never reach the `createContext` handler.


Steps to reproduce:

1. `GET http://localhost:3000/api/trpc/,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,?batch=1`
2. Depending on the number of "procedures", wait 0-timeout for the request to resolve, whilst hogging the connection
3. Get a bunch of errors, (though attackers won't care)
```
   "error": {
      "json": {
        "message": "No procedure found on path \"\"",
        "code": -32004,
        "data": {
          "code": "NOT_FOUND",
          "httpStatus": 404,
```
Note that the error handling from createContext never triggers as the invalid procedure error triggers (as it should) before the context.


Suggest the batch handling function to also have a `maxItem` prop to limit the number to prevent denial of service in batch mode.

Example NextJS API route handler:

  return response
}

const MAX_BATCH_SIZE = 20

const handler = async (req: NextReq…

    npm: 10.8.2 - /usr/local/bin/npm
    pnpm: 8.15.6 - /usr/local/bin/pnpm
  npmPackages:
    @tanstack/react-query: ^5.90.16 => 5.90.16 
    @trpc/client: canary => 11.8.2-canary.3+f48ed5c18 
    @trpc/next: canary => 11.8.2-canary.3+f48ed5c18 
    @trpc/react-query: canary => 11.8.2-canary.3+f48ed5c18 
    @trpc/server: canary => 11.8.2-canary.3+f48ed5c18 
    next: ^15.5.9 => 15.5.9 
    react: ^19.2.3 => 19.2.3 
    typescript: ^5.9.3 => 5.9.3 
```

### Describe the bug

When I set `maxDurationMs` in `SSEStreamProducerOptions`, the subscription becomes idle after `maxDurationMs` is reached.

I was expecting `maxDurationMs` to define the maximum duration of a single HTTP request. But I would expect the tRPC client to send a new request to maintain the subscription active.

If stopping the subscription is actually the expected behavior, could you please let me know what is the correct way to configure tRPC subscriptions in a serverless environment (i.e., Vercel), where HTTP requests have a max duration?

My goals are:

1. Limited downtime on reconnect: With the default configuration, tRPC will take some time to "realize" that the HTTP request timed out and then reconnect. During that time, new events are not received. I know I can use tracked events so that I don't miss events. But I'd also like to keep the app responsive as much as possible. I think the solution would be to proactively reconnect before the serverless timeout is reached.

2. No Vercel timeout: I don't want every subscription request to end with a Vercel timeout. That does not sound clean and will get noisy in logs.

### Link to reproduction

https://stackblitz.com/edit/github-rmw4rvvn-3umdr23r?file=src%2Fpages%2Fapi%2Ftrpc%2F[trpc].ts

### To reproduce

Just open the reproduction link, let it start and wait to see the subscription become idle.

### Additional information

_No response_

problems.title: bug: httpBatchStreamLink loses buffered chunks on normal stream completion due to closeOrAbort using controller.error()
problems.issue_body:
### Provide environment information

```
System:
  OS: macOS 26.2
  CPU: (10) arm64 Apple M1 Max
  Memory: 78.77 MB / 32.00 GB
  Shell: 5.9 - /bin/zsh
Binaries:
  Node: 24.11.1
  pnpm: 10.9.0
npmPackages:
  @trpc/client: ^11.1.0 => 11.7.2
  @trpc/server: ^11.1.0 => 11.7.2
  @trpc/react-query: ^11.1.0 => 11.7.2
  @tanstack/react-query: ^5.74.4 => 5.90.12
  next: 15.2.8
  react: ^19.0.1 => 19.2.1
  typescript: ^5 => 5.9.3
```

### Describe the bug

When using `httpBatchStreamLink`, **normal stream completion causes all buffered (enqueued but unconsumed) chunks in the `ReadableStream` to be silently discarded**. This is because `closeOrAbort` calls `cancelAll()` → `controller.error(reason)` for **both** the `close` and `abort` handlers of the `WritableStream`.

Per the [WHATWG Streams specification](https://streams.spec.whatwg.org/#rs-error), `controller.error()` immediately transitions the stream to the `"errored"` state and **discards all enqueued chunks** in the internal queue. In contrast, `controller.close()` transitions to `"closed"` and allows the consumer to drain all remaining buffered data.

#### Root cause

In [`src/unstable-core-do-not-import/stream/jsonl.ts`](https://github.com/trpc/trpc/blob/main/packages/server/src/unstable-core-do-not-import/stream/jsonl.ts):

```typescript
// Line 588-591
const closeOrAbort = (reason?: unknown) => {
  headDeferred?.reject(reason);
  streamManager.cancelAll(reason);  // ← always calls controller.error()
};

// Line 615-616 — both handlers use the same function
close: closeOrAbort,
abort: closeOrAbort,
```

And in `cancelAll`:

```typescript
// Line 484-488
function cancelAll(reason: unknown) {
  for (const controller of controllerMap.values()) {
    controller.error(reason);  // ← discards buffered chunks per WHATWG spec
  }
}
```

On normal completion, the `WritableStream`'s `close` handler is called (not `abort`), meaning the server has successfully sent all data. However, `closeOrAbort` treats this identically to an abort — it calls `controller.error(undefined)`, which:

1. Discards all chunks that were `enqueue()`d but not yet `read()` by the consumer
2. Passes `undefined` as the error reason, which causes downstream issues (e.g., AI SDK's `makeRequest` catch block does `err.name` on `undefined`, throwing a `TypeError`)

#### Impact

This creates a **race condition** between the producer (HTTP response → JSONL parser → `controller.enqueue()`) and the consumer (`ReadableStream` → application code). For long streaming responses (e.g., 1500+ chunks from an LLM), the producer often finishes writing all chunks before the consumer has read them all. When `close` fires, the remaining buffered chunks are lost.

**Observed behavior:**
- Short responses: usually works (consumer keeps up with producer)
- Long responses: **truncated output** — the consumer receives only a portion of the data
- The stream ends with `error(undefined)` instead of `close()`, so consumers cannot distinguish between a normal compl…

problems.title: TLS lowerMaximumSecurityTo off-by-one error
problems.issue_body:
|[](https://github.com/Jon-Work)| @Jon-Work reported| |-|-| |Trac ID|trac#10232| |Type|defect| |Created|2021-07-16 15:56:18Z| I'm trying to configure the supported SSL and TLS versions for a server. I am using the twisted.internet.ssl.CertificateOptions constructor's lowerMaximumSecurityTo parameter. But the configuration I get is not what the documentation says. The documentation says lowerMaximumSecurityTo is "The maximum TLS version to use." Which seems reasonable. So specifying lowerMaximumSecurityTo=ssl.TLSVersion.TLSv1_2, I expect to disable TLS 1.3 and still support TLS 1.2, 1.1 and 1.0. However, that actually disables both TLS 1.3 and TLS 1.2, which is wrong. The problem is line 78 of src/twisted/internet/_sslverify.py: excludedVersions = [x for x in versions[: versions.index(oldest)]] Since Python's list-slicing excludes the index specified, there is an off-by-one error there. It just needs a +1 to the number returned by index().
Searchable metadata ``` trac-id__10232 10232 type__defect defect reporter__Jon_Work Jon-Work priority__normal normal milestone__None None branch__ branch_author__ status__new new resolution__None None component__core core keywords__ssl_tls ssl tls time__1626450978587660 1626450978587660 changetime__1626450978587660 1626450978587660 version__None None owner__None None ```

problems.title: regression from 25.5.0: optionsForClientTLS no longer negotiates ALPN
problems.issue_body:
A big disclaimer: I used AI to investigate this, and while it looks plausible I'm lost in the `_sslverify.py` internals, so the reason may be a different one.

It looks like after #12506 passing `"h2"` in `acceptableProtocols` to `optionsForClientTLS()` is not enough to get your client connection negotiate HTTP/2, which can be reproduced using docs/core/examples/tls_alpn_client.py and docs/core/examples/tls_alpn_server.py. In trunk `set_alpn_protos()` is called in `twisted.internet._sslverify.OpenSSLCertificateOptions._makeTLSConnection()` on the *connection* while in 25.5.0 it's called in `twisted.internet._sslverify._setAcceptableProtocols()` on the *context* and `twisted.internet._sslverify.ClientTLSOptions.clientConnectionForTLS()` (in both versions) creates a connection directly, not via `_makeTLSConnection()`.

This is more of a task/chore than a feature request. Since Twisted now support Python 3.9 and newer only. The project can stop importing the following types from the `typing` module: `List`, `Dict`, `Set` and `Tuple` 

Instead the following types can be used without any imports in Python 3.9+: `list`, `dict`, `set` and `tuple` 

The goal is to clean-up the project a little bit and modernize the code. These typing module types may be removed in the future.

problems.title: Update typing annotations project wide using `pyupgrade --py39-plus`
problems.issue_body:
This issue tracks the work of applying `pyupgrade --py39-plus` across the Twisted codebase.

`pyupgrade --py39-plus` enables (among other things) the [PEP 585](https://peps.python.org/pep-0585/) typing rewrites (e.g. `List[str]` -> `list[str]`) in an automatic way such that human error by doing this work manually is eliminated.

The goal is to apply this conversion project wide mainly for consistency.

To limit the scope of changes, only `pyupgrade --py39-plus` will be run at first. Later we can also run `pyupgrade --py310-plus` when Python 3.9 support is dropped. But with the Python 3.9+ changes out of the way, then the Python 3.10+ changes will be much smaller and easier to review.

My plan is to focus on one module at a time unless the Git diff is small (less than ~100 lines changed).

Adding `from __future__ import annotations` also allows us to drop `Union` and `Optional`.

====

problems.title: ReferenceError / crash when a class property inside a mocked module matches the name of an import (TypeScript + useDefin
Coverage chunk writes should ensure `reportsDirectory/.tmp` exists before writing `coverage-*.json`
problems.issue_body:
### Describe the bug

When coverage is enabled, Vitest writes per-suite coverage JSON files into a temp directory under `coverage.reportsDirectory`.

In the unpatched `vitest@4.1.3` coverage code path, `onAfterSuiteRun()` writes a coverage chunk without first ensuring that `reportsDirectory/.tmp` still exists:

```js
const filename = resolve(this.coverageFilesDirectory, `coverage-${uniqueId++}.json`);
const promise = promises.writeFile(filename, JSON.stringify(coverage), 'utf-8');
```

We have previously observed this class of failure in coverage-enabled workspace runs, where the temp directory was missing at the point a coverage file was opened or stat'ed, with errors like:

```text
Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, open '/.../coverage/<project>/.tmp/coverage-0.json'
```

or:

```text
Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, lstat '/.../coverage/<project>/.tmp'
```

Because `vitest@4.1.3` still has the same write-without-`mkdir` assumption in this code path, we are currently carrying a local defensive patch that recreates the temp directory immediately before each coverage chunk write:

```js
const promise = promises
  .mkdir(this.coverageFilesDirectory, { recursive: true })
  .then(() => promises.writeFile(filename, JSON.stringify(coverage), 'utf-8'));
```

This is a no-op when the directory already exists, but it makes the write robust if the temp directory is missing at write time.

The practical impact is that a test run that otherwise completed successfully can still fail at the coverage persistence step. From a user perspective, that is a high-friction failure mode because:

- tests may already be green
- the failure happens late, during coverage result flushing
- the error points at Vitest's internal `.tmp/coverage-*.json` files rather than at user test code
- it can make CI or task-runner integrations look flaky even when the tests themselves are stable


### Reproduction

I do not have a public minimal reproduction repo yet because the original workspace where this was first observed is private.

What I can state directly:

1. We observed coverage failures on the `.tmp/coverage-*.json` path family and on missing `.tmp` directories in real workspace runs.
2. The unpatched `vitest@4.1.3` code path still performs a bare `writeFile()` into that temp directory.
3. We added a local defensive patch to that `writeFile()` path.

The scenario where that patch is necessary is:

1. Run Vitest with coverage enabled and a concrete `coverage.reportsDirectory`.
2. Let Vitest create its temp coverage directory during `clean()`.
3. Later, when `onAfterSuiteRun()` tries to write `coverage-*.json`, the temp directory is no longer present.
4. The run fails with `ENOENT` on `reportsDirectory/.tmp/coverage-*.json` or on the missing `.tmp` directory itself.

From Vitest's perspective, the key condition is simply this: `onAfterSuiteRun()` currently assumes the temp directory created earlier still exists at write time.

I suspect this can show up more e…

problems.title: DOMException [DataCloneError]: Object that needs transfer was found in message but not listed in transferList
problems.issue_body:
### Describe the bug

The code in package `web-worker` breaks the MessagePort transfer into the worker, so any packages that relies on that behaviour like a [comlink](https://www.npmjs.com/package/comlink) - does not work.

The code

https://github.com/vitest-dev/vitest/blob/0685b6f027576589464fc6109ddc071ef0079f16/packages/web-worker/src/utils.ts#L34-L36

The code were added in https://github.com/vitest-dev/vitest/pull/9118

User do not see any warning or error. I have spend a few hours to debug my code before I've go to the `web-worker` package code.

When i enable the debug logs i see the error message

```
create message event, using native structured clone
failed to clone message, dispatch "messageerror" event: DOMException [DataCloneError]: Object that needs transfer was found in message but not listed in transferList
```

### Reproduction

The worker code
```ts
self.addEventListener('message', (evt) => {
	self.postMessage("received");
});
```

The test code
```ts
import MyWorker from './MyWorker?worker';

test('Worker can receive a MessagePort', async () => {
	const worker: Worker = new MyWorker();

	const { port1 } = new MessageChannel();

	const onMessage = vi.fn();
	worker.addEventListener('message', onMessage);

	worker.postMessage({ data: port1 }, [port1]);

	await vi.waitUntil(() => onMessage.mock.calls.length);
	expect(onMessage).toHaveBeenCalled();
}, 500);
```

problems.title: --project='!name' doesn't exclude browser project instances
problems.issue_body:
## Describe the bug

`--project='!name'` negation patterns do not exclude browser-based projects because browser instances get a derived name with a suffix (e.g. `project-b (chromium)`), which doesn't match the negation regex.

Positive selection works correctly. Negation works for non-browser projects.

## Reproduction

https://github.com/felamaslen/vitest-project-exclude-repro

```bash
git clone https://github.com/felamaslen/vitest-project-exclude-repro.git
cd vitest-project-exclude-repro
npm install
npx playwright install chromium
```

Positive selection works:

```bash
npx vitest run --project=project-a
# Test Files  1 passed (1)
# Tests       1 passed (1)
```

Negation does not exclude the browser project:

```bash
npx vitest run '--project=!project-b'
# ✓ |project-a| a.test.ts > project-a test
# ✓ |project-b (chromium)| b.test.ts > project-b test
# Test Files  2 passed (2)
# Tests       2 passed (2)
```

**Expected:** 1 test file (only `project-a`).
**Actual:** 2 test files (both run).

## Cause

Browser projects create instances with derived names like `project-b (chromium)`. The negation regex `^(?!project\-b$)` anchors to end-of-string, so it doesn't match `project-b (chromium)`, and the instance passes through the filter.

## Workaround

Adding a trailing wildcard works:

```bash
npx vitest run '--project=!project-b*'
# Test Files  1 passed (1)
# Tests       1 passed (1)
```

## System Info

- vitest: 4.1.4
- @vitest/browser-playwright: 4.1.4
- OS: macOS (arm64)
- Node: v22

problems.title: `describe.concurrent.for` doesn't enable `concurrent`
Add jest-junit-compatible naming options to junit-reporter
problems.issue_body:
### Clear and concise description of the problem

As a developer using Vitest I want configure the JUnit reporter the same way I can configure jest-junit so that the generated JUnit XML file still looks the same after a migration from Jest to Vitest.

Vitest's built-in JUnit reporter names `<testsuite>` elements with the relative file path and names `<testcase>` elements with the full test hierarchy joined by `>`. This differs substantially from jest-junit, which offers rich template-based naming through `suiteNameTemplate`, `classNameTemplate`, `titleTemplate`, and `ancestorSeparator`. Migrating from jest to Vitest therefore produces noticeably different CI XML output.

### Suggested solution

Four new **opt-in** options are added to the `JUnitOptions` interface. Defaults are unchanged so existing reports are not affected.

### New options

| Option | Type | Default | Description |
|--------|------|---------|-------------|
| `suiteNameTemplate` | `string \| function` | – | Template for `<testsuite name>`. Placeholders: `{title}`, `{filename}`, `{filepath}`, `{basename}`, `{displayName}` |
| `titleTemplate` | `string \| function` | – | Template for `<testcase name>`. Same placeholders as `classnameTemplate`. |
| `ancestorSeparator` | `string` | `" > "` | Separator for ancestor describe names when building `{classname}` and the default test title. |
| `noStackTrace` | `boolean` | `false` | Omit stack trace text from `<failure>` bodies (message attribute is still written). |


### Alternative

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

### Validations

- [x] Follow our [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/vitest-dev/vitest/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md)
- [x] Read the [Contributing Guidelines](https://github.com/vitest-dev/vitest/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md).
- [x] Read the [docs](https://vitest.dev/guide/).
- [x] Check that there isn't already an issue that requests the same feature to avoid creating a duplicate.

Related PR: #10189

problems.title: `spyOn` is not possible on private or protected method
problems.issue_body:
### Describe the bug

TypeScript [allows accessing private and protected methods using bracket notation](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/classes.html#caveats). This is useful for unit testing, if you want to check a private/protected method works as expected.

Vitest does not allow using `spyOn` on a private/protected method (e.g. you want to test a public method that uses a private method to load data) unless you cast to any. So `vi.spyOn(myObject, 'privateMethod')` does not work while  `vi.spyOn(myObject as any, 'privateMethod')` works as expected. But this comes with the caveat that Vitest does not know about the types this function returns.

See the linked StackBlitz for an example.

### Reproduction

https://stackblitz.com/edit/vitest-dev-vitest-ucls7umq

problems.title: Unhandled errors caught during a run are missing from the JUnit XML report
problems.issue_body:
### Describe the bug

When Vitest catches an unhandled rejection or uncaught exception during a test run (via its worker process-level handler), the CLI prints an `⎯ Unhandled Errors ⎯` section, the run summary shows `Errors 1 error`, and the process exits with code `1`. None of this is reflected in the JUnit XML report — `<testsuites>` and `<testsuite>` both still report `failures="0" errors="0"` and contain only the passing testcases.

CI systems that gate on the JUnit artifact (Jenkins JUnit plugin, GitLab MR test widget, Azure Pipelines test tab, CircleCI test insights, etc.) therefore report the build as green even though the run actually failed.

A real-world example where this bites: a worker process exiting unexpectedly surfaces as:

```
[vitest-pool]: Worker forks emitted error
Caused by: Error: Worker exited unexpectedly
```

…which lands in the same `Unhandled Errors` channel and is similarly absent from the JUnit XML.

### Expected

The JUnit report should reflect the unhandled error that Vitest already counted in its `Errors` summary line — for example via a non-zero `errors=` count on the suite, an `<error>` element, or a synthetic `<testcase>` carrying the error.

### Actual

`vitest run` stdout (truncated):

```
 ✓ src/repro.test.ts (1 test) 2ms
⎯⎯⎯⎯⎯⎯ Unhandled Errors ⎯⎯⎯⎯⎯⎯
Vitest caught 1 unhandled error during the test run.
…
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  1 passed (1)
     Errors  1 error
```

Exit code: `1`. `junit-report.xml`:

```xml
<testsuites name="vitest tests" tests="1" failures="0" errors="0" …>
    <testsuite name="src/repro.test.ts" tests="1" failures="0" errors="0" skipped="0" …>
        <testcase classname="src/repro.test.ts" name="passes …" time="…"/>
    </testsuite>
</testsuites>
```

### Related history

- #4799 / #4819 (closing #4516) covered **synchronous** throws inside `beforeAll` / `afterAll`. They do not cover errors that go through Vitest's process-level unhandled-error handler.
- #3423 reported a similar symptom but was closed without a reproduction.

### Reproduction

Repo: https://github.com/gbleu/vitest-junit-unhandled-repro

```sh
git clone https://github.com/gbleu/vitest-junit-unhandled-repro
cd vitest-junit-unhandled-repro
npm install
npm test; echo "exit=$?"
cat junit-report.xml
```

Observe `Errors 1 error` and `exit=1` in stdout, while `junit-report.xml` reports `failures="0" errors="0"`.

### System Info

```shell
System:
  OS: macOS 26.4.1
  CPU: (10) arm64 Apple M4
  Memory: 1.30 GB / 32.00 GB
  Shell: 5.9 - /bin/zsh

Browsers:

npmPackages:

```

problems.title: Browser Mode leaks a manual mock across spec files when the same module is mocked via two ids
problems.issue_body:
### Describe the bug

This repro is pure TypeScript. Vue components are not required.

`src/probe.spec.ts` registers two manual mocks for the same logical module using two different raw ids:

- `vi.mock('~/modal', ...)`
- `vi.mock('./modal', ...)`

`src/target.spec.ts` does not register any mock. It only imports another module that touches `~/modal` again.

Both spec files pass when run individually, but when they run in the same Browser Mode process, the second file fails after the first file passes:

```text
[vitest] There was an error when mocking a module
[birpc] rpc is closed, cannot call "resolveManualMock"
```

This looks like a Browser Mode mock lifecycle bug. During the second file import, Vitest appears to still try resolving the manual mock registered in the previous file, even though the second file did not register that mock.

### Reproduction

Repository repro:

[A-kirami/vitest-browser-manual-mock](https://github.com/A-kirami/vitest-browser-manual-mock)

Commands:

```bash
bun vitest run --config vite.config.ts --no-cache src/probe.spec.ts
bun vitest run --config vite.config.ts --no-cache src/target.spec.ts
bun vitest run --config vite.config.ts --no-cache --reporter verbose src/probe.spec.ts src/target.spec.ts
```

Notes:

- `--no-cache` is important here because Vitest's sequencer uses cached file stats and durations to sort files.
- With cache enabled, the non-mocking file can run first and hide the problem.
- With `--no-cache`, the repro behaves like a fresh run and the failing order stays stable.

Expected behavior:

- `src/probe.spec.ts` passes alone
- `src/target.spec.ts` passes alone
- both specs also pass when run together in the same Browser Mode process

Actual behavior:

- the single-file runs pass
- the combined run fails after `src/probe.spec.ts` passes

Additional observation:

On a fresh run, `vitest list` also fails:

```bash
bun vitest list --config vite.config.ts --no-cache src/probe.spec.ts src/target.spec.ts
```

with:

```text
Mock /src/modal.ts wasn't registered. This is probably a Vitest error. Please, open a new issue with reproduction.
```

### System Info

```shell
System:

  CPU: (20) x64 12th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-12700H

problems.title: Code coverage file overmatching when one package path is substring of another
problems.issue_body:
### Describe the bug

This is a request to please reopen https://github.com/vitest-dev/vitest/issues/9275 but I've now provided an MWE

### Reproduction

MWE: https://stackblitz.com/edit/vitest-dev-vitest-ca8yqwjf?file=packages%2Ftest%2Findex.ts

This MWE has 3 workspaces:
- `test`: a workspace with full test coverage, but a dependency on its sibling workspaces
- `test-a`: a workspace with partial test coverage, but a name that matches the prefix `test` (our first workspace)
- `no-clash`: a workspace that also has partial test coverage, but a name that doesn't share a prefix with the other two workspaces

Repro steps:
1. `npm i`
2. `npm test -w packages/test-a`: as expected, a coverage failure
3. `npm test -w packages/no-clash`: as expected, a coverage failure
4. `npm test -w packages/test`: **unexpected coverage failure** since it's including `test-a` in its coverage (the `no-clash` workspace is notably not reported)

```
 % Coverage report from istanbul
-----------|---------|----------|---------|---------|-------------------
File       | % Stmts | % Branch | % Funcs | % Lines | Uncovered Line #s 
-----------|---------|----------|---------|---------|-------------------
All files  |      80 |      100 |   66.66 |      80 |                   
 test      |     100 |      100 |     100 |     100 |                   
  index.ts |     100 |      100 |     100 |     100 |                   
 test-a    |      50 |      100 |      50 |      50 |                   
  index.ts |      50 |      100 |      50 |      50 | 2                 
-----------|---------|----------|---------|---------|-------------------
ERROR: Coverage for functions (66.66%) does not meet global threshold (100%)
```

problems.issue_body:
### Describe the bug

Running browser tests with playwright and `viewport: { width: 1280, height: 800 }`

await page.screenshot()

problems.title: [Regression] Browser mode: Failure screenshot not created when unstable error occurs in v4.1.0-beta.4
problems.issue_body:
### Describe the bug

Regression: In v4.1.0-beta.4, the changes from https://github.com/vitest-dev/vitest/pull/9552 (fix: don't take failure screenshot if tests have artifacts created by `toMatchScreenshot`) unintentionally prevent a failure screenshot from being captured and saved in unstable test failures.

**Problem:**
When a test fails with an unstable screenshot error (e.g., `Could not capture a stable screenshot within...`), Vitest now only provides a reference screenshot artifact. This makes it impossible to see what was actually rendered at the point of instability, hampering debugging ability. Previously, an 'internal:failureScreenshot' artifact was attached, but after this change, it is missing for these failures if any `internal:toMatchScreenshot` artifacts exist.

**Expected:**
When a test fails due to instability or any other reason that doesn't generate regular diff/actual artifacts, the failure screenshot should still be saved and attached, probably in `.vitest-attachments` folder though and not next to referenced screenshot. 


### Reproduction

Attaching minimal reproduction case

[unstable-reproduction-test.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25393333/unstable-reproduction-test.zip)

problems.title: v4: vi.mocked() regression for static class methods types
Improve error message when fixture doesn't call `use` before timeout
Support monorepos with project references in types testing
problems.issue_body:
### Clear and concise description of the problem

As a developer using Vitest in a monorepo with TS project references, I want Vitest to support build mode so that it can run types tests.

### Suggested solution

https://github.com/flint-fyi/flint/blob/223a99c80be4e0871154306654fb5ede37989c45/patches/vitest.patch but behind a flag, à la [rolldown-plugin-dts](https://github.com/sxzz/rolldown-plugin-dts#build).

I'm happy to send in a PR but there's obviously a reason we're passing all of these flags in the first place, which I don't know.

### Alternative

Just build with TS, use TSTyche, use eslint-plugin-expect-type, etc; none of them are integrated into Vitest though.

### Additional context

https://github.com/vitest-dev/vitest/issues/3752 is locked.

### Validations

- [x] Follow our [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/vitest-dev/vitest/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md)
- [x] Read the [Contributing Guidelines](https://github.com/vitest-dev/vitest/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md).
- [x] Read the [docs](https://vitest.dev/guide/).
- [x] Check that there isn't already an issue that requests the same feature to avoid creating a duplicate.
